topics.title: Concept Behind Food Product Design and Development Process: Concept of Product Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept Behind Food Product Design and Development Process: Concept of Product Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Food Product Development (FPD)** is defined as the **systematic, multidisciplinary, and iterative process** of conceiving, formulating, prototyping, testing, and commercializing new or improved food products that satisfy identified consumer needs, comply with regulatory standards, deliver economic value to the firm, and are technically feasible to manufacture at scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Food Product Design and Development is the disciplined sequence of activities — from idea generation to market launch — that transforms a *concept* into a *commercially viable, safe, legal, and consumer-acceptable* food product.

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic maps directly to:
- **CO1:** *Recall the fundamental principles, definitions, and sequential stages of the food product development process.*
- **Bloom's Level:** Remember (L1) → Understand (L2).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant vs. The Factory Kitchen"**
> 
> Imagine a home chef who invents a fantastic mango-flavour yogurt in her kitchen. She is excited — but to take it to a supermarket shelf, she must:
> 1. **Finalize the recipe** (formulation standardization),
> 2. **Ensure it tastes the same every time** (quality consistency),
> 3. **Prove it is safe** (shelf-life, microbial testing),
> 4. **Print a legal label** (FSSAI compliance, nutrition panel),
> 5. **Produce 10,000 cups a day** (scale-up engineering), and
> 6. **Convince consumers to buy it** (marketing).
> 
> That journey — *idea → standardized, safe, legal, profitable product on a retail shelf* — is the **essence of food product development**.

### 1.3 Key Industry Metrics (Standard Benchmark Values)

- **Failure rate of new food products:** approximately **80% fail within the first 2 years** of launch.
- **Average time from idea to launch:** **12 to 36 months** depending on product complexity.
- **Average cost of launching a new food product:** ranges between **USD 1 million – 5 million** for a major FMCG firm.
- **Global new product launches (annually):** approximately **20,000 – 30,000 SKUs** are introduced worldwide.
- **Return on Innovation Investment (ROI):** typically **5–10%** of total company revenue for R&D-driven firms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FPD Matters to an Engineer:** Unlike a mechanical or software product, a *food* product is a **living, biochemical, perishable matrix**. The engineer must simultaneously control **microbiology, chemistry, nutrition, sensory perception, packaging, cost, regulation, and consumer psychology** — making FPD one of the most *integrative* engineering disciplines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophy Behind the Concept

The **concept of product development** is rooted in three foundational pillars:

1. **Consumer Pull** — *latent or expressed demand* from the market.
2. **Technology Push** — *new ingredients, processes, or packaging* enabling novel solutions.
3. **Strategic Fit** — alignment with the *company's vision, capabilities, and profitability goals*.

A successful FPD programme is the **intersection of all three**. A brilliant technology without consumer demand fails; a strong consumer demand without technical feasibility fails; either without strategic fit is rejected by the firm.

### 2.2 Why Do Firms Develop New Food Products?

| **Driver Category** | **Specific Reason** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| Consumer-driven | Changing tastes, health consciousness, convenience | Low-sugar, high-protein ready-to-drink beverages |
| Competitive-driven | Match or beat competitor launches | Private-label dairy desserts |
| Regulatory-driven | Compliance with new food laws | Trans-fat-free bakery shortenings |
| Technology-driven | New processing techniques (HPP, ohmic heating, 3D food printing) | HPP-pressed fruit juices |
| Resource-driven | Utilisation of surplus or waste streams | Upcycled brewers' spent grain crackers |
| Strategic-driven | Market expansion, brand extension | Spice brand launching ready-to-cook meal kits |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Stage-Gate Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although FPD is qualitative in nature, the following **process-engineering table** is treated as the "formula sheet" for this topic. Memorize it thoroughly — it is the most frequently asked structure in KTU exams.

| **Stage No.** | **Stage Name** | **Core Activity** | **Key Deliverable (Gate Output)** | **Avg. Duration** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Stage 0** | **Strategy & Charter** | Define innovation strategy, portfolio gaps, project approval | Approved Project Charter | 1–2 months |
| **Stage 1** | **Discovery / Idea Generation** | Brainstorming, trend-scoping, consumer insights, idea sourcing | 50–100 raw ideas | 2–4 months |
| **Gate 1** | **Idea Screening** | Score ideas against feasibility, market size, strategic fit | Top 5–10 qualified ideas | 1–2 weeks |
| **Stage 2** | **Concept Development** | Translate ideas into product concepts with target consumer, benefit, positioning | Concept statement, draft label, mock-up | 2–3 months |
| **Gate 2** | **Concept Testing** | Quantitative consumer test (n $\geq$ 150); purchase-intent scoring | Validated concept with $\geq$ 60% top-2-box purchase intent | 1–2 months |
| **Stage 3** | **Business Case Building** | Formulation lock-in, cost engineering, regulatory review, financial model | Approved Business Case (NPV, IRR, payback) | 2–4 months |
| **Gate 3** | **Business Case Review** | Senior management go/no-go decision | Approved budget for development | 2–4 weeks |
| **Stage 4** | **Product & Process Development** | Pilot-plant trials, shelf-life studies, packaging validation, scale-up | Lab-to-pilot prototype with specifications | 4–9 months |
| **Stage 5** | **In-Plant & Commercial Trials** | Test-market, sensory panels, consumer home-use tests, line trials | Market-validated, line-ready product | 3–6 months |
| **Gate 5** | **Launch Authorization** | Final regulatory, legal, and operational sign-off | Launch Plan | 1 month |
| **Stage 6** | **Launch & Commercialization** | Production start, distribution, trade marketing, PR, advertising | Product on shelf | Ongoing |
| **Stage 7** | **Post-Launch Review** | Sales tracking, consumer complaint analysis, reformulation if needed | Lessons-learned report, v2 roadmap | 6–18 months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Stage-Gate model (Cooper, 1980s) is the **default expected answer** for any "explain the product development process" question. Mention the word **"Gates"** explicitly — they are decision checkpoints, *not* activities.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time-to-Market Reduction:** Stage-gate discipline can cut development time by **30–50%** by killing weak projects early.
- **Risk Management:** Approximately **70% of project cost overruns** originate from inadequate Gate 2 (Concept) and Gate 3 (Business Case) reviews.
- **Cross-Functional Integration:** FPD unites **R&D, Marketing, Regulatory, Production, Procurement, and Finance** into a single value stream — analogous to a *production line for innovation*.
- **Compliance Engineering:** Each Gate enforces FSSAI, Codex, ISO 22000, and HACCP checkpoints, reducing product recall probability by up to **60%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Process Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **process-oriented and conceptual** (not numerical), the "derivation" below is the **exhaustive logical deduction of the FPD process**, broken into 10 rigorous analytical steps. Each step is supported by its *why*, *how*, and *engineering output*.

### 3.1 The Ten Logical Steps of the Concept of Product Development

**Step 1 — Strategic Alignment (Why start?)**
$$\text{FPD Initiative} = f(\text{Consumer Insight},\ \text{Technology Capacity},\ \text{Corporate Strategy})$$

A new product idea is never an accident. The firm first audits its **innovation portfolio** and identifies whitespace opportunities. The output is a **Project Charter** that defines scope, target consumer, success metrics (KPIs), and budget ceiling.

> *Engineering logic:* Without a charter, the project lacks a *fitness function* — there is no way to evaluate later whether the product "succeeded."

**Step 2 — Idea Generation (Where do ideas come from?)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Idea Pool} &= \text{Consumer Ethnography} \\
&\cup \text{Trade Show Scanning} \\
&\cup \text{Competitor Teardowns} \\
&\cup \text{Scientist / Supplier Leads} \\
&\cup \text{Open Innovation Platforms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*How:* Techniques such as **SCAMPER**, **TRIZ**, **Design Thinking empathise-define-ideate** loops, and **Voice of Customer (VOC)** studies are deployed. Typical output: **50–200 raw ideas** per project sprint.

**Step 3 — Idea Screening (Which idea survives?)**

Each idea is rated on three weighted dimensions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{idea} &= w_1 \cdot M_{market} + w_2 \cdot F_{feasibility} + w_3 \cdot A_{strategic\ fit} \\
\text{where}\ w_1 + w_2 + w_3 &= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Only ideas with $S_{idea} \geq 0.65$ advance. This is **Gate 1** — the *funnel* that prevents weak projects from consuming engineering resources.

**Step 4 — Concept Development (What is the product?)**

The surviving idea is converted into a **product concept** — a written statement covering:

| **Element** | **Description** |
|:---|:---|
| Target Consumer | A specific demographic / psychographic segment |
| Core Benefit | The single most important reason to buy |
| Occasion | When, where, and how the product is consumed |
| Positioning | How it differs from competitive benchmarks |
| Price Tier | Premium / Mid / Value category |

> *Engineering logic:* A concept is a *functional specification* for the eventual food product. It is the "design brief" handed to the formulator.

**Step 5 — Concept Testing (Will anyone buy it?)**

Quantitative research with $\geq 150$ representative consumers. Key metrics:
- **Top-2-Box Purchase Intent** (must be $\geq 60\%$ for a pass)
- **Uniqueness score** (must be $\geq 60\%$ above category norm)
- **Believability score** (must be $\geq 50\%$)
- **Price elasticity** (Van Westendorp PSM analysis)

> *Engineering logic:* This is the **fail-fast gate** — cheaper to kill a concept now than to kill a product after plant trials.

**Step 6 — Business Case Development (Is it worth doing?)**

Three sub-modules are executed in parallel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Business Case} &= \text{Technical Feasibility} \\
&\cap \text{Financial Viability} \\
&\cap \text{Regulatory Compliance}
\end{aligned}
$$

Financial model includes:
- **NPV** (Net Present Value) of project over 5 years
- **IRR** (Internal Rate of Return), must exceed **WACC + 5%**
- **Payback period**, ideally $\leq 36$ months
- **Gross Margin**, must clear category-specific threshold (typically $\geq 35\%$ for FMCG)

**Step 7 — Product & Process Development (How do we make it?)**

- **Formulation R&D** in lab (1–10 kg batches)
- **Pilot-plant trials** (50–500 kg batches) on production-relevant equipment
- **Shelf-life acceleration studies** (ASLT at elevated temperature/humidity, then Arrhenius extrapolation to ambient)
- **Packaging validation** (OTR, WVTR, seal integrity, migration testing)
- **Sensory profiling** with trained panel (n $\geq 10$, $\geq 3$ replicates)
- **Nutritional analysis** (per FSSAI / Codex methods)
- **Microbiological challenge studies**

Output: **Master Formula + Process Specification + Packaging Spec + Quality Standards**.

**Step 8 — In-Plant & Market Testing (Does it work in the real world?)**

- **Production line trials** at commercial speeds
- **Test-marketing** in a limited geography (e.g., 1–2 cities)
- **Consumer home-use tests (HUT)** with $\geq 100$ households
- **Eye-tracking & shelf-set analysis** in simulated or real stores

> *Engineering logic:* This step converts a *lab-validated prototype* into a *manufacturable, consumer-validated product*.

**Step 9 — Launch Authorization (Are we ready?)**

Senior management reviews:
- Regulatory clearances (FSSAI license, FSSAI product approval, label compliance)
- Production capacity confirmed
- Distribution and trade marketing plans signed off
- Risk register with mitigation owners

If approved, **Gate 5** is passed and full launch commences.

**Step 10 — Launch, Commercialization & Post-Launch Review (Did it succeed?)**

- Production ramp-up, distribution fill, advertising rollout
- Sales tracking (Weeks 4, 12, 26, 52)
- Consumer complaint monitoring
- **Post-launch reformulation** if sensory or stability issues surface
- **Lessons-learned documentation** fed back into Stage 0 of next project

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Closure:** The FPD process is a **closed-loop, learning system** — the output of one project becomes the input wisdom of the next. This is the *organisational learning* that distinguishes mature food companies from novice ones.

### 3.2 Engineering-Logic Summary Matrix

| **Phase** | **Dominant Function** | **Primary Risk** | **Risk-Mitigation Tool** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1–3 | Strategy & Ideation | Wasted effort on weak ideas | Stage-gate funnel |
| 4–5 | Concept | Market failure | Concept testing, PSM |
| 6 | Business Case | Financial loss | NPV, sensitivity analysis |
| 7 | R&D | Technical failure | ASLT, pilot plant, QbD |
| 8 | Scale-up | Manufacturing failure | Line trials, FMEA |
| 9 | Launch Readiness | Compliance failure | Regulatory checklist |
| 10 | Commercial | Slow uptake | Marketing, reformulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Stage-Gate Food Product Development Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Market Need or Strategic Trigger]) --> stage0["STAGE 0: Strategy and Charter"]
    stage0 --> gate1[/"GATE 1: Idea Screening"/]
    gate1 -->|Approved| stage1["STAGE 1: Idea Generation and Discovery"]
    stage1 --> gate2[/"GATE 2: Concept Testing"/]
    gate2 -->|Pass 60% Intent| stage2["STAGE 2: Concept Development"]
    stage2 --> gate3[/"GATE 3: Business Case Review"/]
    gate3 -->|Go Decision| stage3["STAGE 3: Product and Process Development"]
    stage3 --> stage4["STAGE 4: In-Plant and Market Trials"]
    stage4 --> gate4[/"GATE 4: Launch Authorization"/]
    gate4 -->|Approved| stage5["STAGE 5: Launch and Commercialization"]
    stage5 --> stage6["STAGE 6: Post-Launch Review"]
    stage6 --> loopback([Feedback to Stage 0 of Next Project])
    gate1 -.->|Reject| kill1([Project Killed])
    gate2 -.->|Reject| kill2([Project Killed])
    gate3 -.->|Kill| kill3([Project Killed])
    gate4 -.->|Kill| kill4([Project Killed])
    stage6 --> loopback
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Rectangular nodes are *Stages* (activities), parallelogram-shaped nodes are *Gates* (decision checkpoints), and circular nodes are *terminals* (kill or feedback loops). Dotted lines indicate the **kill path** — a feature unique to the Stage-Gate model.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cross-Functional Inputs to FPD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    consumer["Consumer Insights and VOC"] --> fpdcore
    rd["R and D and Formulation"] --> fpdcore
    process["Process Engineering and Scale-up"] --> fpdcore
    packaging["Packaging Technology"] --> fpdcore
    regulatory["Regulatory and Quality"] --> fpdcore
    finance["Finance and Costing"] --> fpdcore
    marketing["Marketing and Brand"] --> fpdcore
    supply["Supply Chain and Procurement"] --> fpdcore
    fpdcore["FOOD PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT CORE PROCESS"] --> output["Commercialised Food Product on Shelf"]
    output --> feedback["Post-Launch Sales and Consumer Data"]
    feedback --> consumer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask *"List the functions involved in FPD."* The eight functions above form a **complete answer** and demonstrate the *multidisciplinary engineering* nature of food product development.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A carries 3 marks (short answer), Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** (two sub-parts of 7 marks each). Mark allocation, depth, and answer structure reflect actual KTU valuation key expectations.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*Define food product development. List any four reasons why food companies develop new products.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Food product development is the systematic, multidisciplinary process of transforming a product idea into a commercially viable, safe, legal, and consumer-acceptable food product, encompassing ideation, formulation, testing, scale-up, and market launch.
>
> **Any four reasons (1.5 marks — 0.375 each, or 4 × ~0.4):**
> 1. To meet **changing consumer preferences** (e.g., demand for low-sugar, high-protein foods).
> 2. To **respond to competitive pressure** (counter-launch against rival brands).
> 3. To **comply with new regulations** (e.g., trans-fat bans, front-of-pack labelling laws).
> 4. To **exploit new technologies** (e.g., high-pressure processing, 3D food printing).
> 5. To **utilise surplus raw materials** or reduce food waste streams.
> 6. To **expand into new market segments** or geographies.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*What is the Stage-Gate process? Name its main stages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Stage-Gate process** (developed by Dr. Robert G. Cooper) is a project management model that divides the new product development journey into a series of **Stages (activities)** separated by **Gates (decision checkpoints)**. Each Gate serves as a quality and strategic filter, allowing the firm to **continue, revise, recycle, or kill** a project based on predefined criteria, thereby minimising risk and resource wastage.
>
> **Main Stages (2 marks — any four to five acceptable):**
> 1. **Stage 0** – Strategy and Charter
> 2. **Stage 1** – Discovery and Idea Generation
> 3. **Stage 2** – Concept Development
> 4. **Stage 3** – Business Case Building
> 5. **Stage 4** – Product and Process Development
> 6. **Stage 5** – Testing and Validation (in-plant and market)
> 7. **Stage 6** – Launch and Commercialization
> 8. **Stage 7** – Post-Launch Review

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **various stages involved in the new food product development process** with a neat flow diagram. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Discuss the **factors influencing new food product development** with suitable examples from the Indian food industry. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stages of New Food Product Development (7 marks)**

The FPD process is best described by the **Stage-Gate model** (Cooper), comprising the following sequential stages:

1. **Stage 0 — Strategy and Charter:** The firm identifies portfolio gaps, defines innovation strategy, and approves project charters. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Stage 1 — Idea Generation:** Ideas are sourced from consumer insights, competitor analysis, supplier technology, and trend reports. Techniques like **SCAMPER and Design Thinking** are used. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Gate 1 — Idea Screening:** Ideas are evaluated against market size, technical feasibility, and strategic fit using a scoring matrix; only top ideas advance. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Stage 2 — Concept Development:** Surviving ideas are written as product concepts stating target consumer, benefit, occasion, and positioning. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Gate 2 — Concept Testing:** Quantitative consumer research (n $\geq 150$) with **Top-2-Box Purchase Intent $\geq 60\%$** as a pass criterion. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Stage 3 — Business Case:** Formulation lock-in, cost engineering, regulatory review, NPV/IRR computation, and risk assessment. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Gate 3 — Business Case Review:** Senior management go/no-go decision based on financial viability. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Stage 4 — Product & Process Development:** Lab trials, pilot-plant scale-up, ASLT (Accelerated Shelf-Life Testing), packaging validation, sensory profiling. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **Stage 5 — In-Plant & Market Testing:** Line trials at commercial speed; test-marketing; consumer home-use tests. *(0.5 mark)*
10. **Gate 4 — Launch Authorization:** Final regulatory and operational sign-off. *(0.5 mark)*
11. **Stage 6 — Launch & Commercialization:** Production start, distribution, trade and consumer marketing. *(0.5 mark)*
12. **Stage 7 — Post-Launch Review:** Sales tracking, complaint analysis, reformulation, lessons-learned fed back to next project. *(0.5 mark)*

> **Neat Flow Diagram (1 mark):** A flow diagram showing all stages and gates in sequence, with kill-paths branching from each gate, must be drawn.

**(b) Factors Influencing New Food Product Development (7 marks)**

1. **Consumer Trends (1.5 marks):** Health consciousness, vegetarianism, veganism, gluten-free, organic, clean-label demands. *Example: Britannia's NutriChoice range.*
2. **Technological Advances (1.5 marks):** HPP, ohmic heating, pulsed electric field, 3D food printing, novel ingredients (plant proteins, alternative sweeteners). *Example: Licious plant-based meat.*
3. **Regulatory Environment (1 mark):** FSSAI labelling, fortification mandates, trans-fat bans. *Example: Mandatory +F symbol for fortified foods.*
4. **Competitive Pressure (1 mark):** Need to match or preempt rival launches. *Example: Maggi's quick response to Yippee! Noodles.*
5. **Raw Material & Supply Chain Dynamics (1 mark):** Seasonal availability, import costs, sustainability. *Example: Use of millets in ITC Aashirvaad range responding to climate-resilient cropping.*
6. **Economic & Cost Factors (0.5 mark):** Inflation of input costs driving reformulation with cheaper alternatives without compromising quality.
7. **Demographic Shifts (0.5 mark):** Urbanisation, nuclear families, working women driving demand for ready-to-eat and convenience foods.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**
*(a)* Describe the **Stage-Gate process model in food product development** with a neat diagram and explain the role of **Gates** in the process. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Explain the **role of market research and consumer testing** in food product development. Describe the types of consumer tests conducted at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stage-Gate Process Model (7 marks)**

The **Stage-Gate process** is a structured innovation management framework that organises FPD into **five to seven Stages** of activity separated by **four to five Gates** (decision points). The model is **front-loaded**, meaning that the maximum learning and project killing happen *before* significant money is spent.

**Key characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Stages** are *action-oriented* — multidisciplinary teams execute defined activities.
- **Gates** are *decision-oriented* — senior management reviews deliverables against preset criteria and chooses one of four actions: **Go, Kill, Hold, or Recycle**.
- **Front-loading** ensures that weak projects are identified at the **cheapest possible point**.
- **Parallel processing** of technical, market, and financial workstreams reduces calendar time.

**Roles of Gates (3 marks):**
1. **Quality Control** — ensures each deliverable meets minimum standards before more money is committed.
2. **Risk Management** — explicit kill-decisions at each gate prevent the *sunk-cost fallacy*.
3. **Resource Allocation** — only the strongest projects advance, freeing R&D capacity.
4. **Cross-Functional Alignment** — gates force R&D, Marketing, Finance, and Regulatory to agree on go/no-go.
5. **Strategic Discipline** — gates enforce portfolio coherence with the firm's overall innovation strategy.
6. **Learning Capture** — even killed projects generate knowledge for the firm's innovation memory.

**Neat Diagram (1 mark):** A flow diagram showing alternating **Stage → Gate → Stage** blocks with kill-paths emerging from each gate, plus a feedback loop from Post-Launch Review back to Strategy.

**(b) Role of Market Research and Consumer Testing (7 marks)**

Market research and consumer testing are the **voice-of-customer mechanisms** that de-risk the FPD process. They provide *empirical evidence* to support the inherently subjective decisions in product innovation.

**Purpose (1.5 marks):**
- Identify unmet needs and white-space opportunities.
- Validate concepts before formulation investment.
- Refine product attributes (taste, texture, packaging, price).
- Predict market share, trial rate, and repeat-purchase probability.
- Diagnose failures post-launch.

**Types of Consumer Tests at Each Stage (5.5 marks):**

| **FPD Stage** | **Type of Test** | **Sample Size** | **Key Output** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| Idea Generation | Qualitative ethnography, focus groups, in-home visits | 8–30 | Insight themes, need-states |
| Concept Development | Concept screening surveys | 150–300 | Purchase intent score |
| Concept Testing | Quantitative monadic test | 150–300 | Top-2-Box, uniqueness, believability |
| Product Testing (Proto 1) | Central location tests (CLT) — blind | 100–200 | Hedonic scores, attribute diagnostics |
| Product Testing (Proto 2) | Home-use tests (HUT) | 100–150 | Repeat-purchase intent, usage occasions |
| Pre-Launch | Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter | 200+ | Optimal price range |
| Pre-Launch | Simulated test market (e.g., BASES) | 300+ | Trial rate, projected market share |
| Post-Launch | Tracking studies, complaint log mining | Continuous | Issue diagnosis, reformulation triggers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Stage" with "Gate":** Many students write "Idea Screening" as a stage — it is a *Gate* (a decision). Examiners deduct **1 mark** per such error.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark "explain the process" question *must* include a flow diagram. Missing it typically costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring the *consumer* perspective:** FPD is not just R&D. Any answer that omits consumer testing, market research, or purchase-intent metrics is considered **incomplete** in KTU valuation.
> 4. **No Indian examples:** Generic global examples (Coca-Cola, Nestlé) are acceptable, but adding *Indian case studies* (Britannia, ITC, Amul, Haldiram's, Britannia NutriChoice, Licious, Paper Boat) consistently scores **0.5–1 bonus mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Writing only the "happy path":** Always mention that **projects can be KILLED at any gate**. A process answer that has no kill-path is technically incomplete.
> 6. **Confusing product development with product design:** Product *design* focuses on formulation, sensory, and packaging attributes. Product *development* is the *end-to-end process* from idea to launch. Examiners are strict about this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** FPD = systematic, multidisciplinary transformation of an idea into a *safe, legal, consumer-accepted, profitable* food product.
- **Stage-Gate Model** is the *default expected answer* for any "explain the FPD process" question. **Always draw the diagram.**
- **Stages** = activities (work happens). **Gates** = decisions (Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle).
- **Seven canonical stages:** Strategy → Discovery → Concept → Business Case → Development → Testing → Launch → Post-Launch.
- **Front-loading principle:** Maximum learning and maximum project-killing happen *before* large money is spent.
- **The three pillars of FPD:** *Consumer Pull* + *Technology Push* + *Strategic Fit*.
- **Six functions of FPD (must be able to list all):** R&D, Marketing, Production/Engineering, Regulatory/Quality, Finance, Supply Chain.
- **Concept testing** requires $\geq 150$ consumers and a Top-2-Box Purchase Intent $\geq 60\%$ for a pass.
- **ASLT** (Accelerated Shelf-Life Testing) uses Arrhenius kinetics to predict ambient shelf-life from elevated-temperature data.
- **Business case** must pass NPV, IRR ($\geq$ WACC + 5%), and payback ($\leq 36$ months) criteria.
- **FSSAI compliance** is mandatory at Gate 4 (Launch Authorization) — product approval, label, and license.
- **Post-launch review** closes the loop: lessons feed into the next project's Stage 0 — this is *organisational learning*.
- **Indian examples to memorise:** Britannia NutriChoice (health), Maggi (instant noodles), Licious (protein), ITC Aashirvaad (millets), Amul (dairy innovation), Paper Boat (beverages).
- **Failure rate:** ~80% of new food products fail within 2 years — hence the *rigour* of the Stage-Gate discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Success and Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Success and Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Success** in the context of food product development refers to a newly launched food product achieving its pre-defined commercial, technical, and strategic objectives within a stipulated time frame. These objectives typically include **market share capture**, **profitability benchmarks**, **consumer adoption rate**, and **distribution penetration**.

Conversely, **Product Failure** is defined as the inability of a newly introduced food product to meet one or more of its commercial, technical, or market-driven objectives, resulting in discontinuation, market withdrawal, or significant underperformance relative to forecasts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification of product outcomes is **not binary**. Industry frameworks categorize outcomes into a spectrum ranging from **Absolute Success** to **Absolute Failure**, including intermediate states like **Partial Success**, **Relative Failure**, and **Dud** (neither success nor failure). Students must distinguish between these categories, as KTU exam questions often test this classification precision.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are launching a new **instant filter coffee premix** in Kerala. You invest ₹50 lakhs in formulation, packaging, and initial marketing.

- **Product Success Analogy:** The premix sells out within 3 months, captures 8% of the regional instant coffee market, and earns a 15% ROI. The shelf-stable product has a 12-month shelf life, retail chains reorder consistently, and consumers post positive reviews online. **This is Product Success.**

- **Product Failure Analogy:** The same premix launches with a poorly understood "bitter robusta" profile that South Indian consumers find unappealing. Sales are 70% below forecast, retailers refuse reorders after initial stock clears, and the product is delisted within 6 months. **This is Product Failure.**

- **Partial Success Analogy:** The premix sells moderately, achieves break-even, but fails to capture the projected market share or ROI. It survives in the market but does not thrive. **This is a Partial Success / Relative Failure.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Success and failure in food product development are measured against **pre-launch objectives**, not against an absolute standard. A product can be a commercial success in one geography and a failure in another, depending on regional consumer expectations, price sensitivity, and competitive intensity.

## 1.3 Industry Benchmarks & Statistics

New food product development is a high-risk activity. Industry research consistently highlights:

- Approximately **70% to 80%** of new food products fail within their first year in the market.
- Only about **10% to 15%** of new packaged food products achieve sustained commercial success.
- The average development cost for a major food product launch can range from **₹50 lakhs to ₹50 crores** depending on scale, regulatory complexity, and category.
- Time-to-market for a typical packaged food product ranges from **12 to 36 months** from concept to retail shelf.

These statistics underscore the critical importance of understanding success and failure factors early in the product development lifecycle — a core focus of PEFTT411.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Product Outcomes (Industry Standard Framework)

The food industry does not view success and failure as a simple binary. The following five-category framework is widely accepted:

### 2.1.1 Absolute Success
- Product **exceeds all forecasted objectives** — sales, profit, market share, and consumer acceptance.
- Achieves category leadership or significant niche dominance.
- Example: A premium organic cold-pressed juice that becomes the top-selling SKU in its category within 18 months.

### 2.1.2 Partial Success
- Product **meets some objectives but falls short** on others.
- Typically achieves acceptable sales but below forecast, or strong consumer trials but weak repeat purchase.
- Example: A probiotic yogurt drink that gains good trial rates but low repurchase due to taste fatigue.

### 2.1.3 Dud
- Product neither succeeds nor fails dramatically.
- Survives in the market with minimal marketing support, often as a "filler SKU" in the company's portfolio.
- Generates neither significant profit nor significant loss.

### 2.1.4 Relative Failure
- Product is technically withdrawn or repositioned.
- Achieves some sales but well below expectations, leading to reformulation, repackaging, or relaunch.
- Example: A gluten-free snack that performed poorly initially but succeeded after reformulation and repositioning as a "fitness snack."

### 2.1.5 Absolute Failure
- Product is **completely withdrawn from the market**.
- Significant financial loss; brand equity may be damaged.
- Example: A novel insect-protein bar that was rejected outright by consumers due to cultural aversion.

## 2.2 Critical Factors Influencing Product Success

Food product success depends on the intersection of multiple factors. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following classification:

### 2.2.1 Organizational Factors
- Senior management commitment to innovation.
- R&D infrastructure and technical capability.
- Cross-functional coordination between marketing, R&D, production, and supply chain.
- Innovation culture and risk tolerance.
- Budget allocation for new product development.

### 2.2.2 Marketing Factors
- Quality of market research and consumer insights.
- Strength of brand equity and brand association.
- Effectiveness of positioning strategy.
- Pricing strategy relative to perceived value.
- Distribution and shelf availability.
- Promotional and launch campaign effectiveness.

### 2.2.3 Product / Technical Factors
- Product quality (sensory, nutritional, safety).
- Differentiation from competitive products.
- Packaging functionality and appeal.
- Shelf life and stability.
- Regulatory compliance (FSSAI, BIS, etc.).
- Cost of goods sold and production scalability.

### 2.2.4 Project / Process Factors
- Adherence to Stage-Gate process.
- Quality of launch execution.
- Speed-to-market vs. development thoroughness.
- Prototype testing rigor.
- Pilot plant and scale-up success.

## 2.3 Critical Factors Influencing Product Failure

Research identifies the most common reasons for food product failure, in order of frequency:

1. **Inadequate market analysis** — misunderstanding consumer needs, wants, and trends.
2. **Poor product positioning** — unclear value proposition or wrong target segment.
3. **Weak differentiation** — product is too similar to existing market offerings.
4. **Pricing errors** — priced too high for perceived value or too low to sustain margins.
5. **Sensory shortcomings** — taste, texture, aroma, or appearance not aligned with consumer expectations.
6. **Insufficient marketing support** — poor launch campaign, weak distribution, low advertising spend.
7. **Technical problems** — shelf life issues, packaging defects, production inconsistencies.
8. **Timing errors** — launching too early (product not ready) or too late (market opportunity missed).
9. **Competitive intensity** — better-priced or better-positioned competitor products entering simultaneously.
10. **Organizational issues** — internal politics, poor cross-functional collaboration, or premature termination.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Metric | Application in Food Product Dev |
|---|---|---|
| Product Success Rate | Percentage of new products meeting objectives | Industry benchmark: **10–15%** for new packaged foods |
| Trial Rate | Percentage of target consumers who purchase at least once | Success requires **> 40% trial** in target segment |
| Repeat Purchase Rate | Percentage of triers who buy again within 30 days | Success requires **> 50% repeat** within 30 days |
| ROI Threshold | Minimum return on investment over 24 months | Typical threshold: **> 15% IRR** for FMCG launches |
| Time-to-Market | Concept-to-shelf duration | Optimal: **12–18 months** for packaged food |
| Cannibalization Rate | Percentage of new product sales from existing company products | Acceptable: **< 30%** cannibalization |
| Brand Dilution Index | Decline in parent brand equity post-launch | Must remain **< 5%** dilution |
| Failure Cost Ratio | Total loss ÷ total investment in NPP | Industry average: **40–60%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU 2024 questions may ask you to calculate or interpret a simple success/failure metric. The table above covers the high-yield formulas. Note that no advanced mathematical derivations are required for this topic — it is primarily **conceptual and analytical**, not computational.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

Understanding product success and failure is essential for:

- **Strategic portfolio management** in food companies.
- **Resource allocation** decisions for R&D budgets.
- **Risk mitigation** through Stage-Gate evaluation.
- **Post-launch review (PLR)** processes to extract learnings.
- **Innovation pipeline health** assessment in FMCG, dairy, bakery, beverage, and snack industries.
- **Academic and industrial research** in food technology and management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Evaluation Matrix

## 3.1 The Cooper's Stage-Gate Success-Failure Evaluation Framework

The most widely used framework in food product development for predicting and evaluating success and failure is **Robert G. Cooper's Stage-Gate Process**. Below is the step-by-step analytical breakdown:

### Step 1: Idea Generation (Discovery)
- The process begins with sourcing new product ideas from consumers, distributors, R&D, competitors, and trend analysts.
- Each idea is logged with a brief description and preliminary strategic fit assessment.

### Step 2: Stage 1 — Initial Screen
- The first gate evaluates whether the idea aligns with the company's strategy, market trends, and technical feasibility.
- **Decision Criteria:** Strategic fit, market attractiveness, technical feasibility, regulatory pathway clarity.
- **Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle decision** is made.

### Step 3: Stage 2 — Preliminary Business Case
- A more detailed business case is constructed, including market sizing, competitive analysis, preliminary financials, and technical assessment.
- **Deliverable:** A 10–15 page preliminary business case document.

### Step 4: Gate 2 — Second Decision Point
- Senior management reviews the preliminary business case.
- **Decision Criteria:** Market potential, ROI projection, risk assessment, resource requirement.
- Project proceeds, is killed, held, or recycled.

### Step 5: Stage 3 — Development
- Detailed product formulation, process development, packaging design, and consumer testing.
- Sensory panels, shelf-life studies, nutritional analysis, and regulatory compliance are conducted.

### Step 6: Stage 4 — Validation
- In-home usage tests, central location tests, and test marketing are conducted.
- **Critical success predictors** measured: trial rate, repeat purchase, willingness to pay, distribution acceptance.

### Step 7: Gate 3 — Final Go-to-Market Decision
- Launch decision is finalized based on validation results.
- Production scale-up, distribution contracts, and launch marketing are committed.

### Step 8: Stage 5 — Launch and Post-Launch Review
- Product enters the market.
- A **Post-Launch Review (PLR)** is conducted 6–12 months after launch to assess actual vs. forecasted performance.
- Findings feed back into the next innovation cycle.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Success vs. Failure Predictors

The following analytical matrix maps each evaluation criterion to its expected impact on success or failure:

| Evaluation Criterion | Strong Performance Predicts | Weak Performance Predicts |
|---|---|---|
| Consumer Insight Quality | High success probability | High failure probability |
| Product Differentiation | Defensible market position | Commoditization, price war |
| Sensory Acceptability | Strong trial + repeat | Trial without repeat |
| Pricing Alignment | Strong value perception | Slow uptake or margin erosion |
| Distribution Coverage | Wide retail penetration | Shelf gaps, low visibility |
| Launch Marketing Spend | Strong awareness, trial | Weak awareness, low trial |
| Shelf Life Adequacy | No returns, consistent quality | Returns, complaints, brand damage |
| Regulatory Compliance | Smooth market entry | Legal issues, recalls |

## 3.3 Mathematical Formulation — Success Probability Model

While food product success prediction is largely qualitative, a simplified **composite success score** can be constructed as follows:

$$
S = w_1 \cdot C + w_2 \cdot D + w_3 \cdot P + w_4 \cdot M + w_5 \cdot T
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Composite Success Score (0 to 100)
- $C$ = Consumer Insight Score (0 to 100)
- $D$ = Product Differentiation Score (0 to 100)
- $P$ = Pricing-Market Fit Score (0 to 100)
- $M$ = Marketing Strength Score (0 to 100)
- $T$ = Technical Readiness Score (0 to 100)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5$ = Weighted importance factors, where:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 1
$$

### Interpretation Thresholds:

$$
S \geq 75 \implies \text{High probability of Absolute Success}
$$

$$
60 \leq S < 75 \implies \text{Partial Success likely}
$$

$$
45 \leq S < 60 \implies \text{Dud or Relative Failure risk}
$$

$$
S < 45 \implies \text{High probability of Absolute Failure}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Suppose a new product scores the following in a pre-launch evaluation: $C = 80$, $D = 70$, $P = 75$, $M = 65$, $T = 85$, with equal weights $w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = w_5 = 0.2$.

$$
S = (0.2 \times 80) + (0.2 \times 70) + (0.2 \times 75) + (0.2 \times 65) + (0.2 \times 85)
$$

$$
S = 16 + 14 + 15 + 13 + 17 = 75
$$

Since $S = 75$, the product is at the threshold of **High Probability of Absolute Success**. This justifies proceeding to launch with confident expectations.

## 3.4 Detailed Analysis — Why Food Products Fail More Than Other FMCG Categories

Food products face unique challenges that increase failure rates:

### 3.4.1 High Consumer Trial Cost in Sensory Terms
Unlike a shampoo or detergent where trial is relatively low-risk, food products are **ingested**. A poor sensory experience creates an immediate, visceral rejection that is rarely reversible.

### 3.4.2 Short Product Life Cycles
Food trends (e.g., keto, plant-based, low-sugar) evolve rapidly. A product developed for a trend may be obsolete by launch.

### 3.4.3 Regulatory Stringency
FSSAI 2006, FSSAI labelling regulations 2020, advertising regulations, and BIS standards impose strict compliance. Non-compliance can lead to forced withdrawal.

### 3.4.4 Cold Chain and Distribution Sensitivity
Many food products require temperature-controlled logistics, which increase cost and failure risk in distribution.

### 3.4.5 Cultural and Regional Variation
India, and specifically Kerala, has strong regional food preferences. A product designed for a pan-Indian audience may fail in Kerala due to taste, texture, or form factor mismatch.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Post-Launch Review (PLR) Procedure

The PLR is the formal mechanism for classifying a product as a success or failure:

### Step 1: Define Evaluation Period
- Typically 6 to 12 months post-launch.

### Step 2: Collect Performance Data
- Sales volume, revenue, market share, distribution numbers, consumer complaint rates, return rates, repeat purchase data.

### Step 3: Compare Against Launch Forecasts
- Actual vs. forecasted sales, ROI, trial, repeat.

### Step 4: Classify Outcome
- Apply the five-category framework (Absolute Success, Partial Success, Dud, Relative Failure, Absolute Failure).

### Step 5: Conduct Root Cause Analysis
- Identify what worked, what did not, and why.

### Step 6: Extract Learnings
- Feed learnings back into the next Stage-Gate cycle.

### Step 7: Decide Action
- Continue, harvest, reposition, reformulate, or withdraw.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Product Success and Failure Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([New Food Product Launched]) --> nodeB{Does it meet forecast sales targets?}
    nodeB -->|Yes, exceeds 120 percent| nodeC[Absolute Success]
    nodeB -->|Yes, between 80 and 120 percent| nodeD[Partial Success]
    nodeB -->|Marginal, 50 to 80 percent| nodeE[Dud]
    nodeB -->|Below 50 percent| nodeF{Reformulate or Reposition possible?}
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Relative Failure - Repositioned]
    nodeF -->|No| nodeH[Absolute Failure - Withdrawn]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Expand Distribution]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Targeted Marketing Push]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Maintain as Portfolio Filler]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Re-Launch Evaluation]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Post-Mortem Analysis and Learnings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart provides a simplified decision tree that mirrors how FMCG companies like ITC, Nestlé, and Britannia classify and act upon product outcomes. KTU examiners often award marks for representing classification logic in flowchart form.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stage-Gate Process Showing Success and Failure Checkpoints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Stage 1 - Idea Generation] --> gate1{Gate 1 - Initial Screen}
    gate1 -->|Kill| kill1[Project Terminated]
    gate1 -->|Go| stage2[Stage 2 - Business Case]
    stage2 --> gate2{Gate 2 - Business Review}
    gate2 -->|Kill| kill2[Project Terminated]
    gate2 -->|Go| stage3[Stage 3 - Development]
    stage3 --> gate3{Gate 3 - Pre-Launch Review}
    gate3 -->|Kill| kill3[Project Terminated]
    gate3 -->|Go| stage4[Stage 4 - Launch and Commercialization]
    stage4 --> plr[Post-Launch Review]
    plr --> outcome{Outcome Classification}
    outcome --> succ[Absolute or Partial Success]
    outcome --> dud[Dud]
    outcome --> fail[Relative or Absolute Failure]
    fail --> learnings[Learnings Fed Back to Stage 1]
    learnings --> stage1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Success Factor Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph orgBlock[Organizational Factors]
        org1[Senior Management Commitment]
        org2[R and D Capability]
        org3[Innovation Culture]
    end
    subgraph mktBlock[Marketing Factors]
        mkt1[Consumer Insight Quality]
        mkt2[Brand Equity]
        mkt3[Distribution Strength]
    end
    subgraph prdBlock[Product Technical Factors]
        prd1[Sensory Quality]
        prd2[Shelf Life and Stability]
        prd3[Regulatory Compliance]
    end
    subgraph prcBlock[Project Process Factors]
        prc1[Stage Gate Discipline]
        prc2[Pilot Plant Success]
        prc3[Launch Execution]
    end
    orgBlock --> successCenter((Product Success))
    mktBlock --> successCenter
    prdBlock --> successCenter
    prcBlock --> successCenter
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** Each of the four factor blocks (Organizational, Marketing, Product, Process) feeds into a central "Product Success" node. In a KTU 14-mark question on success factors, this four-block classification should be your default organizing structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Partial Success" in the context of food product development. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

Partial Success refers to a product outcome where the launched food product achieves **some, but not all, of its pre-defined commercial and strategic objectives**. Typically, the product may achieve acceptable sales volume or break-even profitability, but fall short of forecasted market share, ROI, or repeat purchase targets. The product remains in the market and generates revenue, but does not deliver the full value envisioned at the launch stage. Examples include a probiotic yogurt drink that achieves strong initial trial but moderate repeat purchase, or a premium cookie that sells well in urban metros but underperforms in tier-2 and tier-3 cities relative to forecast.

### Question 2: List any three critical reasons for the failure of new food products in the Indian market. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

Three critical reasons for new food product failure in India are:

1. **Inadequate consumer insight and market research** — Misunderstanding regional taste preferences, price sensitivity, or cultural food habits leads to products that do not resonate with the target segment.

2. **Sensory shortcomings** — A food product that fails on taste, texture, aroma, or visual appeal will lose repeat purchase, which is the strongest predictor of long-term failure.

3. **Weak distribution and shelf availability** — Even a well-formulated product will underperform if it is not physically available at the point of purchase when the consumer seeks it, especially in rural and semi-urban India.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students write about success and failure factors in a **generic**, unstructured manner, mixing organizational, marketing, product, and process factors into a single list. This loses marks. Always use the **four-block classification** (Organizational, Marketing, Product/Technical, Project/Process).
> - Do not confuse **partial success** with **absolute success**, or **dud** with **relative failure**. The five-category framework is a high-value differentiator in valuation.
> - Avoid providing real-world examples without context. Always tie examples back to a specific factor. For instance, do not just say "Maggi faced a ban"; say "Maggi's 2015 ban resulted from a regulatory compliance failure (lead content exceeding FSSAI limits) combined with a crisis communication gap (marketing factor), illustrating the intersection of technical and marketing factors in absolute failure."

---

### Question A: "Product success and failure in the food industry is determined by the interplay of organizational, marketing, product, and project factors." Critically analyse this statement with suitable examples from the Indian food industry. [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — Discuss the four major factor categories that influence product success, with examples. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The statement is accurate and reflects the consensus view in food product development literature. Product outcomes are determined by the integrated performance across four factor categories:

**1. Organizational Factors** — These include senior management commitment, R&D capability, innovation culture, and resource allocation. A company like **ITC** has institutionalized innovation through dedicated R&D centers and an innovation-focused leadership, which has enabled consistent product success across categories (Aashirvaad, Sunfeast, Bingo!). Conversely, companies that treat innovation as a one-off project rather than a cultural priority tend to suffer serial failures.

**2. Marketing Factors** — These include consumer insight quality, brand equity, positioning, pricing, distribution, and launch campaign strength. **Amul** has built a powerful brand equity that allows it to launch new products with high initial trust, reducing perceived trial risk. The failure of many new entrants in the Indian dairy category is often due to weak brand equity, poor distribution, or misaligned positioning.

**3. Product / Technical Factors** — These include sensory quality, nutritional profile, shelf life, packaging, regulatory compliance, and differentiation. The **2015 Maggi noodles crisis** is a classic example of product/technical factor failure — elevated lead content violated FSSAI standards, leading to a nationwide ban and reputational damage, despite Maggi's strong brand equity (a marketing factor that helped recovery post-2016).

**4. Project / Process Factors** — These include Stage-Gate discipline, pilot plant success, launch execution rigor, and post-launch review quality. **Britannia's** successful relaunch of products like Bourbon and Treat demonstrates strong process discipline, while many SMEs in India's food sector fail not because of weak products, but because of poor launch execution and absence of structured post-launch review.

#### Part (b) — Discuss the five-category classification of product outcomes and explain why it is more useful than a binary success/failure view. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The food industry uses a **five-category classification** for product outcomes:

1. **Absolute Success** — Product exceeds all forecasts. Example: A new flavored yogurt by **Epigamia** that captures 5% of the premium yogurt segment within 12 months.

2. **Partial Success** — Product meets some objectives. Example: A new variant of **Parle-G** that performs well in North India but underperforms in South India.

3. **Dud** — Product neither succeeds nor fails. Example: A niche SKU of **ITC** that survives in the portfolio with minimal marketing support.

4. **Relative Failure** — Product withdrawn, then repositioned and relaunched. Example: A failed launch of a sugar-free candy that succeeded after reformulation and repositioning as a diabetic-friendly product.

5. **Absolute Failure** — Product completely withdrawn with significant loss. Example: Some early plant-based meat analogues in India that failed to gain consumer acceptance.

This five-category framework is **more useful than a binary view** for several reasons:

- It enables **nuanced portfolio management** — a Dud is not a Failure and may be retained as a low-revenue filler SKU.
- It informs **differentiated post-launch actions** — a Partial Success may warrant marketing investment, while an Absolute Failure requires immediate withdrawal and root-cause analysis.
- It supports **better learning extraction** — Root cause analysis differs sharply between a Relative Failure (repositioning opportunity) and an Absolute Failure (strategic or conceptual error).
- It aligns with **financial reality** — financial classification (profitable vs. loss-making) is independent of market performance classification.

In conclusion, the statement is validated by industry practice. The interplay of the four factor categories determines which of the five outcome categories a product ultimately falls into. A balanced, well-resourced company that manages all four factors in concert is far more likely to achieve Absolute Success.

---

### Question B: "The Stage-Gate process is the most effective framework for predicting and managing product success and failure." Discuss the Stage-Gate process and explain how it enables success and mitigates failure in food product development. [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern — Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### Part (a) — Describe the Stage-Gate process with its key stages and gates. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The **Stage-Gate Process**, developed by Robert G. Cooper, is the most widely adopted innovation management framework globally, including in the Indian food industry. It structures product development as a sequence of stages separated by decision gates.

**Key Stages:**

- **Stage 1 — Discovery / Idea Generation:** Sourcing and logging new product ideas from internal and external sources.
- **Stage 2 — Preliminary Business Case:** Quick market, technical, and financial assessment to build a preliminary business case.
- **Stage 3 — Development:** Detailed formulation, process development, packaging design, consumer testing, and regulatory work.
- **Stage 4 — Validation:** In-home usage tests, central location tests, test marketing, and pilot launch.
- **Stage 5 — Launch and Commercialization:** Full market launch with production scale-up, distribution, and marketing execution.

**Key Gates (Decision Points):**

- **Gate 1 — Initial Screen:** Quick strategic fit, feasibility, and attractiveness check.
- **Gate 2 — Business Case Review:** Detailed review of market potential, ROI, and risk.
- **Gate 3 — Pre-Launch Review:** Validation of test market results and final go-to-market decision.
- **Gate 4 — Post-Launch Review:** Evaluation of actual vs. forecasted performance.

At each gate, management decides: **Go, Kill, Hold, or Recycle.** This structured decision-making is the core mechanism by which the Stage-Gate process prevents weak projects from advancing and consuming resources, and ensures that only well-validated products reach launch.

#### Part (b) — Explain how the Stage-Gate process enables product success and mitigates failure. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The Stage-Gate process enables product success and mitigates failure through several mechanisms:

**1. Early Failure Detection and Resource Conservation:**
By introducing decision gates early, the process identifies weak projects before significant investment is committed. For example, a concept failing consumer sensory testing at Stage 4 is killed before scale-up investment, saving the company from a likely market failure.

**2. Cross-Functional Discipline:**
Each gate requires input from R&D, marketing, finance, supply chain, and regulatory. This cross-functional discipline prevents the kind of internal misalignment that causes many food product failures (e.g., R&D developing a product that marketing cannot position effectively).

**3. Risk Reduction Through Sequential Validation:**
Each stage validates one or more critical assumptions — consumer need, technical feasibility, commercial viability, and execution capability. Sequential validation reduces the probability of catastrophic failure at launch.

**4. Data-Driven Decision Making:**
The Stage-Gate process mandates the use of measurable criteria (trial rate, repeat purchase, sensory scores, ROI projections) at each gate. This replaces intuition-based decisions with data-driven ones, which are less prone to cognitive bias and organizational politics.

**5. Learning Capture and Feedback:**
The **Post-Launch Review (PLR)** at Gate 4 captures learnings from both successes and failures, feeding them back into Stage 1 of the next cycle. This creates a **continuous improvement loop** that progressively improves the organization's success rate.

**Limitations to Acknowledge:**

- The Stage-Gate process can be **slow and bureaucratic**, potentially causing the company to miss fast-moving market trends.
- It may **stifle radical innovation**, which often requires tolerance for ambiguity and iteration outside the structured process.
- In SMEs with limited resources, the discipline of multiple gates may be impractical.

**Conclusion:**

Despite its limitations, the Stage-Gate process remains the most effective framework for managing the high-risk, high-investment nature of food product development. Indian food majors like **Britannia, ITC, and Nestlé India** use modified Stage-Gate systems to balance structure with speed. When combined with strong consumer insight, technical excellence, and disciplined execution, it significantly improves the probability of success and reduces the probability of catastrophic failure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Product Success and Failure**

- **Five Outcome Categories** (must know for KTU): Absolute Success, Partial Success, Dud, Relative Failure, Absolute Failure. These are NOT a binary classification.

- **Four Factor Categories** (default organizing structure for any 14-mark answer): Organizational, Marketing, Product/Technical, Project/Process. Always use this four-block structure in essay answers.

- **Top 3 Failure Reasons** (highest yield for 3-mark questions): Inadequate market research, weak product positioning, sensory shortcomings.

- **Success Benchmark**: Industry success rate for new food products is **10–15%**; failure rate is **70–80%** in the first year.

- **Cooper's Stage-Gate Process**: 5 stages (Discovery, Business Case, Development, Validation, Launch) separated by 4 gates. Each gate has Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle outcomes.

- **Composite Success Score** $S$: A weighted average of Consumer Insight, Differentiation, Pricing, Marketing, and Technical scores, normalized to 0–100. Threshold for Absolute Success is $S \geq 75$.

- **Post-Launch Review (PLR)**: Conducted 6–12 months after launch to classify outcome and extract learnings. This is a **mandatory** final gate in the Stage-Gate process.

- **Unique Food Industry Risk Factors**: Sensory trial cost, short trend cycles, regulatory stringency (FSSAI), cold chain dependency, regional cultural variation.

- **KTU 2024 Exam Tips**:
  * Always tie real-world Indian examples (Maggi, Amul, ITC, Britannia, Nestlé India, Parle, Epigamia) to specific factors — never give examples in isolation.
  * For 14-mark questions, structure answers with **clear sub-headings** matching the four-factor classification.
  * Mention **PLR** explicitly when discussing failure mitigation — it is a high-value term in the KTU 2024 rubric.
  * Always state the **five-category classification** when defining success or failure — not just "successful" or "unsuccessful."

- **Common Student Pitfalls**:
  * Confusing Partial Success with Absolute Success.
  * Treating product failure as solely a marketing problem (ignoring technical and process factors).
  * Failing to mention regulatory compliance (FSSAI) as a product/technical factor.
  * Writing about success factors without classifying them into the four-block structure.
  * Forgetting to discuss Post-Launch Review when asked about "mitigating failure."

- **Key Indian Case Studies to Remember**:
  * **Maggi 2015 Ban** — Product/Technical (FSSAI non-compliance) + Marketing (crisis communication) failure, later recovered through marketing strength.
  * **Amul's Product Portfolio** — Organizational innovation culture + strong distribution = consistent success.
  * **Epigamia** — Strong consumer insight in the Greek yogurt category led to category creation and success.
  * **ITC's Bingo!** — Strong product differentiation in a crowded snack market led to success despite late entry.
  * **Britannia Bourbon** — Sustained success through continuous product and packaging innovation (process factor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors for Success: Market Survey Process for Product Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors for Success: Market Survey Process for Product Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Market Survey in Product Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Market Survey** is a systematic, structured, and scientific process of gathering, recording, and analyzing information about the **preferences, buying behaviour, needs, attitudes, and opinions** of consumers or potential consumers with respect to a specific product, service, or idea. In the context of food product development, it acts as the **commercial bridge** between laboratory-level food innovation and real-world consumer acceptance.

In simpler words, before a food company decides to launch a new flavoured yogurt, a new snack, or a ready-to-eat meal, it must first ask the market: *"What do you actually want? What are you willing to pay for? What will you reject?"* The mechanism used to ask and analyse these questions is the **Market Survey Process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Remember:**
> - **Market Survey:** A short, often quantitative study aimed at assessing a specific market phenomenon.
> - **Market Research:** A broader, ongoing process that includes surveys, observations, experiments, and secondary data.
> - **Product Development:** The complete journey from idea generation to commercial launch (also called the **Stage-Gate Process**).

---

## 1.2 Defining "Factors for Success" in Food Product Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition:**
> **Success Factors** (also called **Critical Success Factors, CSFs**) in food product development are the **essential internal and external conditions, attributes, and strategic levers** that determine whether a new food product will be accepted by the market, technically feasible to manufacture, legally compliant to sell, and financially viable to sustain in the long term.

These factors act as the **performance indicators** that a product development team monitors at every stage. They decide whether the project will:
- Move from one **Stage-Gate** to the next, OR
- Be **terminated / killed** to save company resources.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of launching a new food product like **opening a new restaurant in Kerala**.

Before opening, the owner does not just start cooking. He:
1. **Talks to neighbours** (Market Survey) – "Would you prefer a Chinese restaurant or a traditional Kerala meals outlet?"
2. **Walks around the area** (Field Research) – Sees if there are schools, offices, or families nearby.
3. **Checks competitor prices** (Secondary Data) – "How much does the existing chai shop charge?"
4. **Tries a small menu** (Test Marketing) – "Let me introduce 3 dishes first and see which sells."
5. **Sets the price** (Pricing Strategy) – "Will customers pay ₹200 for a biryani?"
6. **Gets proper licenses** (Regulatory Compliance) – FSSAI license, GST, trade license.
7. **Plans a unique selling point (USP)** – "Our food is 100% coconut-oil-free" (Differentiation).

> **All these steps together are the Market Survey Process. If the owner skips them, the restaurant will fail. This is exactly what happens to food products that fail in the market — the company skipped the survey.**

---

## 1.4 Why Is the Market Survey Process Considered a "Factor for Success"?

The market survey is not a single factor — it is a **multi-layered factor** that feeds into almost every other success factor. Look at the hierarchy:

$$
\text{Product Success} \;=\; f(\text{Market Survey}, \text{Formulation}, \text{Regulatory}, \text{Finance}, \text{Operations})
$$

Here, the **Market Survey** is the first input that "fuels" all the other factors. Without it:
- The product may be technically perfect but **no one wants it**.
- The price may be wrong.
- The packaging may be wrong.
- The distribution channel may be wrong.

In the food industry, the failure rate of new products is alarmingly high:
> **Approximately 70–80% of new food products fail within the first year of launch** (industry-cited figure used in marketing textbooks).

The single biggest cause of this failure is **inadequate or absent market research**.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Success Factors in Food Product Development

The major categories of success factors that the market survey addresses are:

| **S.No** | **Category** | **What it Covers** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Consumer Factors** | Taste, preference, demographics, psychographics, lifestyle |
| 2 | **Market Factors** | Demand, supply, competition, market size, market share |
| 3 | **Product Factors** | Quality, shelf life, packaging, convenience, safety |
| 4 | **Financial Factors** | Cost, pricing, profit margin, break-even |
| 5 | **Technical Factors** | Process feasibility, equipment availability, scalability |
| 6 | **Regulatory Factors** | FSSAI, BIS, AGMARK, FPO, legal labelling |
| 7 | **Distribution Factors** | Channels, cold chain, retail reach |
| 8 | **Promotional Factors** | Branding, advertising, digital marketing |

The **Market Survey** is the tool that *quantifies and qualifies* all eight categories before product launch.

---

## 1.6 Highlighted Takeaways for This Section

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 1 Recap:**
> - **Market Survey** is the scientific process of collecting consumer/market opinions.
> - **Success Factors** are the CSFs that determine if a product will survive in the market.
> - The market survey is the **starting point** of new product development (NPD).
> - Skipping the market survey is the **#1 reason** for new food product failure.
> - The survey covers Consumer, Market, Product, Financial, Technical, Regulatory, Distribution, and Promotional factors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Market Survey Process — A Structured Theoretical Framework

The market survey process is **not random questioning**. It follows a strict scientific sequence. According to standard marketing and food product development literature adopted by KTU, the process consists of **six sequential stages**.

Let us analyse each stage in depth with the **WHY** and **HOW** behind it.

---

### Stage 1 — Defining the Research Problem and Objectives

**WHY is this stage needed?**
A poorly defined problem leads to irrelevant data. The researcher must know exactly *what* he/she wants to learn.

**HOW is it done?**
- Conduct internal discussions with the R&D, marketing, and sales teams.
- Use the **SMART framework** for objectives:
  - **S** – Specific
  - **M** – Measurable
  - **A** – Achievable
  - **R** – Relevant
  - **T** – Time-bound
- Define the **research questions** clearly.

**Example:**
- *Bad objective:* "Find out about customers."
- *Good objective:* "Determine the willingness of urban working women in Kochi (aged 25–40) to purchase a ₹50 millet-based ready-to-drink beverage for breakfast, within 3 months."

---

### Stage 2 — Designing the Research Plan

**WHY is this stage needed?**
To decide *how* the data will be collected, from *whom*, and using *which tools*.

**HOW is it done?**
The researcher selects:
- **Type of data:**
  - **Primary Data:** Collected fresh for the first time (surveys, interviews, focus groups).
  - **Secondary Data:** Already existing data (government reports, published journals, company sales records).
- **Research Approach:**
  - **Qualitative Research** → explores "why" and "how" people think. Tools: Focus Groups, In-depth Interviews, Ethnography.
  - **Quantitative Research** → measures "how many" or "how much". Tools: Structured Questionnaires, Rating Scales, Conjoint Analysis.
- **Sampling Method:**
  - **Probability Sampling:** Random, Stratified, Cluster, Systematic.
  - **Non-Probability Sampling:** Convenience, Judgemental, Snowball, Quota.
- **Sample Size Determination** (critical for validity).

---

### Stage 3 — Data Collection (Field Work)

**WHY is this stage needed?**
This is where the actual opinions, behaviours, and preferences are recorded.

**HOW is it done?**
Common tools of data collection in food industry:

| **Tool** | **Description** | **Used For** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Questionnaire** | A structured set of questions | Quantitative studies |
| **Interview Schedule** | Face-to-face or telephonic questioning | Detailed qualitative insights |
| **Focus Group Discussion (FGD)** | 6–12 participants + moderator | Concept testing, sensory reactions |
| **Observation** | Watching consumer behaviour in stores/aisles | Buying behaviour, shelf impact |
| **In-Home Test** | Product used at home for a few days | Real-life usage feedback |
| **Central Location Test (CLT)** | Product tested at a controlled venue | Sensory evaluation, packaging |
| **Hall Test / Mall Intercept** | Interviewing shoppers in a mall | Quick preference polling |
| **Sensory Panel** | Trained tasters evaluating product | Taste, texture, aroma scoring |

---

### Stage 4 — Data Analysis and Interpretation

**WHY is this stage needed?**
Raw data is meaningless unless it is converted into actionable insights.

**HOW is it done?**
- **Tabulation** of data into frequency tables, cross-tabulations.
- **Statistical Tools Used:**
  - **Mean, Median, Mode** for central tendency.
  - **Standard Deviation, Variance** for spread.
  - **Chi-Square Test** for association between two categorical variables.
  - **ANOVA** for comparing means of more than two groups.
  - **Regression Analysis** for predicting one variable from another.
  - **Conjoint Analysis** for measuring trade-offs consumers make.
  - **Factor Analysis** for identifying key drivers of preference.
  - **Cluster Analysis** for segmenting the market.
- **Software Tools:** SPSS, R, Excel, XLSTAT, NVivo (for qualitative data).

---

### Stage 5 — Reporting and Recommendations

**WHY is this stage needed?**
Top management needs a concise, visual, decision-ready report — not a 200-page raw data dump.

**HOW is it done?**
- Prepare a **Market Research Report** with:
  - Executive Summary
  - Research Objectives
  - Methodology
  - Key Findings (with charts/graphs)
  - Conclusions
  - Recommendations
  - Limitations
- Present to the **New Product Development (NPD) Committee** for **Go / No-Go** decision.

---

### Stage 6 — Follow-Up and Decision Implementation

**WHY is this stage needed?**
The market keeps changing; the survey must validate its predictions.

**HOW is it done?**
- Conduct **Test Marketing** (limited launch in a small geography).
- Track **actual sales vs. forecasted sales**.
- Modify product/price/packaging based on real-world feedback.
- Decide on **National Roll-Out** or **Product Withdrawal**.

---

## 2.2 Factors for Success — The Extended Theoretical List

For a food product to be successful, the market survey must confirm **all of the following factors**:

### A. Consumer-Centric Factors
- **Taste & Flavour Acceptance** → Hedonic rating must be ≥ 7 on a 9-point scale.
- **Texture & Mouthfeel** → Critical for bakery, dairy, snack products.
- **Visual Appeal** → Colour, shape, surface texture.
- **Convenience** → Ready-to-eat, microwaveable, single-serve.
- **Health Perception** → Low sugar, low fat, high protein, clean-label, organic.
- **Cultural Fit** → E.g., a samosa concept may not work in Western markets.
- **Price Sensitivity** → Elasticity of demand.

### B. Market & Competition Factors
- **Market Size & Growth Rate (CAGR).**
- **Competitor Analysis** → Number of brands, market share, pricing tiers.
- **White Spaces** → Unmet consumer needs.
- **Trend Alignment** → Vegan, gluten-free, plant-based, immunity-boosting.

### C. Product-Related Factors
- **Shelf Life Stability.**
- **Packaging Innovation.**
- **Brand Recall & Logo Design.**
- **Product Differentiation / USP.**

### D. Technical & Operational Factors
- **Process Feasibility** on existing equipment.
- **Raw Material Availability** (local vs. imported).
- **Scalability** from lab to industrial scale.

### E. Regulatory & Safety Factors
- **FSSAI Compliance.**
- **Labelling Requirements** (nutritional info, allergens, veg/non-veg mark).
- **Permissible Additives** (within prescribed limits).
- **Packaging Migration** safety.

### F. Financial Factors
- **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS).**
- **Gross Margin %** → typically 30–50% for FMCG food.
- **Break-Even Volume.**
- **Return on Investment (ROI).**

### G. Distribution & Promotional Factors
- **Channel Selection** (Modern Trade, General Trade, E-commerce, HoReCa).
- **Cold Chain Availability** for perishables.
- **Trade Margins** to retailers and distributors.
- **Promotional Budget** (ATL vs. BTL).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **mathematically verifiable formulas** a KTU student must know for this topic. They are commonly tested in numerical or short-answer questions.

### 2.3.1 Sample Size Determination

For an **infinite population** (when the population size is unknown or very large):

$$
n \;=\; \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)}{e^{2}}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = Required sample size
- $Z$ = Z-score (1.96 for 95% confidence, 2.576 for 99% confidence)
- $p$ = Estimated population proportion (use $0.5$ when unknown — gives max sample size)
- $e$ = Margin of error (acceptable error, e.g., 0.05 for ±5%)

**Adjusted sample size for a finite population:**

$$
n_{adj} \;=\; \dfrac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n - 1}{N}}
$$

Where $N$ = total known population size.

### 2.3.2 Confidence Interval for a Population Mean

$$
CI \;=\; \bar{x} \pm Z \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

Where:
- $\bar{x}$ = Sample mean
- $\sigma$ = Population standard deviation
- $n$ = Sample size

### 2.3.3 Market Share Formula

$$
\text{Market Share (\%)} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Sales of Company Product}}{\text{Total Sales of the Market}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.4 Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)

$$
CAGR \;=\; \left( \dfrac{V_{final}}{V_{initial}} \right)^{\frac{1}{t}} - 1
$$

Where $t$ = number of years.

### 2.3.5 Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)

$$
PED \;=\; \dfrac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}
$$

Where $\Delta Q_d$ = change in quantity demanded, $\Delta P$ = change in price.

**Interpretation:**
- $\vert PED \vert > 1$ → Elastic demand (price-sensitive)
- $\vert PED \vert < 1$ → Inelastic demand (price-insensitive)

### 2.3.6 Break-Even Point (in Units)

$$
BEP_{(units)} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Selling Price per Unit} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}
$$

### 2.3.7 Likert Scale Average Score (used in consumer surveys)

$$
\text{Average Score} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum (f_i \cdot s_i)}{N}
$$

Where $f_i$ = frequency of response, $s_i$ = score value, $N$ = total respondents.

---

### 2.3.8 Complete KTU Formula Table (Master Reference)
