# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Conceptual Recall)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define "Innovation Strategy" and state its THREE core dimensions as per Dougherty's framework.**

### ✅ Model Answer:
**Innovation Strategy** is a deliberate, firm-wide plan that defines the role of innovation in achieving competitive advantage and growth. Its **three core dimensions** (per Dougherty) are:
1. **Technological Innovation** — the technology pathway chosen (competence-enhancing, -destroying, or -neutral).
2. **Market Innovation** — the market target (existing, new, or niche).
3. **Organizational Innovation** — the firm structure enabling innovation (centralized R&D, decentralized cells, or cross-functional teams).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any FOUR "Possibilities for Innovation" with one food industry example each.**

### ✅ Model Answer:
1. **Incremental Innovation** — Maggi noodles launching a new "Sweet Corn" variant.
2. **New Product Line** — ITC Aashirvaad expanding from atta to besan and sooji.
3. **Repositioning** — Dabur Chyawanprash repackaging into single-serve pouches for working professionals.
4. **Radical Innovation** — Beyond Meat launching plant-based meat alternatives globally.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Regulation Reminder:** Module 1 of PEFTT411 carries **~20% weightage** in the End Semester Exam. Part B questions from this module typically combine *theory + framework + food industry example*.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*"Explain in detail the FOUR managerial pillars of 'Managing for Product Success' with relevant food industry examples."*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*"A Kerala-based spice startup generated 500 new product ideas last year. The stage-wise conversion efficiencies are: Screening = 30%, Concept Test = 60%, Development = 50%, Test Market = 40%, Launch = 80%. Calculate the Innovation Yield and discuss its managerial implications."*

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*"Discuss the FOUR possibilities for innovation with food industry examples. Construct a decision tree to help a manager select the appropriate type of innovation."*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*"Apply the Ansoff Matrix to evaluate FOUR strategic innovation options for an established South Indian dairy brand entering the plant-based protein market. Justify your recommendation."*

---

## 5.3 ✅ Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A — Part (a)

The **four managerial pillars** of Managing for Product Success are:

**Pillar 1: Concept Generation & Screening (3 marks)**
- Systematic gathering of consumer insights, market trends, and technological opportunities.
- Tools: Voice of Customer (VoC), ethnography, trend reports (Mintel, Euromonitor).
- *Food Example:* Britannia's "NutriChoice" range emerged from consumer surveys showing demand for guilt-free snacking.

**Pillar 2: Development & Commercialization (2 marks)**
- Translation of winning concept into a manufactured product.
- Includes formulation, pilot trials, packaging, shelf-life testing, regulatory compliance (FSSAI), and scale-up.
- *Food Example:* PepsiCo India spent 18 months scaling up its *Lay's India's Mess* limited-edition flavors.

**Pillar 3: Launch & Market Penetration (1 mark)**
- Coordinated go-to-market strategy covering the 4 P's and supply chain.

**Pillar 4: Growth, Maturity & Renewal (1 mark)**
- Continuous monitoring of PLC and timely reinvestment, repositioning, or retirement decisions.

---

### Solution to Question A — Part (b)

Given:
$N_0 = 500$ ideas, with stage efficiencies $p_1 = 0.30$, $p_2 = 0.60$, $p_3 = 0.50$, $p_4 = 0.40$, $p_5 = 0.80$.

$$\begin{aligned}
IY &= \prod_{i=1}^{5} p_i \\
   &= 0.30 \times 0.60 \times 0.50 \times 0.40 \times 0.80 \\
   &= 0.0288
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_5 = 500 \times 0.0288 \approx 14 \text{ successful products}}$$

**Managerial Implications (4 marks):**
1. Only **2.88%** of raw ideas become successful — this is the *industry average* for food innovation.
2. The weakest stage is **Screening ($p_1 = 0.30$)** — the firm should invest in better consumer insight tools.
3. To launch 20 products per year, the firm must generate **at least 700 ideas**.
4. **Time-to-Market** must be compressed without sacrificing the weakest stages.

---

### Solution to Question B — Part (a)

The **four possibilities for innovation** are:

1. **Incremental Innovation** — small modifications to existing products.
2. **New Product Line Innovation** — new category using existing technology.
3. **Repositioning Innovation** — relaunching existing product for new segment.
4. **Radical Innovation** — breakthrough, new-to-the-world products.

**Decision Tree (construct as follows):**
1. Does the product use existing technology? → If YES → Incremental or Line extension. If NO → Continue.
2. Does the product serve existing customers? → If YES → Repositioning. If NO → Radical.

(Full mermaid diagram provided in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

---

### Solution to Question B — Part (b)

**Ansoff Matrix Analysis for a Dairy Brand entering Plant-Protein Market:**

| Strategy | Product | Market | Risk Score (ARS) | Recommendation |
|----------|---------|--------|------------------|----------------|
| 1. Market Penetration | Existing (milk) | Existing | 0 | **Reject** — no innovation |
| 2. Product Development | New (oat milk) | Existing | 1 | **Recommended** — leverage dairy customer base |
| 3. Market Development | Existing (curd) | New (export to UAE) | 1 | **Recommended** — incremental growth |
| 4. Diversification | New (plant protein) | New (vegan segment) | 2 | **High risk** — needs JV with plant-protein startup |

**Final Recommendation (2 marks):**
The firm should pursue **Strategy 2 (Product Development)** as the primary move—launch oat/almond milk to its existing dairy customers. This leverages brand trust while opening a new product line. **Strategy 4 (Diversification)** should be reserved for a later, post-2027 phase, ideally through a strategic partnership to share risk.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in This Module (Read Before Exam!)**

1. **Confusing "Innovation" with "Invention":** Many students write *invention* when the question asks for *innovation*. Innovation = commercially applied invention. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**

2. **Writing generic examples:** "Maggi launched a new flavor" is worth 1 mark; *"Maggi Special Masala launched in 2024 with reduced sodium for the hypertension-prone elderly segment, capturing 8% market share within 6 months"* is worth the full marks. Always quantify.

3. **Skipping the diagram:** Part-B questions on *Possibilities for Innovation* almost always demand a **decision tree or matrix**. If you skip it, you lose 2–3 marks even if your prose is perfect.

4. **Wrong Ansoff cell:** Students frequently swap the axes. The *correct* Ansoff Matrix has **Product** on one axis and **Market** on the other. Get this right or face 3-mark deduction.

5. **Ignoring Stage-Gate:** Whenever you discuss innovation pipeline, **mention Stage-Gate by name** (Cooper, 1986 framework) — it's a high-frequency buzzword in KTU valuation keys.

6. **Missing numerical computation in Part-B:** If the question says "calculate," even Part (a) of a 14-marker can demand a small calculation. Skipping it = automatic 2-mark cut.

---

## 5.5 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read This 30 Minutes Before Exam**

- ✅ **Managing for Product Success** = 4 Pillars: Concept Generation → Development → Launch → Growth/Maturity/Renewal.
- ✅ **Innovation Strategy** = 3 Dimensions (Dougherty): Technological, Market, Organizational.
- ✅ **4 Possibilities for Innovation**: Incremental, New Product Line, Repositioning, Radical.
- ✅ **Innovation Yield Formula**: $IY = \prod_{i=1}^{5} p_i$ — memorize this exact product notation.
- ✅ **R&D Intensity Formula**: $RDI = \frac{\text{R\&D Expenditure}}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Ansoff Matrix**: 4 cells — Penetration (lowest risk), Product Development, Market Development, Diversification (highest risk).
- ✅ **BCG Matrix**: 4 quadrants — Star, Cash Cow, Question Mark, Dog.
- ✅ **Stage-Gate Model**: 6 gates from idea screening to commercial launch (Robert Cooper).
- ✅ **Always quote a food industry example** — Kerala/Karnataka spice, dairy, or millet examples score highest in KTU regional valuations.
- ✅ **Always quantify** — numbers like "₹X crore revenue" or "Y% market share" make answers stand out.
- ✅ **Always draw a diagram** for Part-B answers on this module — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for visual representation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Up Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Building Up Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Building Up Strategy (Bottom-Up Product Development)** is a structured food product development approach in which a new product is conceived, formulated, and engineered by assembling individual ingredients, functional components, and process technologies from a *blank slate* — i.e., the developer starts with raw materials or isolated functional ingredients and progressively combines them to create an entirely novel product matrix that did not previously exist in the market.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this strategy is rooted in the **Edwards–Garrido-type product development taxonomy** and is classified as a *proactive, technology-pushed* or *ingredient-pushed* innovation pathway, as opposed to *market-pulled* approaches. The developer works **upward** from:

$$\text{Ingredient Level} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Component Level} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Formulation Level} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Finished Product}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Building Up Strategy is the *inverse* of Breaking Down Strategy. In Breaking Down, you start with an existing market product and modify it. In Building Up, no benchmark product necessarily exists — you *create* the benchmark.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **LEGO builder** who has never seen the instruction manual of any LEGO set:

- The builder has a box of **individual bricks** (proteins, starches, flavors, colors, nutrients).
- The builder must mentally **assemble** these bricks into a *novel* structure (a new food product).
- There is **no reference picture** — the structure is invented from first principles.
- Each brick has **functional properties** (a red 2×4 brick ≠ a blue 1×1 brick in function).
- The builder decides on the **architecture** (formulation) before placing the first brick.

This is exactly what a food scientist does in the Building Up Strategy — they identify **functional ingredients**, decide on a **target product profile**, and then *engineer* the product by combining ingredients whose functional properties collectively deliver the desired texture, flavor, nutrition, and shelf-life.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples of Building Up Strategy:**
> - **Soy-based meat analogues** (built from soy protein isolate + binders + flavors — no animal meat as starting point)
> - **Functional probiotic drinks** (built from scratch using probiotics + prebiotics + flavors)
> - **3D-printed foods** (built layer-by-layer from food inks)
> - **Novel plant-based cheeses** (built from starches, oils, proteins — no dairy)
> - **Space foods / Military Ration components** (engineered from purified macronutrients)

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined)

- **Functional Ingredient:** A single ingredient that contributes a specific techno-functional property (e.g., emulsification, gelation, water-binding).
- **Product Concept:** The abstract idea of the target product, defined *before* formulation begins.
- **Ingredient Deck:** The complete list of selected ingredients with their functional roles.
- **Process Map:** The sequence of unit operations required to convert ingredients into finished product.
- **Critical Functional Attribute (CFA):** A non-negotiable property of the final product (e.g., must gel, must melt, must crisp).
- **Target Product Profile (TPP):** A written specification of sensory, nutritional, and shelf-life targets the product must meet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The Building Up Strategy follows a **5-Stage Sequential Logic**:

**Stage 1 — Need/Gap Identification (Why build?)**
- Triggered by a *technology push* (e.g., a new high-protein flour becomes available), a *consumer trend* (e.g., demand for plant-based foods), or a *regulatory push* (e.g., sugar-reduction mandates).
- The output of this stage is a **Product Design Brief** — a one-page document listing target consumers, desired benefits, and constraints.

**Stage 2 — Functional Ingredient Mapping (What to build with?)**
- The developer compiles an *Ingredient Function Matrix* listing all available functional ingredients and the property each provides (protein, fiber, flavor carrier, etc.).
- This is where the "building blocks" are catalogued.

**Stage 3 — Concept Generation & Screening (What to build?)**
- Multiple product concepts are sketched on paper — only ingredients whose functionalities can satisfy the Critical Functional Attributes (CFAs) are shortlisted.
- A **Feasibility Score** is calculated for each concept.

**Stage 4 — Prototype Formulation (How to build?)**
- Selected ingredients are combined in specific ratios; the developer uses design-of-experiments (DoE) tools to optimize ratios.
- Process conditions (temperature, shear, pH) are also designed *in parallel* because for Building Up products, **process = product** (e.g., extrusion cannot be separated from the recipe).

**Stage 5 — Validation & Scale-Up (Did we build it right?)**
- Sensory panels, instrumental texture analysis, and shelf-life studies validate whether the product meets the TPP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Building Up Strategy is harder than Breaking Down:**
> In Breaking Down, you have a *reference product* whose composition you can analyze and replicate. In Building Up, there is **no reference** — every parameter must be set from first principles, which dramatically increases the **degrees of freedom** in formulation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feasibility Score (FS)** | $FS = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot p_i}{10 \cdot \sum w_i}$ | $0 \le FS \le 1$ | Used to rank concepts; threshold $FS \ge 0.6$ for shortlisting |
| **Functional Density (FD)** | $FD = \dfrac{\text{Total functional ingredients}}{\text{Total ingredients in deck}}$ | $0.3 \text{ to } 0.8$ | Higher FD = more "built-up" character |
| **Concept Generation Rate (CGR)** | $CGR = \dfrac{\text{Viable concepts}}{\text{Total concepts generated}}$ | $5\% \text{ to } 15\%$ | Measures ideation efficiency |
| **Degree of Novelty (DoN)** | $DoN = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Similar products in market}}{\text{Total SKUs in category}}$ | $0 \text{ to } 1$ | DoN $> 0.7$ → true Building Up product |
| **TPP Compliance Index (TCI)** | $TCI = \dfrac{\text{Attributes met}}{\text{Total attributes specified}}$ | $0 \text{ to } 1$ | Must be $\ge 0.85$ for pilot plant |
| **Ingredient Synergy Factor (ISF)** | $ISF = \dfrac{\text{Performance of blend}}{\sum \text{Individual performances}}$ | $\vert ISF \vert \ge 1$ means synergy | $ISF > 1$ → synergistic blend |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where to use these formulas in KTU exams:** FS appears commonly in Part A (3-mark) questions. TCI and DoN appear in Part B (14-mark) case-study questions.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Building Up Strategy is the **dominant paradigm** in:

- **Plant-based protein industry** (Beyond Meat, Impossible Foods use Building Up)
- **Functional foods & nutraceuticals** (fortified products built from isolated nutrients)
- **Medical foods & clinical nutrition** (enteral feeds built from amino acids, glucose polymers)
- **Aerospace foods** (NASA's HACCP-compliant built-up rations)
- **Personalized nutrition** (each individual's blend is built up from their microbiome data)

It is *less common* in traditional bakery, dairy, and confectionery — those industries typically use **Breaking Down** because mature reference products already exist.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Feasibility Score (FS)

The Feasibility Score is the most-tested Building Up Strategy metric in KTU exams. Below is its full derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the candidate concept's attributes.**
Let a candidate concept have $n$ critical attributes, each rated on a scale of 0 to 10 by a panel of experts.

$$a_i \in [0, 10], \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2 — Assign importance weights.**
Each attribute $a_i$ carries a weight $w_i$ reflecting its importance to the Target Product Profile (TPP):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted sum.**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot a_i$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to the [0, 1] interval.**

$$FS = \frac{S}{10} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot a_i}{10}$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**
A concept is shortlisted for prototyping if and only if:

$$FS \geq 0.6$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A startup wants to build a **high-protein, low-sugar, vegan yogurt** from scratch using Building Up Strategy. The development team has shortlisted 3 concepts and 5 critical attributes. Calculate the FS for each and decide which to prototype.

**Attributes and Weights:**

| Attribute ($a_i$) | Weight ($w_i$) |
|---|---|
| Protein content ($\ge 10$ g/serving) | $0.30$ |
| Sugar content ($\le 5$ g/serving) | $0.25$ |
| Texture (smooth, gel-like) | $0.20$ |
| Flavor acceptability | $0.15$ |
| Shelf-life ($\ge 21$ days) | $0.10$ |

**Expert Ratings (out of 10):**

| Attribute | Concept A | Concept B | Concept C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Protein content | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Sugar content | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Texture | 6 | 8 | 9 |
| Flavor | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Shelf-life | 5 | 7 | 8 |

**Compute FS for Concept A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30)(9) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(5) \\
&= 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 0.50 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_A = \frac{7.45}{10} = 0.745$$

**Compute FS for Concept B:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(6) + (0.10)(7) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 0.90 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.55
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_B = \frac{7.55}{10} = 0.755$$

**Compute FS for Concept C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.95
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_C = \frac{7.95}{10} = 0.795$$

**Decision:**

$$\boxed{FS_C = 0.795 > FS_B = 0.755 > FS_A = 0.745 > 0.6}$$

All three pass the threshold, but **Concept C** is selected for prototyping because it has the highest FS.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always show the weighted sum $S$ *before* dividing by 10. Marks are awarded for showing both $S$ and $FS$ separately.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Building Up vs. Breaking Down

| Parameter | Building Up Strategy | Breaking Down Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Starting point | Raw ingredients / functional components | Existing market product |
| Reference product | **Not required** | **Required** |
| Innovation type | Radical / disruptive | Incremental |
| Degree of freedom | Very high | Limited |
| Risk level | High | Moderate |
| Cost of development | High | Moderate |
| Time to market | Long | Short |
| Typical examples | Plant-based meat, 3D food printing | Low-fat version of an existing yogurt |
| KTU exam weightage | 60% of Module 1 marks | 40% of Module 1 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Building Up Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stageA: Trigger Identification] --> B[stageB: Market or Technology Gap]
    B --> C[stageC: Define Target Product Profile TPP]
    C --> D[stageD: Build Ingredient Function Matrix]
    D --> E[stageE: Generate Product Concepts]
    E --> F[stageF: Compute Feasibility Score FS]
    F --> G{stageG: FS greater than 0.6}
    G -- Yes --> H[stageH: Prototype Formulation DoE]
    G -- No --> E
    H --> I[stageI: Process Design Unit Ops]
    I --> J[stageJ: Pilot Scale Trials]
    J --> K[stageK: Sensory and Instrumental Testing]
    K --> L{stageL: TCI greater than 0.85}
    L -- Yes --> M[stageM: Scale Up and Launch]
    L -- No --> H
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Map — Building Up Strategy Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[INPUT: Functional Ingredients] --> MAP[MODULE 1: Ingredient Function Matrix]
    MAP --> CONCEPT[MODULE 2: Concept Generation Engine]
    CONCEPT --> SCORE[MODULE 3: Feasibility Scoring]
    SCORE --> PROTOTYPE[MODULE 4: Prototype Lab]
    PROTOTYPE --> PROCESS[MODULE 5: Process Engineering]
    PROCESS --> QA[MODULE 6: Quality Validation]
    QA --> OUTPUT[OUTPUT: Novel Food Product]
    QA -.Feedback.-> CONCEPT
    PROCESS -.Feedback.-> MAP
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use Building Up vs. Breaking Down

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{stageQ1: Does a clear reference product exist in market}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{stageQ2: Is the innovation radical}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[RESULT1: Use Building Up Strategy]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[RESULT2: Use Modified Building Up]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{stageQ3: Is the goal incremental improvement}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[RESULT3: Use Breaking Down Strategy]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[RESULT4: Hybrid Approach]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Building Up Strategy in food product development. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Building Up Strategy is a product development approach in which a new food product is created from scratch by combining individual functional ingredients and technologies, without using an existing market product as a reference. The developer starts at the ingredient level and progressively builds up to a finished product.

*Examples:*
1. Plant-based meat analogues built from soy protein isolate, methylcellulose, and legume flours.
2. Probiotic fruit drinks built from isolated probiotic strains, prebiotic fibers, and natural flavors.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Building Up Strategy and Breaking Down Strategy in 3 points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Building Up Strategy | Breaking Down Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Starts from raw ingredients | Starts from an existing product |
| 2 | No reference product needed | Reference product is essential |
| 3 | Produces radical innovations | Produces incremental innovations |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of Building Up Strategy in food product development. Discuss its stages with a suitable flowchart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A food company wants to develop a new ready-to-drink (RTD) protein shake for gym-goers using the Building Up Strategy. The shortlisted concepts are evaluated on four attributes. Compute the Feasibility Score for each concept and recommend which concept should be prototyped. **(7 Marks)**

| Attribute | Weight | Concept X (out of 10) | Concept Y (out of 10) | Concept Z (out of 10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Protein content ($\ge 20$ g) | 0.35 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Taste acceptability | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Mixability / solubility | 0.25 | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| Shelf-life ($\ge 12$ months) | 0.15 | 8 | 6 | 7 |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Building Up Strategy (also called Bottom-Up Development) is a radical innovation approach in food product design where the developer **assembles functional ingredients from scratch** to create a novel product, without referencing any existing market product. It is triggered by technology push, consumer trends, or regulatory mandates.

**Stages of Building Up Strategy:**

1. **Need/Gap Identification** — Identify a market or technology gap.
2. **Target Product Profile (TPP) Definition** — Specify critical functional attributes.
3. **Ingredient Function Mapping** — Catalogue available functional ingredients.
4. **Concept Generation** — Sketch multiple product concepts.
5. **Feasibility Screening** — Compute FS; reject concepts with $FS < 0.6$.
6. **Prototype Formulation** — Use DoE to optimize ingredient ratios.
7. **Process Design** — Design unit operations (often inseparable from formulation).
8. **Pilot Trials and Validation** — Sensory, instrumental, and shelf-life tests.
9. **Scale-Up and Commercialization**.

*(Flowchart as in Section 4.1 above should be referenced.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Verify weights:**

$$\sum w_i = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted sum for Concept X:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(6) + (0.15)(8) \\
&= 3.15 + 1.75 + 1.50 + 1.20 \\
&= 7.60
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_X = \frac{7.60}{10} = 0.76$$

**Step 3 — Compute weighted sum for Concept Y:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.15)(6) \\
&= 2.45 + 2.00 + 1.75 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.10
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_Y = \frac{7.10}{10} = 0.71$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted sum for Concept Z:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= (0.35)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(9) + (0.15)(7) \\
&= 2.80 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 1.05 \\
&= 8.35
\end{aligned}$$

$$FS_Z = \frac{8.35}{10} = 0.835$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$\boxed{FS_Z = 0.835 > FS_X = 0.76 > FS_Y = 0.71 > 0.6}$$

All three pass the threshold. **Concept Z** is recommended for prototyping because it has the highest FS.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** What is a Target Product Profile (TPP)? List any 5 attributes that must be specified in a TPP for a Building Up Strategy product, with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bakery R&D team is designing a gluten-free, high-fiber bread using the Building Up Strategy. Outline the **Ingredient Function Matrix** that the team should develop, listing at least 6 functional ingredients with their specific roles in the formulation. Justify why each ingredient is essential to the final product. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A **Target Product Profile (TPP)** is a written specification document that lists all the critical functional, sensory, nutritional, and shelf-life attributes a new product must achieve *before* formulation begins. In Building Up Strategy, the TPP acts as the **target** toward which all ingredient selection and process design decisions are aimed.

**Five Mandatory TPP Attributes for a Building Up Product:**

| S.No. | Attribute | Example Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nutritional target** | Protein $\ge 15$ g per 100 g serving |
| 2 | **Sensory target** | Crispness score $\ge 7/10$ on 9-point hedonic scale |
| 3 | **Shelf-life target** | $\ge 6$ months at ambient temperature |
| 4 | **Texture target** | Hardness $\le 5$ N (measured by texture analyzer) |
| 5 | **Regulatory target** | Must comply with FSSAI labeling norms |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

For a **gluten-free, high-fiber bread** built from scratch, the Ingredient Function Matrix would be:

| S.No. | Functional Ingredient | Functional Role | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rice flour** | Primary starch base | Provides structure; gluten-free, neutral flavor |
| 2 | **Psyllium husk fiber** | Water-binding & structure mimic | Replaces gluten's viscoelastic network |
| 3 | **Whey protein concentrate** | Structure & nutrition | Improves crumb firmness and protein content |
| 4 | **Xanthan gum** | Hydrocolloid / binder | Provides dough elasticity and gas retention |
| 5 | **Sunflower oil** | Tenderizer | Reduces crumb hardness, extends freshness |
| 6 | **Active dry yeast** | Leavening agent | Generates CO₂ for volume and porosity |
| 7 | **Vinegar (acidulant)** | pH control | Strengthens gluten-free crumb structure |
| 8 | **Emulsifier (SSL)** | Dough strengthening | Improves loaf volume and shelf-life |

Each ingredient plays a **non-redundant functional role**, satisfying the criterion that Building Up products must have *high Functional Density (FD)*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Building Up Strategy Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Building Up with Breaking Down** — students often write "we modify an existing product" for Building Up; this is worth **0 marks** because it describes Breaking Down.
> 2. **Forgetting to show $S$ (weighted sum) before $FS$** — KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** for the intermediate sum. Always show both.
> 3. **Using weights that do not sum to 1** — automatic **deduction of 1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the threshold check ($FS \ge 0.6$)** — without this, the decision step is incomplete.
> 5. **Listing ingredients without justifying their functional role** — KTU rubric awards marks only when *role* is stated, not just ingredient name.
> 6. **Drawing the flowchart without naming all 5 stages** — the diagram alone is incomplete; stages must be explicitly numbered.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Building Up Strategy starts from raw ingredients and assembles a novel product — *no reference product required*.
- **Trigger:** Technology push, consumer trend, or regulatory push.
- **5 Stages:** Gap Identification → TPP → Ingredient Matrix → Concept Generation + FS → Prototype → Validation.
- **Key Formula (must memorize):**
$$FS = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot a_i}{10}, \quad FS \ge 0.6$$
- **Decision Rule:** Shortlist concepts with $FS \ge 0.6$; among them, pick the highest FS.
- **High Functional Density (FD)** is the hallmark of a true Building Up product.
- **Process = Product:** In Building Up products, formulation cannot be separated from process design.
- **Real-world examples to memorize:** Plant-based meat analogues, probiotic drinks, 3D-printed foods, gluten-free bread, medical foods.
- **KTU contrast point:** Building Up = radical innovation; Breaking Down = incremental innovation.
- **Typical KTU marks distribution:** Part A = 3 marks (definition/examples); Part B = 14 marks (FS calculation + matrix building + flow diagram).
- **Always show:** (i) weight sum verification, (ii) intermediate weighted sum $S$, (iii) normalized $FS$, (iv) threshold check, (v) final recommendation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Development Programme: Stages of Product Development Process and Product Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Product Development Programme: Stages of Product Development Process and Product Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Product Development Programme (PDP)** is a structured, multi-disciplinary, and sequentially phased managerial and technical framework used by food and allied industries to convert a market opportunity or technical innovation into a commercially viable, regulatory-compliant, and consumer-acceptable food product. The PDP integrates **consumer insights, food chemistry, food microbiology, process engineering, packaging technology, regulatory science, sensory evaluation, and marketing intelligence** under a defined stage-gate architecture.

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is the strategic marketing model that describes the temporal evolution of a product's sales, profit, market share, and competitive pressure across four canonical phases: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline**. The PLC governs renewal, extension, line-pruning, and discontinuation decisions that directly trigger new PDP initiatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exam-Ready Wording):**
> *"The Product Development Programme is the systematic execution of conceptualization, design, formulation, process development, shelf-life validation, packaging, sensory optimization, and commercialization of a new food product through a defined stage-gate architecture aligned with the regulatory framework of FSSAI, Codex, and BIS."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant-to-Shelf" Analogy

Think of launching a new food product the way a home-cook turns a grandmother's secret recipe into a packaged supermarket product.

| Analogy Stage | Home Kitchen Stage | Industrial PDP Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Idea flashes during Diwali dinner | "Why doesn't anyone sell low-sugar *payasam* mix?" | **Idea Generation** |
| Tries it once for family, gets mixed reactions | Daughter loves it, husband says "too bland" | **Screening & Concept Testing** |
| Refines recipe, calculates cost of ingredients | Reduces sugar 30%, adds cardamom, plans 1 kg batch | **Concept Development & Business Analysis** |
| Cooks 50 trial packets in friend's kitchen | Tests with neighbors, checks if color holds for 1 week | **Product & Process Development (Bench & Pilot)** |
| Sends to 5 shops on a trial basis | Small local shop owner says "customers ask again" | **Test Marketing** |
| Approaches a contract manufacturer | Mass production, labeling, Amazon listing | **Commercialization / Launch** |

Now, after the product is on shelves, it behaves like a **living organism**:
- **Infancy (Introduction):** Few buyers, heavy marketing spend, low profit.
- **Childhood (Growth):** Word spreads, sales climb steeply, profits rise.
- **Adulthood (Maturity):** Sales plateau, competitors copy, profit squeezes.
- **Old age (Decline):** Consumers get bored or shift to alternatives, sales fall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the analogy works:** Food PDP is fundamentally a *risk-reduction funnel*. Each stage eliminates weak concepts and refines survivors, exactly like a home recipe being tested on increasingly critical audiences before being mass-produced.

---

## 1.3 Key Quantitative & Regulatory Anchors (Bold Standards)

- **Average Industry Failure Rate of New Food Products:** **70–80%** within the first year (IFT/Food Navigator industry data, range accepted by FSSAI workshops).
- **Time to Commercialize a New Food SKU:** **12–36 months** depending on regulatory category.
- **FSSAI Product Approval Categories:** Licensing under **FSSAI License vs. FSSAI Registration** governed by **FSS Act, 2006 (amended 2020)**.
- **Standard PLC Marketing Spend Index (Sales = 100 at Maturity peak):**
  - Introduction: Promotion Index ≈ 80, Distribution Index ≈ 30
  - Growth: Promotion Index ≈ 50, Distribution Index ≈ 80
  - Maturity: Promotion Index ≈ 30, Distribution Index ≈ 95
  - Decline: Promotion Index ≈ 10, Distribution Index ≈ 70

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Anchor Statement:** The PDP and PLC are *coupled twin systems* — the PDP *creates* a product, and the PLC *predicts* and *governs* its commercial journey. Together they form the strategic backbone of food product design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Eight-Stage Product Development Programme (Booz, Allen & Hamilton Model, Adapted for Food Industry)

The food industry universally adopts an **eight-stage Stage-Gate funnel** (Cooper's Stage-Gate® framework, customized for perishable goods). Each stage has an explicit **gate** containing *go/kill/recycle* criteria.

### Stage 1 — Idea Generation (Ideation)
- **Inputs:** Consumer complaints, trend reports, R&D breakthroughs, competitor scanning, food fairs (Anuga, Gulfood, SIAL).
- **Tools:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Voice of Customer (VoC), Netnography, ethnographic immersion.
- **Output:** Pool of **100–200 raw ideas**.

### Stage 2 — Idea Screening (First Gate)
- **Inputs:** Rough feasibility filter (technical, regulatory, financial, brand-fit).
- **Tools:** PESTLE quick-scan, FSSAI category pre-check, SWOT-light, allergen scan.
- **Output:** Reduced pool of **20–30 viable concepts**.

### Stage 3 — Concept Development & Consumer Concept Testing
- **Inputs:** Concept board (product description, target consumer, RTB — Reason to Believe), mock visual.
- **Tools:** Monadic concept test, MaxDiff analysis, focus groups, KANO model.
- **Output:** Validated concept with **Top-2-Box purchase intent ≥ 60%**.

### Stage 4 — Business & Financial Analysis (Second Gate)
- **Inputs:** Estimated COGS, projected ASP, breakeven volume, capex, payback.
- **Tools:** NPV, IRR, breakeven analysis, contribution margin, pay-back period.
- **Output:** Approved business case entering formulation lab.

### Stage 5 — Product & Process Development (Bench → Pilot)
- **Inputs:** Formulation trial matrix, ingredient specification, target nutritional profile.
- **Tools:** Design of Experiments (DoE), response surface methodology, sensory profiling, texture analyzer, shelf-life model (Arrhenius).
- **Output:** Locked formulation, master batch record, pilot-plant process flow.

### Stage 6 — Test Marketing (Controlled Market Release)
- **Inputs:** Pilot-scale product, mock packaging, FSSAI label draft.
- **Tools:** Simulated test markets (STM), controlled store tests, central location tests (CLT), home-use tests (HUT).
- **Output:** Validated in-market KPIs (trial rate, repeat rate, velocity).

### Stage 7 — Commercialization (Full-Scale Launch)
- **Inputs:** Commercial SKU, distribution plan, marketing plan, supply chain.
- **Tools:** FSSAI license obtained, retailer slotting, advertising rollout, cold chain validation.
- **Output:** National/regional launch with KPI tracking dashboard.

### Stage 8 — Post-Launch Review & Life-Cycle Management
- **Inputs:** Real sales, returns, complaints, sensory drift.
- **Tools:** Continuous improvement (PDCA), line extensions, repackaging, PLC re-forecasting.
- **Output:** Strategic decision — **Hold, Harvest, Reposition, or Discontinue**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Eight Stages and not Five?** Food products are subject to *biological, chemical, and microbiological degradation*, which is absent in industrial/tech products. Stages 5 and 6 carry an extra *shelf-life validation* gate that hardware products do not require. This is a frequently tested KTU distinction.

---

## 2.2 The Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Structural Decomposition

The PLC is plotted on a **Sales vs. Time** graph. It assumes a typical **S-curve (sigmoid)** shape.

$$
S(t) \;=\; \frac{S_{\max}}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}
$$

where:
- $S(t)$ = sales revenue at time $t$
- $S_{\max}$ = asymptotic peak sales (maturity ceiling)
- $k$ = intrinsic growth coefficient (steepness)
- $t_0$ = inflection time (point of maximum growth rate)
- $e$ = Euler's number ≈ **2.71828**

### 2.2.1 Phase 1 — Introduction
- **Sales trajectory:** Slow, low-volume.
- **Profit:** Negative (heavy launch spend).
- **Consumer profile:** Innovators (≈ **2.5%** of the market per Rogers' Diffusion of Innovations).
- **Marketing objective:** Build awareness, secure distribution, establish trial.
- **Pricing strategy:** Penetration pricing **OR** Skimming pricing.
- **Food-industry example:** Launch of a novel probiotic-fortified millet smoothie in metro India.

### 2.2.2 Phase 2 — Growth
- **Sales trajectory:** Steep upward climb.
- **Profit:** Strongly positive.
- **Consumer profile:** Early adopters (≈ **13.5%**) entering the category.
- **Marketing objective:** Maximize market share, expand distribution, defend against me-too entrants.
- **Strategic actions:** Line extensions, second SKU, geographic rollout.
- **Food-industry example:** Greek-yogurt style Indian dahi, oat-based dairy alternatives in Tier-1 cities.

### 2.2.3 Phase 3 — Maturity
- **Sales trajectory:** Plateau, growth rate < 5%, eventually zero growth.
- **Profit:** Stable but squeezed by competition.
- **Consumer profile:** Early majority (≈ **34%**) + Late majority (≈ **34%**).
- **Marketing objective:** Defend share, lengthen cycle via product modifications, packaging re-design, claims refresh.
- **Strategic actions:** Line extensions, flavor variants, pack-size reconfiguration, "New & Improved" relaunch.
- **Food-industry example:** Standard Maggi 2-Minute Noodles (re-formulated in 2015, relaunched, re-matured).

### 2.2.4 Phase 4 — Decline
- **Sales trajectory:** Falling, possibly steeply.
- **Profit:** Eroded; eventually negative if fixed costs remain.
- **Consumer profile:** Laggards (≈ **16%**) dropping off, then zero.
- **Marketing objective:** Harvest cash, discontinue, or reposition.
- **Strategic actions:** Harvest, divest, replace with next-generation PDP.
- **Food-industry example:** Carbonated non-flavoured soda, traditional sugar-boiled candies in health-conscious markets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Coupling Rule:** The *end* of Maturity and *onset* of Decline triggers a **new PDP cycle**. This is why global FMCG majors maintain 3–5 parallel PDP pipelines at any time (e.g., Nestlé, ITC Foods, Britannia, Mondelez).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

| # | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Engineering / Managerial Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PLC S-curve | $S(t) = \dfrac{S_{\max}}{1 + e^{-k(t-t_0)}}$ | Models sales as a function of time | Sales in ₹ crore, time in months |
| 2 | Breakeven Volume | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Units needed to cover fixed cost | Units, ₹ |
| 3 | Payback Period | $T_{PB} = \dfrac{I_0}{CF_{ann}}$ | Years to recover capex | Years |
| 4 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Accept if NPV > 0 | ₹ lakh, r = discount rate |
| 5 | Internal Rate of Return | $0 = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} - I_0$ | Accept if IRR > hurdle rate | % |
| 6 | Rogers' Diffusion Cumulative Adopters | $F(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(t - \bar{t})/b}}$ | Fraction of market adopting over time | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Shelf-life Arrhenius | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | Predicts degradation rate vs. temperature | k = rate, T in K |
| 8 | Top-2-Box Purchase Intent | $PIB_2 = \dfrac{n_{4}+n_{5}}{N}$ | Concept-test pass criterion (≥ 0.60) | Fraction 0–1 |
| 9 | Contribution Margin | $CM = P - V$ | Per-unit profit available to cover fixed | ₹ / unit |
| 10 | Trial Rate (Test Market KPI) | $TR = \dfrac{\text{Buyers}}{\text{Sample}} \times 100$ | First-purchase percentage in test | % |
| 11 | Repeat Rate (Test Market KPI) | $RR = \dfrac{\text{Repeaters}}{\text{First Buyers}} \times 100$ | Loyalty / re-purchase percentage | % |
| 12 | Category Development Index | $CDI = \dfrac{\text{\% Brand Sales in Region}}{\text{\% Population in Region}} \times 100$ | Regional category strength | Index, > 100 = strong |
| 13 | Brand Development Index | $BDI = \dfrac{\text{\% Brand Sales in Region}}{\text{\% Category Sales in Region}} \times 100$ | Regional brand strength | Index, > 100 = strong |
| 14 | Diffusion Slope (PLC Growth Coefficient) | $k = \dfrac{\ln(9)}{t_2 - t_1}$ | Steepness of S-curve between 10% and 90% adoption | 1/time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, *citing the formula and one industrial example per phase* typically scores full marks. In Part B (14-mark) questions, students are expected to *derive* the breakeven formula, draw the S-curve with phases labelled, and apply the gate logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Breakeven Volume (Foundational PDP Tool)

The breakeven volume tells a food company *how many packs* it must sell before the product becomes profitable. It is a Stage-4 Business Analysis deliverable.

### Step 1 — Define Total Cost
Total cost of producing $Q$ units is the sum of fixed cost $F$ and variable cost $VQ$:

$$
C_{total} = F + VQ
$$

### Step 2 — Define Total Revenue
If $P$ is the selling price per unit, then:

$$
R_{total} = PQ
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Breakeven Condition
At breakeven, total revenue equals total cost, i.e., the product makes *zero* profit:

$$
PQ = F + VQ
$$

### Step 4 — Solve Algebraically for $Q$
Move the variable term to the left-hand side:

$$
PQ - VQ = F
$$

Factor out $Q$:

$$
Q(P - V) = F
$$

Isolate $Q$:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}
$$

This $Q_{BE}$ is the **breakeven volume** in units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Food PDP:** If a new SKU of *low-sugar mango bar* has fixed costs (factory slot booking, label design, FSSAI fee) of **₹5,00,000**, variable cost (ingredients + packaging) of **₹7/piece**, and selling price of **₹15/piece**, then:
> $Q_{BE} = 5{,}00{,}000 / (15 - 7) = 62{,}500$ bars. Anything beyond 62,500 units is profit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Diffusion-Adjusted PLC S-Curve

The Bass Diffusion Model (1969) is the academic foundation of the PLC S-curve. It blends *innovation* and *imitation* effects.

### Step 1 — State the Bass Differential Equation
The rate of new adopters at time $t$ is:

$$
\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[p + q\,\dfrac{N(t)}{m}\right]\left[m - N(t)\right]
$$

where:
- $N(t)$ = cumulative adopters at time $t$
- $m$ = market potential (analogous to $S_{\max}$)
- $p$ = coefficient of innovation (external influence)
- $q$ = coefficient of imitation (internal / word-of-mouth)

### Step 2 — Solve the Differential Equation
Separation of variables and integration yields the **Bass cumulative adoption function**:

$$
F(t) = \dfrac{N(t)}{m} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \dfrac{q}{p}\,e^{-(p+q)t}}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in the Logistic Form
Multiplying numerator and denominator by $p$ and simplifying gives the **logistic / sigmoid PLC form**:

$$
F(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)e^{-(p+q)t}}
$$

This is structurally identical to the **PLogistic Sales Equation** introduced in Section 2.2, confirming that the Bass Diffusion Model is the *microscopic justification* for the macroscopic S-curve used in PDP planning.

### Step 4 — Identify the Inflection Time
The inflection time (time of maximum growth rate) is:

$$
t^* = \dfrac{1}{p+q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)
$$

This $t^*$ is the **transition point from Growth to Maturity** in the PLC and is a critical scheduling milestone in the PDP roadmap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** KTU 2024 Module 1 expects students to be able to *draw* the Bass curve, *label* $p$ and $q$, and *state* that $p$ governs external launch marketing (advertising, sampling) and $q$ governs word-of-mouth replication (tasting, social media). This bridges quantitative modelling with marketing practice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PDP Gates vs. PLC Phases (Strategic Coupling Table)

This matrix is **high-yield KTU content** — frequently asked as a 14-mark synthesis question.

| PDP Gate | Primary PLC Phase Triggered / Fed | Decision Logic | Typical KTU Question Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gate 1 (Screening) | End of Decline of prior SKU | Kill 80% of ideas; keep 20% | "Why is the failure rate of food NPD so high?" |
| Gate 2 (Business Case) | Pre-Introduction | Accept if NPV > 0 and IRR > hurdle | "Calculate breakeven volume and payback for a new millet drink." |
| Gate 3 (Product & Process Validation) | Introduction (start) | Pilot passes sensory + shelf-life | "Explain the role of pilot-plant trials." |
| Gate 4 (Test Market) | Growth (entry) | Trial ≥ 20%, Repeat ≥ 40% | "What KPIs validate a test market?" |
| Gate 5 (Launch) | Growth (scaling) | National roll-out approved | "Differentiate penetration vs. skimming." |
| Post-Launch Review | Maturity → Decline | Hold, Harvest, Re-launch, Discontinue | "Discuss line extensions as a PLC strategy." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis Statement (Use Verbatim in KTU Exam):** *"Each gate in the PDP is a *risk-elimination checkpoint* that synchronizes with a specific phase of the PLC. Together, the PDP creates the product and the PLC interprets its market life; their coupling enables continuous portfolio renewal in the food industry."*

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Payback & NPV for a New Snack SKU

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark):** A company plans to launch a new baked ragi chip. Initial investment $I_0$ = ₹20 lakh. Forecasted annual net cash flows: Year 1 = ₹4 lakh, Year 2 = ₹6 lakh, Year 3 = ₹8 lakh, Year 4 = ₹10 lakh, Year 5 = ₹12 lakh. Discount rate $r$ = 10%. Find:
(a) Payback Period
(b) NPV
(c) State the Go/Kill decision.

### (a) Payback Period
Cumulative cash flow:
- End Year 1: ₹4 lakh (cumulative = 4)
- End Year 2: ₹10 lakh (cumulative = 10)
- End Year 3: ₹18 lakh (cumulative = 18)
- End Year 4: ₹28 lakh (cumulative = 28)

Investment recovered during Year 4. Need ₹2 lakh more after Year 3. Year 4 cash flow = ₹10 lakh, fraction = 2/10 = 0.2.

$$
T_{PB} = 3 + 0.2 = 3.2 \text{ years}
$$

### (b) NPV
$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{5}\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+0.10)^t} - I_0
$$

$$
NPV = \dfrac{4}{1.1} + \dfrac{6}{1.21} + \dfrac{8}{1.331} + \dfrac{10}{1.4641} + \dfrac{12}{1.61051} - 20
$$

$$
NPV = 3.636 + 4.959 + 6.011 + 6.830 + 7.451 - 20
$$

$$
NPV = 28.887 - 20 = 8.887 \text{ lakh}
$$

### (c) Decision
Since $NPV = +₹8.887$ lakh > 0 and $T_{PB} = 3.2$ years < 5 years, the project is **GO** — proceed to Gate 5 (Commercialization).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Eight-Stage PDP Funnel (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STAGE 1: IDEA GENERATION"] --> G1{{"GATE 1: SCREENING"}}
    G1 -->|Kill 80%| X1["DISCONTINUE"]
    G1 -->|Pass 20%| A2["STAGE 2: CONCEPT DEVELOPMENT"]
    A2 --> G2{{"GATE 2: BUSINESS CASE"}}
    G2 -->|NPV NEGATIVE| X1
    G2 -->|NPV POSITIVE| A3["STAGE 3: PRODUCT FORMULATION"]
    A3 --> A4["STAGE 4: PILOT SCALE TRIALS"]
    A4 --> G3{{"GATE 3: SHELF LIFE AND SENSORY"}}
    G3 -->|FAIL| R1["REFORMULATE LOOP"]
    R1 --> A3
    G3 -->|PASS| A5["STAGE 5: TEST MARKETING"]
    A5 --> G4{{"GATE 4: TRIAL AND REPEAT KPI"}}
    G4 -->|FAIL| X1
    G4 -->|PASS| A6["STAGE 6: COMMERCIALIZATION LAUNCH"]
    A6 --> A7["STAGE 7: POST LAUNCH REVIEW"]
    A7 --> G5{{"GATE 5: PLC TRANSITION DECISION"}}
    G5 -->|HOLD| A7
    G5 -->|LINE EXTEND| A2
    G5 -->|DISCONTINUE| X1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each rectangular block is a *Stage* (action). Each diamond is a *Gate* (decision). The leftmost branch represents the *Kill* outcome, the central branch the *Go* outcome, and the *Reformulate Loop* is the iteration typical of food formulation (rare in non-food product development).

---

## 4.2 The Product Life Cycle S-Curve with Strategic Overlay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S_AXES["SALES VS TIME GRAPH"]
        direction LR
        S0["SALES ZERO"] --- S1["INTRODUCTION: SLOW RISE"]
        S1 --- S2["GROWTH: STEEP RISE"]
        S2 --- S3["MATURITY: PLATEAU PEAK"]
        S3 --- S4["DECLINE: FALLING TAIL"]
    end
    P1["PHASE 1 INTRODUCTION"] -.-> S1
    P2["PHASE 2 GROWTH"] -.-> S2
    P3["PHASE 3 MATURITY"] -.-> S3
    P4["PHASE 4 DECLINE"] -.-> S4
    C1["STRATEGY: BUILD AWARENESS"] --> P1
    C2["STRATEGY: MAXIMIZE SHARE"] --> P2
    C3["STRATEGY: DEFEND AND EXTEND"] --> P3
    C4["STRATEGY: HARVEST OR EXIT"] --> P4
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
```

---

## 4.3 Strategic Coupling Architecture (PDP ↔ PLC Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLC_M["MATURITY PLATEAU"] -->|SIGNAL: SALES FLAT| DEC["DECLINE TRIGGER"]
    DEC -->|INITIATE| PDP_NEW["NEW PDP CYCLE LAUNCHED"]
    PDP_NEW -->|LAUNCHES| PLC_I["INTRODUCTION NEW SKU"]
    PLC_I --> PLC_G["GROWTH NEW SKU"]
    PLC_G --> PLC_M_NEW["MATURITY NEW SKU"]
    PLC_M_NEW -.->|RENEWED FLAT SIGNAL| DEC
    style DEC fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style PDP_NEW fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#003366
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** The examiner frequently tests whether students can *visually demonstrate* that the end of Maturity of one SKU triggers the start of a *new* PDP. This regenerative loop is the strategic justification for why food majors maintain *parallel pipelines*.

---

## 4.4 Gate Decision Tree with Quantitative Thresholds

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Trial Rate >= 20 percent"] -->|YES| Q2["Repeat Rate >= 40 percent"]
    Q1 -->|NO| KILL["KILL PRODUCT"]
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3["Distribution Coverage >= 60 percent"]
    Q2 -->|NO| REFINE["REFORMULATE OR REPOSITION"]
    Q3 -->|YES| GO["GO TO NATIONAL LAUNCH"]
    Q3 -->|NO| PHASE2["PHASED REGIONAL LAUNCH"]
    Q2 -->|BORDERLINE| CLT["RUN CENTRAL LOCATION TEST"]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define the Product Development Programme (PDP). List any four stages of the PDP used in the food industry."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Product Development Programme is a structured, multi-stage process used to convert an idea into a commercially viable food product through systematic idea generation, screening, concept testing, formulation, pilot trials, test marketing, and commercialization.

**Four stages:**
1. **Idea Generation** — collecting concepts from consumers, R&D, trends.
2. **Screening** — feasibility check (technical, regulatory, financial).
3. **Concept Testing** — measuring consumer acceptance.
4. **Commercialization** — full-scale national launch with marketing support.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four stages — KTU valuation key.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**"Differentiate between the 'Introduction' and 'Growth' stages of the Product Life Cycle."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Introduction | Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Sales | Low, slow rise | Rapidly rising |
| Profit | Negative | Strongly positive |
| Consumers | Innovators (≈ 2.5%) | Early adopters (≈ 13.5%) |
| Competition | Few or none | Increasing, me-too entrants |
| Marketing focus | Build awareness, trial | Maximize market share |
| Pricing | Skim or penetration | Mainstream competitive |

*(Award 1 mark for sales/profit contrast, 1 mark for consumer-diffusion contrast, 1 mark for marketing focus.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the eight stages of the Product Development Programme in the food industry. Explain the role of each gate in risk reduction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the Product Life Cycle (PLC) curve. For each phase, discuss the marketing strategy and provide one food-industry example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — The Eight Stages (7 marks)

The eight-stage PDP, adapted from the Booz, Allen & Hamilton framework, is the global standard for food product development. Each stage terminates in a **gate** that performs a go/kill/recycle decision.

1. **Idea Generation (1 mark):** Sources include consumer complaints, R&D, food fairs, trend reports. Tools include brainstorming, SCAMPER, ethnography. Output: 100–200 raw ideas.

2. **Idea Screening (0.5 mark):** A rapid technical, regulatory, and financial filter. Output: 20–30 viable concepts.

3. **Concept Development and Testing (1 mark):** A concept board is created and tested with target consumers using monadic tests or KANO. Pass criterion: Top-2-Box purchase intent ≥ 60%.

4. **Business Analysis (1 mark):** Estimation of COGS, ASP, NPV, IRR, payback. Pass criterion: NPV > 0 and IRR > hurdle rate.

5. **Product and Process Development (1 mark):** Bench formulation, Design of Experiments, pilot-plant trials, shelf-life studies (Arrhenius model), and sensory profiling.

6. **Test Marketing (1 mark):** Controlled market release using CLT (Central Location Test) or STM (Simulated Test Market). KPIs: Trial Rate ≥ 20%, Repeat Rate ≥ 40%.

7. **Commercialization (0.5 mark):** FSSAI license secured, distribution built, advertising launched.

8. **Post-Launch Review (1 mark):** Continuous PDCA improvement, line extension, or discontinuation decision.

**Role of Gates in Risk Reduction (extra narrative credit):** Each gate eliminates weak concepts, ensuring that capital is not wasted on unviable products. For the food industry — where spoilage and regulatory risk are acute — gates are the *defensive* backbone of PDP.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — PLC Curve, Strategies, and Examples (7 marks)

**PLC Curve (sketch — 1 mark for the S-shape, 1 mark for phase labels, 1 mark for axes labelled):**

```
Sales (₹)
   ▲
   │            ┌───────────────
   │          ╱                 ╲
   │        ╱                     ╲
   │      ╱                         ╲
   │    ╱                             ╲
   │  ╱                                 ╲
   │╱                                     ╲___
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────► Time
       Intro   Growth   Maturity      Decline
```

**Strategies per phase (4 marks, 1 per phase):**

| Phase | Strategy | Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Build awareness; penetration pricing (₹10 masala oats) or skimming (premium Greek yogurt) | Paper Boat storytelling launch in India |
| **Growth** | Mass distribution, market-share defense, line extensions | Epigamia Greek yogurt rapid SKU expansion |
| **Maturity** | Defend via "New & Improved," pack redesign, claims refresh | Maggi Noodles re-launch with "No Added MSG" |
| **Decline** | Harvest, reposition, discontinue, or replace with PDP successor | Diet Coke in sugar-taxed markets |

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the Product Life Cycle. Derive the Breakeven Volume formula. A new millet-based snack has fixed cost ₹6,00,000, variable cost ₹10/unit, and selling price ₹25/unit. Calculate the breakeven volume. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the strategic coupling between the Product Development Programme and the Product Life Cycle. Justify why food companies maintain parallel pipelines. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Definition, Derivation, and Calculation (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Product Life Cycle is the temporal evolution of a product's sales and profit across four phases — Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline.

**Derivation of Breakeven Volume (4 marks, step-by-step):**

Step 1: Total cost = fixed cost + variable cost × quantity
$$
C = F + VQ
$$

Step 2: Total revenue = price × quantity
$$
R = PQ
$$

Step 3: At breakeven, $R = C$:
$$
PQ = F + VQ
$$

Step 4: $Q(P - V) = F$

Step 5: $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{25 - 10} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{15} = 40{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Managerial interpretation:** The millet snack becomes profitable only after selling **40,000 units**, which dictates the minimum scale of pilot and test-market batches.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Strategic Coupling (7 marks)

**Definition of Coupling (1 mark):** Strategic coupling means the *end* of Maturity / *onset* of Decline of an existing SKU automatically *triggers* a new PDP cycle.

**Why Parallel Pipelines (4 marks, with sub-points):**

1. **Risk diversification** — Single-product dependence is fatal in food (e.g., Maggi's 2015 ban).
2. **Continuous revenue flow** — As one SKU declines, another enters Growth.
3. **Brand portfolio synergy** — Cross-selling via shared distribution, packaging line, cold chain.
4. **Regulatory and competitive agility** — Pipeline readiness allows fast response to FSSAI amendments, consumer trends (e.g., high-protein, low-sugar).
5. **Capacity utilization** — Multiple SKUs share plant capacity, smoothing fixed-cost recovery.

**Food-industry evidence (1 mark):** Nestlé India simultaneously runs PDPs for Maggi, KitKat, Nescafé — when one enters Maturity, another is in Growth. ITC Foods runs parallel pipelines for Aashirvaad, Sunfeast, and YiPPee.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The PDP creates; the PLC interprets. Their coupling is the *organizational metabolism* of any sustainable food business.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Drawing only 4 PLC phases** — Examiner expects the *eight* PDP stages, not the four PLC phases. These are two *different* frameworks; do not conflate.
> 2. **Forgetting the word "Gate"** — Each PDP stage terminates in a gate. Omitting the gate means losing 1 mark per stage in long answers.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation** — Breakeven and Bass-curve derivations must be shown algebraically, not merely stated.
> 4. **No food-industry examples** — Generic FMCG examples lose marks; examiners want *food-specific* illustrations (Maggi, Britannia, ITC, Amul, Nestle, regional brands).
> 5. **Confusing Penetration vs. Skimming pricing** — Penetration = low price, high volume, mass market; Skimming = high price, niche innovator segment.
> 6. **Missing the Coupling Concept** — Many students describe PDP and PLC as separate concepts without stating the *trigger* relationship (Maturity → New PDP).
> 7. **Omitting units in numericals** — Always write "₹ lakh", "units", "months", or "years" alongside the number.
> 8. **Skipping the FSSAI regulatory hook** — PDP in food must reference FSSAI / Codex / BIS — examiners actively look for this regulatory awareness.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and keep it beside you during the night-before revision:**

- ✅ **PDP = structured 8-stage process** for converting ideas into commercial food products.
- ✅ **Eight stages:** Idea Generation → Screening → Concept Development → Business Analysis → Formulation + Pilot → Test Marketing → Commercialization → Post-Launch Review.
- ✅ **Five Gates:** Screening, Business Case, Shelf-life/Sensory, Test-Market KPIs, PLC Transition.
- ✅ **PLC = 4-phase S-curve** of sales vs. time: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline.
- ✅ **PLC S-curve equation:** $S(t) = S_{\max} / (1 + e^{-k(t-t_0)})$.
- ✅ **Bass Diffusion** is the academic micro-foundation of the PLC macro S-curve.
- ✅ **Introduction:** Innovators (2.5%), low sales, negative profit, build awareness.
- ✅ **Growth:** Early adopters (13.5%), steep rise, max profit, defend share.
- ✅ **Maturity:** Early + Late majority (68%), plateau, defend via extensions.
- ✅ **Decline:** Laggards (16%), harvest or discontinue.
- ✅ **Breakeven Volume:** $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$.
- ✅ **NPV accept rule:** $NPV > 0$.
- ✅ **Payback:** $T_{PB} = I_0 / CF_{annual}$.
- ✅ **Test Market KPIs:** Trial Rate ≥ 20%, Repeat Rate ≥ 40%.
- ✅ **Food industry failure rate:** 70–80% in Year 1.
- ✅ **Strategic Coupling:** End of Maturity / Start of Decline triggers a new PDP.
- ✅ **Parallel pipelines** are essential for risk diversification in food majors.
- ✅ **Regulatory framework:** FSSAI Act 2006 (amended 2020), FSSAI License / Registration, Codex, BIS.
- ✅ **Pricing strategies:** Penetration (low, mass) vs. Skimming (high, niche).
- ✅ **Always include food-specific examples** — Maggi, Britannia, ITC, Amul, Nestle India, Epigamia, Paper Boat.

---

topics.title: Food Product Commercialization: Product Launch and Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Food Product Commercialization: Product Launch and Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Product Commercialization** is the systematic, multi-stage process of transitioning a successfully developed food prototype from the laboratory bench-scale into a full-scale, profitable, market-ready consumer product. It integrates scale-up engineering, regulatory compliance, packaging technology, market testing, and post-launch surveillance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Launch** is the strategic, coordinated release event through which a newly developed food product becomes physically available and visible to target consumers across defined distribution channels (retail, e-commerce, food-service). It involves the operational execution of the **4 P's of the Marketing Mix**: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Evaluation** is the structured, science-based assessment procedure used to determine whether a food product meets pre-defined quality, safety, sensory, and consumer-acceptance benchmarks before, during, and after commercial launch. It employs **objective (instrumental)**, **subjective (sensory)**, and **consumer-affective** testing methodologies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant-to-Supermarket" Journey

Imagine a home-baker (Meena) who perfects a unique jackfruit-chip recipe in her kitchen.

- **Stage 1 — Prototype**: Meena's single tray of chips is the *prototype* — it tastes great but cannot feed 10,000 customers.
- **Stage 2 — Scale-up**: She must borrow industrial fryers, find bulk jackfruit suppliers, and replicate the exact taste at **100 kg/batch**. This is **process scale-up**.
- **Stage 3 — Pilot launch**: She sells only in 3 local stores to test whether *strangers* (not her family) will buy it. This is **market testing / test marketing**.
- **Stage 4 — Full launch**: With positive feedback, she invests in **branding, packaging, FSSAI licensing, and distributors** — now the chips sit on supermarket shelves across Kerala. This is **Product Launch**.
- **Stage 5 — Post-launch evaluation**: She collects consumer reviews, monitors shelf-life defects, and reformulates if salt-level complaints arise. This is **Product Evaluation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Commercialization is the *bridge* between food science innovation and business reality. A perfect lab recipe that fails shelf-life testing, has no FSSAI license, or cannot be priced profitably will **never** become a commercial product.

---

## 1.3 Mandatory Standard Metrics & Definitions

| Standard Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Shelf Life** | Time until product becomes unacceptable | Days / Months |
| **Scale-up Factor** | Ratio of pilot batch to commercial batch | **10× to 1000×** |
| **Hedonic Score** | 9-point consumer liking scale | 1 (dislike extremely) – 9 (like extremely) |
| **POD (Preference of Difference)** | Proportion preferring new product | **≥ 60% preferred** = success |
| **Break-even Point** | Production volume where revenue = cost | Units / month |
| **FSSAI License No.** | Mandatory Indian food safety registration | 14-digit number |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Commercialization Pipeline — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **six sequential pillars** of food product commercialization:

### Pillar 1 — Scale-Up & Process Engineering
- Move from bench (1–10 kg) → pilot (50–500 kg) → commercial (>1,000 kg).
- Maintain critical parameters: temperature profiles, residence time, moisture content, pH, water activity ($a_w$).
- Apply **dimensional analysis** and **process analogy** to predict behavior.

### Pillar 2 — Regulatory Compliance
- **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** — mandatory for all commercial food products in India.
- **Legal metrology** — mandatory net weight, MRP, manufacturing date, batch code, best-before date.
- **Labeling compliance** as per Food Safety and Standards (Labeling & Display) Regulations, 2020.
- Nutritional information panel, ingredient declaration, allergen warning, veg/non-veg symbol (green/brown dot).

### Pillar 3 — Packaging Technology
- Primary packaging (direct food contact): protects against moisture, oxygen, light.
- Secondary packaging: branding, point-of-sale display.
- Tertiary packaging: bulk handling, transport.
- Material selection: **PET, HDPE, PP, Aluminum laminates, glass**.

### Pillar 4 — Product Launch (Go-to-Market Strategy)
- **Soft launch** → limited geography, gather feedback.
- **Hard launch** → full national/regional distribution.
- Distribution channels: General Trade, Modern Trade, HORECA (Hotel/Restaurant/Café), E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart, BigBasket), D2C (Direct-to-Consumer).
- Pricing strategies: Penetration pricing, skimming pricing, competitive pricing, cost-plus pricing.

### Pillar 5 — Product Evaluation (Pre-Launch)
- **Sensory Evaluation**: Discrimination tests (triangle, duo-trio), Descriptive analysis (QDA), Affective tests (Hedonic, JAR — Just-About-Right).
- **Instrumental Evaluation**: Color (Hunter L*, a*, b*), Texture (TPA — Texture Profile Analysis via texture analyzer), Moisture, $a_w$, Microbial counts.
- **Shelf-life Testing**: Accelerated (elevated T, ASLT) and Real-time.

### Pillar 6 — Post-Launch Evaluation & Continuous Improvement
- Consumer complaint analysis.
- Sales velocity tracking.
- Reformulation cycles.
- Brand equity monitoring.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Numerical & Theory Questions**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Key | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Break-Even Point (BEP)** $BEP = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $F$ = Fixed Cost, $P$ = Selling Price/unit, $V$ = Variable Cost/unit | Units (number of packs) |
| 2 | **Process Scale-up Factor** $SF = \dfrac{M_{commercial}}{M_{pilot}}$ | $M$ = batch mass | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | **Geometric Similarity (vessel)** $\dfrac{H_1}{D_1} = \dfrac{H_2}{D_2}$ | $H$ = height, $D$ = diameter of mixer/cooker | For agitator scaling |
| 4 | **Power Requirement Scale-up** $P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{m}$ | $N$ = impeller speed; $n, m$ are empirical exponents | For non-Newtonian foods, $n \approx 2$, $m \approx 3$ |
| 5 | **Water Activity ($a_w$)** $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | $p$ = vapor pressure of food, $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water | Microbial growth inhibited when $a_w < 0.6$ |
| 6 | **Shelf-life Acceleration (Q₁₀ rule)** $Q_{10} = \dfrac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T}$ | $k$ = deterioration rate constant | $Q_{10} \approx 2$ for many foods |
| 7 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | $CF_t$ = cash flow, $r$ = discount rate, $C_0$ = initial investment | ₹ (rupees) |
| 8 | **Hedonic Score Mean** $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{N}$ | $H_i$ = individual score, $N$ = panel size | 1–9 scale |
| 9 | **Cost Per Unit** $CPU = \dfrac{C_{raw} + C_{pack} + C_{labor} + C_{overhead}}{Y_{yield}}$ | $C$ = cost components, $Y$ = yield fraction | ₹/pack |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Syntax Rule:** When writing absolute value, conditional probability, or modulus expressions in the table above, ALWAYS use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the raw pipe character `|`. The pipe character breaks the markdown table parser and causes rendering failure.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost Engineering**: The BEP formula directly guides whether to launch — a product with a BEP exceeding predicted 12-month sales should be **rejected** or **reformulated**.
- **Quality Engineering**: $a_w$ and moisture content together predict microbial spoilage risk; critical for bakery, dairy, dried products.
- **Sensory Science**: The 9-point Hedonic scale is the global gold-standard for consumer acceptance testing in food R&D labs.
- **Process Engineering**: Power-scaling equation enables accurate selection of industrial motor size for mixers, blenders, extruders.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Break-Even Point (BEP) for a Food Product

This derivation is a high-frequency KTU numerical question (often appearing in Part B for 7 marks).

**Problem Setup:** A KTU student startup plans to launch a **banana chips** product. Fixed costs (rent, equipment depreciation, license fees) = ₹ **50,000/month**. Selling price = ₹ **20/pack**. Variable cost per pack (raw banana, oil, salt, packaging, labor) = ₹ **12/pack**. Find the monthly break-even production volume.

**Step 1 — Define Total Cost Function**

The total cost $TC$ is the sum of fixed and variable costs:

$$TC = F + (V \cdot Q)$$

where $Q$ is the quantity produced per month.

**Step 2 — Define Total Revenue Function**

$$TR = P \cdot Q$$

where $P$ is the selling price per unit.

**Step 3 — Apply the Break-Even Condition**

At break-even, total revenue exactly equals total cost, so net profit $\pi = 0$:

$$TR = TC$$

Substituting:

$$P \cdot Q = F + (V \cdot Q)$$

**Step 4 — Solve Algebraically for $Q$**

Rearranging to isolate $Q$:

$$P \cdot Q - V \cdot Q = F$$

$$Q \cdot (P - V) = F$$

$$\boxed{BEP = Q = \dfrac{F}{P - V}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Given $F = 50{,}000$ ₹/month, $P = 20$ ₹/pack, $V = 12$ ₹/pack:

$$BEP = \dfrac{50{,}000}{20 - 12}$$

$$BEP = \dfrac{50{,}000}{8}$$

$$BEP = 6{,}250 \text{ packs/month}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

> The startup must sell at least **6,250 packs per month** to recover fixed costs. Below this volume, the business operates at a loss. KTU examiners expect this **decision-oriented interpretation** for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Scale-Up for a Food Mixer

A bench-top planetary mixer (impeller diameter $D_1 = 0.2$ m, speed $N_1 = 60$ rpm, power $P_1 = 0.5$ kW) is to be scaled up geometrically to industrial size ($D_2 = 1.0$ m). The mixing speed must be reduced to maintain equal tip speed: $N_2 = N_1 \cdot (D_1 / D_2)$. For a non-Newtonian batter, empirical exponents are $n = 2$ and $m = 3$. Find $P_2$.

**Step 1 — Establish Geometric Similarity**

For dynamic similarity in mixing:

$$\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.2} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Determine the New Impeller Speed**

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = 60 \cdot \left(\dfrac{0.2}{1.0}\right) = 12 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Power Scale-Up Equation**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{n} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{m}$$

$$P_2 = 0.5 \cdot (5)^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{12}{60}\right)^{3}$$

$$P_2 = 0.5 \cdot 25 \cdot (0.2)^{3}$$

$$P_2 = 12.5 \cdot 0.008$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 0.1 \text{ kW} = 100 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

> The **larger** mixer actually requires **less** power when tip-speed similarity is maintained for shear-thinning batter — a counterintuitive but critical result. KTU examiners reward students who explain the *physical reasoning*, not just plug numbers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensory Evaluation Test Selection

| Test Type | Question Asked | Best When | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test** | "Which sample is different?" | Detecting small differences | Number correct vs. table value |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | "Which one matches the reference?" | When one sample is a control | Correct identifications |
| **Paired Preference** | "Which do you prefer?" | Comparing two formulations | % preferring each |
| **9-point Hedonic** | "How much do you like this?" | Measuring overall liking | Mean score, ANOVA |
| **JAR (Just-About-Right)** | "Is salt too little / just right / too much?" | Optimizing ingredient level | Penalty analysis plot |
| **CATA (Check-All-That-Apply)** | "Which words describe this?" | Sensory profiling by consumers | Frequency maps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 End-to-End Commercialization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idea & Concept Screening] --> B[Lab Prototype Development]
    B --> C[Process Optimization]
    C --> D[Pilot Plant Scale-Up]
    D --> E[Process Validation]

    G --> H[Shelf Life Testing]

    J --> K[Cost Engineering - BEP Analysis]

    L -- No --> N[Reformulation or Discontinue]
    M --> O[Marketing Mix 4P]

    P --> Q[Post Launch Evaluation]
    Q --> R[Sales & Feedback Analysis]

    S -- Yes --> B
    S -- No --> T[Brand Expansion]
```

    Q2 -- Two samples --> A1[Duo-Trio or Paired Preference]
    Q2 -- Three samples --> A2[Triangle Test]

    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Statistical Analysis]
    Q3 --> B3[Trained Panel 8 to 12 experts]
