<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define food product commercialization. List the four sequential stages of commercialization as per KTU 2024 syllabus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Food product commercialization is the systematic process of transforming a laboratory-scale prototype into a market-ready, profitable consumer food product (1 mark).

The four sequential stages are:
1. **Stage I — Idea Generation and Concept Screening** (0.5 mark)
2. **Stage II — Product and Process Development** (0.5 mark)
3. **Stage III — Market Testing and Product Evaluation** (0.5 mark)
4. **Stage IV — Commercialization and Launch** (0.5 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a 9-point hedonic scale? Mention its significance in sensory evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 9-point hedonic scale is a structured affective sensory test where consumers rate their overall liking of a food product on a structured 1–9 scale, where **1 = dislike extremely** and **9 = like extremely** (1.5 marks).

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- Measures **consumer acceptance** rather than just discrimination.
- Statistically analyzable via ANOVA and mean comparison.
- A mean score $\geq 7$ typically indicates commercial viability.
- Used in **product optimization**, **reformulation**, and **final product selection** before launch.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

#### (a) **Explain in detail the sequential steps involved in the commercialization of a food product, from concept to market launch.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Idea Generation and Concept Screening (1 mark):**
Identify market gaps, consumer trends, nutritional needs, and emerging demands. Concepts are screened for technical feasibility, financial viability, and regulatory clearability.

**Step 2 — Product and Process Development (1 mark):**
Recipe formulation, ingredient selection, laboratory prototype preparation. Optimize processing parameters (temperature, time, pH, moisture).

**Step 3 — Process Scale-up (1 mark):**
Transition from bench (1–10 kg) → pilot (50–500 kg) → commercial (>1000 kg). Apply scale-up equations for mixers, heat exchangers, dryers. Validate that sensory and quality attributes remain consistent across scales.

**Step 4 — Regulatory Compliance (1 mark):**
Obtain **FSSAI license** (14-digit registration), comply with **Legal Metrology Act 2009**, **FSS (Labeling & Display) Regulations 2020** — including mandatory declarations of ingredients, allergens, nutritional info, veg/non-veg symbol, MRP, batch code, manufacturing date, best-before date, and country of origin.

**Step 5 — Packaging Design (1 mark):**
Select primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging based on product sensitivity to moisture, oxygen, light, and mechanical damage. Ensure food-grade compliance.

**Step 6 — Shelf-life and Sensory Evaluation (1 mark):**
Conduct accelerated shelf-life testing (ASLT) at elevated temperature/humidity, and real-time testing. Apply **Triangle**, **Hedonic**, and **JAR** sensory tests with trained panels and consumer panels respectively.

**Step 7 — Market Test Launch and Full Commercialization (1 mark):**
Pilot launch in a limited geography, gather consumer feedback, refine, then full-scale national launch with marketing mix execution.

---

#### (b) **A KTU student entrepreneur plans to launch a millet-based snack. The fixed cost is ₹ 80,000 per month. The selling price is ₹ 30 per pack and the variable cost is ₹ 18 per pack. Calculate the break-even point and interpret the result. Also, mention TWO pricing strategies that could be adopted for market entry.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Break-Even Formula (3 marks):**

$$BEP = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$$

$$BEP = \dfrac{80{,}000}{30 - 18}$$

$$BEP = \dfrac{80{,}000}{12}$$

$$\boxed{BEP = 6{,}667 \text{ packs/month}}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The entrepreneur must sell a minimum of **6,667 packs every month** just to cover all costs. Any sales above this volume generate profit; below this, the business incurs a loss.

**Step 3 — Two Pricing Strategies (3 marks):**

1. **Penetration Pricing (1.5 marks):** Set initial price lower than competitors (e.g., ₹ 25/pack) to attract mass-market adoption quickly, build customer base, and deter competitors. Suitable for novel millet snacks where consumer awareness is low.

2. **Skimming Pricing (1.5 marks):** Set initial price high (e.g., ₹ 45/pack) targeting health-conscious urban premium consumers, then gradually reduce. Suitable when the product is perceived as a "superfood" with unique health benefits.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

#### (a) **Discuss the different methods of product evaluation used before and after the commercial launch of a food product.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**A. Pre-Launch Product Evaluation (4 marks):**

**1. Instrumental Evaluation (1 mark):**
- **Color:** Hunter L*, a*, b* via colorimeter/spectrophotometer.
- **Texture:** Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) using texture analyzer — measures hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness.
- **Chemical:** Moisture, $a_w$, pH, titratable acidity, peroxide value (for oils).
- **Microbiological:** Total Plate Count (TPC), yeast & mold count, coliform count, pathogen screening (Salmonella, E. coli).

**2. Sensory Evaluation (1.5 marks):**
- **Discrimination tests:** Triangle test, duo-trio test — detect if a difference exists.
- **Descriptive tests:** Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA) by trained panel — generate full sensory profile.
- **Affective tests:** 9-point Hedonic scale, JAR scale, CATA — measure consumer liking.

**3. Shelf-Life Testing (1 mark):**
- **Accelerated Shelf-Life Testing (ASLT):** Storage at elevated T (e.g., 35°C, 45°C), humidity. Apply $Q_{10}$ rule.
- **Real-time testing:** Storage at ambient conditions for actual product life.
- Monitor for **rancidity, microbial growth, color fade, texture hardening, moisture gain/loss**.

**4. Consumer Test Marketing (0.5 mark):**
- Limited distribution in test stores, gather purchase intent and feedback.

**B. Post-Launch Product Evaluation (3 marks):**

**1. Sales Data Analysis (1 mark):**
- Track sell-through, repeat purchase rate, velocity per SKU. Drop in repeat rate indicates consumer dissatisfaction.

**2. Consumer Complaint Analysis (1 mark):**
- Categorize complaints (taste, packaging, shelf-life, price). Use Pareto chart to prioritize reformulation targets.

**3. Brand Equity Monitoring (1 mark):**
- Track Net Promoter Score (NPS), social-media sentiment, in-store audits. Identify whether the product is achieving long-term brand recall.

---

#### (b) **Explain the FSSAI labeling requirements for a packaged food product in India. List any SEVEN mandatory declarations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**FSSAI Labeling Regulations, 2020** mandate every packaged food product sold in India to carry the following **seven mandatory declarations** on the principal display panel (7 × 1 mark each):

1. **Name of the Food** — must be clear and specific (e.g., "Tender Coconut Water" not just "Beverage").
2. **List of Ingredients** — in descending order of composition, including additives with class names.
3. **Net Quantity** — in grams/millilitres using standard units.
4. **Date Marking** — "Date of Manufacture" / "Best Before" / "Use By" date.
5. **MRP (Maximum Retail Price)** — inclusive of all taxes.
6. **Name and Address of Manufacturer/Packer** — with pin code.
7. **FSSAI Logo and License Number** — the 14-digit registration number, displayed prominently.
8. **Veg/Non-Veg Symbol** — green dot for vegetarian, brown triangle for non-vegetarian.
9. **Allergen Declaration** — for ingredients like nuts, gluten, soy, milk.
10. **Nutritional Information** — Energy (kcal), protein, carbohydrate, fat, sugar, sodium per 100 g or per serving.

> (Note: Any SEVEN out of the above ten may be listed for full 7 marks. Examiners prefer *complete* answers.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> - ❌ Writing only the **definition** without listing the **stages** → loses 1 mark in Part A.
> - ❌ Confusing **break-even** with **profit maximization** — they are mathematically different.
> - ❌ In sensory questions, mixing up **discrimination** vs. **affective** tests — they answer different questions.
> - ❌ Forgetting **FSSAI** in regulatory questions — Kerala examiners expect Indian regulatory awareness.
> - ❌ Skipping the **interpretation step** after a numerical derivation — examiners award 1 mark for "what the result means in business context."
> - ❌ Using vague terms like "consumer testing" instead of specifying **Hedonic, Triangle, JAR, QDA**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Commercialization = Prototype → Scale-up → Compliance → Packaging → Launch → Evaluation.**
- **Product Launch** involves the **4P Marketing Mix** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).
- **Product Evaluation** has **three pillars**: Instrumental, Sensory, Consumer.
- **Break-Even Point** formula: $\displaystyle BEP = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ — must be computed and interpreted.
- **FSSAI License** (14-digit) is non-negotiable for any commercial food sale in India.
- **9-point Hedonic scale**: mean score $\geq 7$ generally signals commercial viability.
- **Shelf-life testing** uses **ASLT** (Accelerated) and real-time methods.
- **Water activity ($a_w$)** below **0.6** inhibits most microbial spoilage.
- **Sensory tests differ in purpose**: Triangle/Duo-Trio (discrimination), Hedonic/JAR (acceptance), QDA (profiling).
- **Pricing strategies**: Penetration, Skimming, Competitive, Cost-Plus.
- **Scale-up equation**: Power scales as $\left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^n \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^m$.
- **Always interpret** numerical results in business/engineering context for full KTU marks.
- **Labeling compliance** under FSSAI 2020 — minimum 7 mandatory declarations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FOOD TECHNOLOGY** is the application of **food science** principles — chemistry, biology, physics, microbiology, and engineering — to the **selection, preservation, processing, packaging, distribution, and use** of safe, nutritious, and wholesome food products.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course *PEFTT411 – Food Product Design and Development*, **Technology** (Module 2) refers to the systematic engineering and scientific framework that transforms a **product concept (Module 1)** into a **manufacturable, shelf-stable, and consumer-acceptable food product**. It is the operational bridge between **idea generation** and **commercial production**.

The discipline encompasses the following interlocking pillars:

| Pillar | Core Function |
|---|---|
| **Processing Technology** | Converts raw agri-materials into edible forms |
| **Preservation Technology** | Extends shelf life and ensures microbial safety |
| **Packaging Technology** | Protects product from external contamination & damage |
| **Quality Assurance Technology** | Ensures compliance with FSSAI / Codex / BIS standards |
| **Automation & Control Technology** | Enables reproducible, large-scale production |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Kitchen on Steroids"**
> Imagine your grandmother's kitchen. She uses fire, salt, oil, and sunlight to preserve food. **Food Technology** is that same kitchen, but engineered, scaled, and standardized. The stove becomes a **heat exchanger**, the salt becomes a precisely dosed **preservative system**, and the sunlight becomes a controlled **UV / thermal sterilization chamber**. The goal is identical — make food safe, tasty, and lasting — but achieved with **scientific precision** rather than intuition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Technology as a Translator"**
> Think of food technology as a **universal translator** between *nature* (raw tomato, milk, fish) and *consumer* (ketchup carton, pasteurized milk pouch, canned tuna). It listens to the molecular "language" of the raw material and translates it into a stable, convenient, attractive product form.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Food Technology)

The following are **high-yield constants and metrics** that KTU examiners frequently test:

- **Water Activity (a_w)** → safe storage threshold for most pathogens: **a_w < 0.86**
- **Thermal Death Time (TDT)** → time to destroy a microbial population at a given temperature
- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time)** → time at a reference temperature to achieve **90 % (1-log) reduction** in microbial count
- **Z-value** → temperature increase (°C) required to reduce D-value by a factor of **10**
- **F-value (F₀)** → equivalent sterilization time at a reference temperature (typically **121.1 °C** for low-acid foods, **z = 10 °C**)
- **Pasteurization reference temperatures**: **72 °C × 15 s** (HTST) or **63 °C × 30 min** (LTLT)
- **Cold Chain Standard**: chilled storage **≤ 4 °C**, frozen storage **≤ -18 °C**
- **FSSAI Standard Plate Count limit** in ready-to-eat foods: **< 10⁴ CFU/g**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically addresses *processing and preservation technologies* that students must map against their chosen product prototype. Marks are awarded for **justifying technology choice**, not merely listing techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Food Technology — Structured Logic

#### Pillar A — **Processing (Unit Operations)**

Food processing is built on a **chain of unit operations**. Each operation is a physical, chemical, or biological transformation with predictable inputs and outputs.

1. **Raw material reception & cleaning** → removes dirt, microbes, pesticide residues
2. **Sorting & grading** → ensures uniform quality
3. **Size reduction / Size enlargement** → grinding, milling, agglomeration
4. **Mixing & blending** → homogenization of ingredients
5. **Heat processing** → pasteurization, sterilization, baking, frying
6. **Mass transfer operations** → drying, evaporation, distillation, extraction
7. **Separation** → filtration, centrifugation, membrane separation
8. **Forming & shaping** → extrusion, molding, sheeting
9. **Packaging & labeling** → primary, secondary, tertiary

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When designing a product (say, a *ready-to-drink mango beverage*), you must justify **why** you chose HTST pasteurization over UHT or HPP — this is a frequent 7-mark question.

#### Pillar B — **Preservation (Hurdle Technology Concept)**

The **Hurdle Technology** framework, pioneered by Lothar Leistner, posits that a combination of **multiple mild preservation factors ("hurdles")** is more effective and produces better sensory quality than one severe treatment.

**Common hurdles used in food preservation:**

| Hurdle | Symbol | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature (high) | **T** | 60 – 121 °C |
| Temperature (low) | **T** | 0 – 4 °C (chill), ≤ -18 °C (frozen) |
| Water activity | **a_w** | 0.60 – 0.95 |
| Acidity (pH) | **pH** | 3.0 – 4.5 (acidic foods) |
| Redox potential | **E_h** | low (anaerobic) |
| Preservatives | **C** | chemical / natural antimicrobials |
| Competitive flora | **CF** | lactic acid bacteria |
| Modified atmosphere | **MA** | N₂ + CO₂ packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key principle:** Each hurdle targets a different microbial weakness. Staphylococcus is sensitive to **low a_w**; Clostridium botulinum is sensitive to **low pH**; Pseudomonas is sensitive to **low temperature**. Combining hurdles **multiplies shelf life** without any single treatment being harsh.

#### Pillar C — **Packaging (Protection + Communication)**

Modern packaging is **active** (interacts with the product) and **intelligent** (senses & communicates).

- **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging):** replaces air with N₂ + CO₂
- **VP (Vacuum Packaging):** removes O₂ → inhibits aerobic spoilage
- **Active packaging:** oxygen scavengers, ethylene absorbers, antimicrobial films
- **Smart packaging:** time-temperature indicators (TTI), freshness sensors, QR traceability
- **Aseptic packaging:** sterile product + sterile package filled in sterile environment

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** for Module 2. Master the variables, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}$ | $N$ = survivors (CFU/mL), $N_0$ = initial count, $t$ = time (min), $D$ = D-value (min) | Calculate microbial log reduction after a thermal process |
| 2 | $\log D_2 = \log D_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{z}$ | $D_1, D_2$ = D-values at $T_1, T_2$ (°C); $z$ = z-value (°C) | Convert D-value between two temperatures |
| 3 | $F_0 = 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}} \cdot \Delta t$ | $T$ = process temp (°C), $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C, $\Delta t$ = time interval | Calculate sterilization lethality (F₀) of a thermal process |
| 4 | $Q_{10} = \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T}$ | $k$ = reaction rate constant | Estimate acceleration of spoilage per 10 °C rise |
| 5 | $\text{SL} = \frac{\text{SL}_{ref}}{Q_{10}^{(T_{ref} - T)/10}}$ | SL = shelf life (days), $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature | Predict shelf life at any storage temperature (Arrhenius-type) |
| 6 | $a_w = \frac{P}{P_0}$ | $P$ = vapor pressure of solution, $P_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water | Predict microbial growth, drying, and storage stability |
| 7 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = K | Arrhenius kinetics for quality loss |
| 8 | $q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $q$ = heat transferred (W), $h$ = heat transfer coeff. (W/m²K), $A$ = area (m²), $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K) | Sizing heat exchangers for pasteurization/sterilization |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/s), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s) | Mass balance for continuous processing lines |
| 10 | $t_{evap} = \frac{m \cdot \lambda}{q}$ | $m$ = mass of water (kg), $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg), $q$ = heat input (W) | Time to concentrate a liquid by evaporation |
| 11 | $\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $D$ = diameter (m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Determine flow regime (laminar < 2300, turbulent > 4000) in pipe/tubular pasteurizer |
| 12 | $Weibull \;\; \log(N/N_0) = -(t/\delta)^p$ | $\delta$ = scale parameter, $p$ = shape parameter | Non-linear microbial inactivation modeling |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule (Strict):** Absolute value and "such that" symbols in this sheet use `\vert` and `\mid` (not raw `pipe`) to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

> [!NOTE]
> A **food technologist** is essentially a **chemical engineer specialized for biological matrices**. The same equations used in petrochemical plants (mass balance, energy balance, heat transfer, Arrhenius kinetics) are applied to milk, fruit pulp, and meat. The difference is that the "reactors" are *food-safe* and the "products" are *perishable*. This is why a B.Tech Food Technology graduate is expected to be fluent in **engineering mathematics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First-Order Kinetics to D-value (Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **14-mark model derivation** for Part B questions on microbial inactivation kinetics. Practice until you can reproduce it line-by-line.

We begin with the **first-order microbial inactivation law**:

$$
-\frac{dN}{dt} = k \cdot N
$$

where $k$ is the specific inactivation rate constant (per minute). This is identical in form to radioactive decay.

**Step 1 — Separate variables**

$$
\frac{dN}{N} = -k \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides from initial $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N$ at time $t$**

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \, t
$$

**Step 4 — Convert natural log to base-10 log (using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$)**

$$
2.303 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \, t
$$

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k}{2.303} \, t
$$

**Step 5 — Define the D-value**

By definition, $D$ is the time required for a **1-log (90 %) reduction**, i.e. when $\log(N/N_0) = -1$:

$$
-1 = -\frac{k}{2.303} \cdot D
$$

Solving:

$$
D = \frac{2.303}{k}
$$

**Step 6 — Final working form**

Substitute $k = 2.303 / D$ back into the integrated rate law:

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}
$$

This is the **canonical survivor curve equation**, the cornerstone of thermal process calculation in food technology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5-log reduction (e.g. from 10⁵ to 1 CFU/mL) requires $t = 5D$. For milk pasteurization targeting *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, $D_{72°C} \approx 0.5$ s, hence the legal HTST spec of **72 °C × 15 s = 30D**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-value and Thermal Death Time Relationship

We start from empirical observation that D-value changes exponentially with temperature. Therefore:

$$
\log_{10} D = a - b \cdot T
$$

where $a, b$ are constants. The **z-value** is defined as the temperature increase needed to reduce D by a factor of 10, i.e. $\log_{10} D$ decreases by 1. Therefore:

$$
b = \frac{1}{z}
$$

So:

$$
\log_{10} D_2 = \log_{10} D_1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{z}
$$

Solving for $D_2$:

$$
D_2 = D_1 \cdot 10^{-(T_1 - T_2)/z}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A higher z-value means the organism is **more heat-resistant** (requires larger temperature jump to kill). *C. botulinum* has $z = 10$ °C — the reference used to define $F_0 = 121.1$ °C.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection for Common Product Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU students must be able to justify a technology choice in 7-mark answers.** This decision matrix is a high-yield template.

| Product Type | Recommended Processing | Recommended Preservation | Recommended Packaging | Key Quality Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RTE Fruit Salad** | Mild blanch + cold chain | MAP + ascorbic acid dip | PET clamshell with O₂ scrubber | Vitamin C retention > 70 % |
| **UHT Milk** | UHT 138 °C × 4 s | Aseptic packaging | 6-layer Tetra Pak | 9-log reduction of *B. cereus* spores |
| **Canned Tomato Puree** | Hot-break + refining + concentrate to 28 °Brix | Retort 121 °C × 15 min | Tin-plated steel can | *C. botulinum* 12-log inactivation |
| **Frozen Peas** | Blanch 90 °C × 90 s | IQF (-30 °C blast) | Polyethylene pouch | Texture (TPA hardness) |
| **Extruded Snack** | HTST extrusion 150 °C | Low a_w (0.3) via drying | Metallized PP pouch | Water activity < 0.6 |
| **Probiotic Yogurt** | Homogenization 200 bar | pH 4.5 + cold chain 4 °C | PS cup with foil lid | Viable count > 10⁶ CFU/g till end of SL |
| **Cold-Pressed Juice** | HPP 600 MPa × 3 min | Refrigeration 4 °C | Glass bottle | Native enzyme inactivation, vitamin retention |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Shelf Life from Arrhenius (Model Answer Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU question:** *"A sauce has a shelf life of 180 days at 25 °C. If the Q₁₀ = 2.5, predict the shelf life at 35 °C and at 4 °C. Comment on the practical implication."*

**Step 1 — State the governing equation**

$$
\text{SL} = \frac{\text{SL}_{ref}}{Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}}
$$

where $\Delta T = T_{ref} - T_{storage}$.

**Step 2 — Case 1: T = 35 °C (ΔT = 25 - 35 = -10 °C)**

$$
\text{SL}_{35} = \frac{180}{2.5^{(-10)/10}} = \frac{180}{2.5^{-1}} = \frac{180}{0.4} = 450 \; \text{days}
$$

Wait — this is a sign-convention trap. Let us re-derive carefully. We want SL at **higher** temperature. Spoilage rate increases; shelf life **decreases**. The correct convention is:

$$
\text{SL}_{T} = \text{SL}_{ref} \cdot Q_{10}^{(T_{ref} - T)/10}
$$

So for $T = 35$ °C:

$$
\text{SL}_{35} = 180 \cdot 2.5^{(25 - 35)/10} = 180 \cdot 2.5^{-1} = 180 / 2.5 = 72 \; \text{days}
$$

**Step 3 — Case 2: T = 4 °C (refrigeration)**

$$
\text{SL}_{4} = 180 \cdot 2.5^{(25 - 4)/10} = 180 \cdot 2.5^{2.1}
$$

$$
2.5^{2.1} = e^{2.1 \cdot \ln 2.5} = e^{2.1 \cdot 0.9163} = e^{1.924} \approx 6.85
$$

$$
\text{SL}_{4} = 180 \cdot 6.85 \approx 1233 \; \text{days} \approx 3.4 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical comment**

Cold chain extends shelf life **~17×** vs. ambient, validating the need for refrigerated distribution. Even a 10 °C rise halves shelf life — the **Q₁₀ effect** is critical in tropical Kerala climate, where ambient storage (30–35 °C) dramatically shortens shelf life.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU student error:** Inverting the Q₁₀ exponent. Always check: "Does raising temperature make spoilage faster?" → **Yes** → shelf life must **decrease**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Food Technology Decision Tree — Mermaid Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material] --> B{Quality Check}
    B -- Pass --> C[Cleaning & Sorting]
    B -- Fail --> Z1[Reject / By-product]
    C --> D[Size Reduction]
    D --> E{Mixing & Formulation}
    E --> F[Processing Step]
    F --> F1[Heat HTST]
    F --> F2[Heat UHT]
    F --> F3[HPP Cold Press]
    F --> F4[Freeze Dry]
    F --> F5[Fermentation]
    F1 --> G[Preservation Hurdles]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> G1[Low aw]
    G --> G2[Low pH]
    G --> G3[Cold Chain]
    G --> G4[Preservatives]
    G1 --> H[Packaging]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> H1[MAP / Vacuum]
    H --> H2[Aseptic]
    H --> H3[Active Smart]
    H1 --> I[QA QC Lab Test]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Storage & Distribution]
    J --> K[Consumer]
```

### 4.2 Hurdle Technology Concept — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microbial Cell] --> B{Hurdle 1: Low aw}
    B --> C{Hurdle 2: Low pH}
    C --> D{Hurdle 3: Cold Temp}
    D --> E{Hurdle 4: Preservatives}
    E --> F{Hurdle 5: Low E redox}
    F --> G[Cell Stress Multiplying]
    G --> H[Inactivation Multi Log]
    H --> I[Shelf Stable Safe Product]
```

### 4.3 Thermal Process Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Food] --> S[Preheating]
    S --> T[Hold Tube at T_target]
    T --> U[Time Equals N x D]
    U --> V[F0 or Pasteurization Units Achieved]
    V --> W[Cooling Section]
    W --> X[Aseptic Packaging]
    X --> Y[Shelf Stable]
```

### 4.4 Technology Selection Matrix — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Product Prototype] --> Q1{Is it low acid pH > 4.6}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Target C botulinum 12 log]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Target Pathogens Plus Spoilage]
    R1 --> S1[Retort 121.1 C]
    R1 --> S2[Autoclave]
    R1 --> S3[Aseptic Filled]
    R2 --> T1{Is it heat sensitive}
    T1 -- Yes --> U1[HPP 600 MPa]
    T1 -- Yes --> U2[PEF Pulsed Electric Field]
    T1 -- Yes --> U3[Non Thermal Plasma]
    T1 -- No --> V1[HTST 72 C x 15 s]
    T1 -- No --> V2[UHT 138 C x 4 s]
```
