<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Consumer Behaviour — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> **Consumer Behaviour** is the systematic study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and wants. In the context of food product design, it is the analytical framework used to understand the *why* and *how* behind every food purchase and consumption decision.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, consumer behaviour is treated as a **cross-functional discipline** that bridges food technology, sensory science, marketing, and human psychology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Consumer Behaviour Matters in Food Product Design:**
> A technically perfect food product that the consumer rejects on the shelf is a **commercial failure**. Consumer behaviour studies convert *technological capability* into *market reality*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Choice" 🍽️

Imagine you walk into a food court with **15 stalls** offering different cuisines. You:
- **Glance** at the menu boards (visual cue → perception)
- **Remember** a friend's recommendation (memory → past experience)
- **Smell** the sizzling tawa (sensory input → physiological response)
- **Check your wallet** (economic factor → affordability)
- **Order** a dish (decision)
- **Eat and rate** it (post-purchase evaluation)

**Every step above is consumer behaviour in action.** The food product developer must engineer each of these touchpoints — the packaging, aroma release, price point, and taste — based on this invisible "script" running inside the consumer's mind.

---

## 1.3 The Role of the Consumer in Product Development

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Module 2):**
> The **Role of Consumers in Product Development** refers to the active or passive involvement of end-users in identifying needs, generating ideas, evaluating prototypes, and validating the final product through structured consumer research methodologies.

### The 5 Roles a Consumer Plays in Product Development:

| # | Role | Description | Food Industry Example |
|---|------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Need Identifier** | Articulates unmet needs | Demands "low-sugar" but tasty breakfast cereal |
| 2 | **Idea Source** | Provides innovation cues through complaints/wishes | "I wish there was a ready-to-eat idli that stays soft for 6 hours" |
| 3 | **Concept Tester** | Evaluates early prototypes via focus groups | Tests 3 variants of a new beverage for acceptance |
| 4 | **Final Validator** | Confirms purchase intent at market level | Shelf test, in-home usage test (IHUT) |
| 5 | **Post-Launch Feedback Loop** | Drives line extensions and reformulations | Drives creation of "Maggi Masala" from noodle base |

---

## 1.4 The Consumer–Product Development Loop (Conceptual Snapshot)

The consumer is **not a passive recipient** but a **co-creator** in modern food product design.

$$\text{Consumer Insight} \rightarrow \text{Product Brief} \rightarrow \text{Prototype} \rightarrow \text{Consumer Test} \rightarrow \text{Refined Product} \rightarrow \text{Market}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:**
> The **Stage-Gate Model** used in food product development mandates a **consumer gate review** at every stage (Concept → Development → Launch). Skipping consumer input is considered a major reason for the **~75% new product failure rate** in the FMCG food sector.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Consumer Behaviour Studies

The following **standard metrics** are mandated by the KTU 2024 PEFTT411 syllabus for consumer behaviour evaluation:

- **Acceptance Score** — measured on a **9-point hedonic scale** (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely)
- **Purchase Intent Score** — measured on a **5-point scale** (definitely would not buy → definitely would buy)
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** — **5-point bipolar scale** for attribute optimization
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — willingness-to-recommend metric, range **−100 to +100**
- **Top-of-Mind Awareness (TOMA)** — unaided brand recall percentage
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Consumer Decision-Making Process (CDP)

The classical **Engel–Blackwell–Miniard (EBM) Model** is the foundation of consumer behaviour analysis. For food products, this is adapted as follows:

### Step-by-Step Logic of the CDP:

1. **Need Recognition** — The consumer perceives a gap between current state and desired state.
   - *Trigger examples*: Hunger, health awareness, social media exposure, advertising.

2. **Information Search** — Two types:
   - **Internal Search** — Memory recall of past experiences.
   - **External Search** — Reading labels, asking friends, online reviews.

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** — Comparing brands on:
   - Price, quality, taste, brand trust, packaging, nutritional profile.

4. **Purchase Decision** — The actual point of sale.
   - Influenced by **situational factors** (stock availability, promotion, store layout).

5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour** — The critical phase:
   - **Cognitive Dissonance** (buyer's regret) if expectation ≠ reality.
   - **Satisfaction/Dissatisfaction** determines repurchase.
   - **Word-of-mouth** influences future buyers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:**
> Post-purchase behaviour is the **starting point of the next CDP cycle**. In food products, this is captured through the **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** metric, which directly feeds back into product reformulation.

---

## 2.2 Factors Influencing Consumer Behaviour — The 4-Factor Model

| Factor | Sub-Determinants | Food Product Example |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Cultural** | Culture, subculture, social class | Region-specific spice preferences (Kerala vs Punjab) |
| **Social** | Reference groups, family, roles | Mother's brand choice for kids' snacks |
| **Personal** | Age, lifestyle, personality, economy | Millennials prefer plant-based proteins |
| **Psychological** | Motivation, perception, learning, beliefs | "Healthy = green packaging" perception bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cultural factors** are the **deepest** and most difficult to change. **Psychological factors** are the most **immediate** triggers of purchase.

---

## 2.3 The Howard–Sheth Model (Simplified)

The **Howard–Sheth Model** postulates that consumer decision-making operates on three levels:

$$\text{Extensive Problem Solving (EPS)} \rightarrow \text{Limited Problem Solving (LPS)} \rightarrow \text{Routinized Response Behavior (RRB)}$$

- **EPS** → New, unfamiliar food category (e.g., first time trying *kombucha*).
- **LPS** → Known category, new brand (e.g., switching from Amul to a local butter brand).
- **RRB** → Habitual purchase (e.g., always buying the same *Parle-G* pack).

---

## 2.4 The Diffusion of Innovation Curve (Everett Rogers)

For new food products, consumer adoption follows:

$$\text{Innovators (2.5\%)} \rightarrow \text{Early Adopters (13.5\%)} \rightarrow \text{Early Majority (34\%)} \rightarrow \text{Late Majority (34\%)} \rightarrow \text{Laggards (16\%)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:**
> The first 16% (Innovators + Early Adopters) are targeted in **test markets** like Bangalore, Mumbai, or Kochi for new food launches in India.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Consumer Behaviour Metrics

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units / Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|------------------|----------|---------------|----------------------------|
| **Mean Hedonic Score** | $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum H_i}{n}$ | Score (1–9) | Higher = greater acceptance |
| **Purchase Intent Index** | $PI = \frac{\sum (w_i \times r_i)}{N_{max}}$ | 0–1 (normalized) | Threshold for launch: $\geq 0.70$ |
| **NPS** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | −100 to +100 | Above **30** = strong loyalty |
| **Top-Box Score** | $TBS = \frac{n_{9}}{N} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | % rating "9 - Like Extremely" |
| **Penalty Analysis (Mean)** | $\Delta J = \bar{H}_{JAR} - \bar{H}_{overall}$ | Hedonic points | Positive = attribute drag |
| **Sample Size (Z-method)** | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Respondents | For 95% CI, p=0.5, e=0.05 → n=385 |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Consumer panel consistency check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Sample Size:**
> For consumer studies under KTU 2024 lab protocols, the minimum sample size is **n = 100** for Central Location Tests (CLT) and **n = 30** per cell for sensory discrimination tests. The **Z-method formula** above gives the *statistical minimum* for survey research.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Consumer Behaviour Insight Used |
|----------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Fortified Food Design** (e.g., iron-fortified rice) | Cultural acceptance studies in tribal belts |
| **Plant-Based Meat** (e.g., GoodDot, Imagine Foods) | Psychological barriers ("real meat" perception) |
| **Functional Beverages** (probiotic drinks) | Health-belief model application |
| **Packaging Design** | Visual perception + shelf-eye-tracking studies |
| **Pricing Strategy** | Reference price psychology (₹10 / ₹20 / ₹99 thresholds) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Sample Size Formula

This derivation is required for the KTU 2024 Statistics module and is a high-frequency exam derivation.

### Step 1: The Standard Normal Confidence Statement

We want the sample mean $\bar{x}$ to be within $\pm e$ of the population mean $\mu$ with confidence level $(1 - \alpha)$.

$$P(\vert \bar{x} - \mu \vert \leq e) = 1 - \alpha$$

### Step 2: Standardize Using the Central Limit Theorem

The sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal with standard error:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Standardizing the probability statement:

$$P\left(\left\vert \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \right\vert \leq \frac{e \sqrt{n}}{\sigma}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

### Step 3: Introduce the Z-Statistic

The bracketed term follows the standard normal distribution, so we use $Z_{1-\alpha/2}$:

$$P(-Z_{1-\alpha/2} \leq Z \leq Z_{1-\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alpha$$

Equating the two probability statements:

$$\frac{e \sqrt{n}}{\sigma} = Z_{1-\alpha/2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for n

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{e^2 \cdot n}{\sigma^2} = Z^2_{1-\alpha/2}$$

Isolating $n$:

$$n = \frac{Z^2_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma^2}{e^2}$$

### Step 5: Substitute the Worst-Case Variance

Since $p(1-p) \leq 0.25$ (maximum at $p = 0.5$), and $\sigma^2$ is unknown for a proportion:

$$\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$$

The final **conservative estimator** becomes:

$$n = \frac{Z^2_{1-\alpha/2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Evaluation for KTU Standard

For 95% confidence, $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $e = 0.05$:

$$n = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.25}{0.0025}$$

$$n = \frac{0.9604}{0.0025}$$

$$n = 384.16$$

Rounded up (ceiling rule for sample size):

$$n = 385 \text{ respondents}$$

This is the universally accepted minimum sample size for consumer survey research.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Consumer Testing Methods

| Method | Type of Data | Sample Size | Output Metric | KTU Application |
|--------|--------------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Focus Group Discussion (FGD)** | Qualitative | 8–12 per group | Themes, perceptions | Concept screening |
| **In-Depth Interview (IDI)** | Qualitative | 20–30 | Latent needs | Ethnographic studies |
| **Central Location Test (CLT)** | Quantitative | 100–300 | Hedonic score, PI | Prototype refinement |
| **Home-Use Test (HUT)** | Quantitative | 50–150 per cell | Repeat purchase intent | Pre-launch validation |
| **Conjoint Analysis** | Quantitative | 150–500 | Part-worth utilities | Optimal product configuration |
| **Eye-Tracking / Shelf Test** | Quantitative + Behavioral | 50–100 | Dwell time, fixation count | Packaging design |
| **Social Listening (Netnography)** | Qualitative + Quantitative | Thousands | Sentiment, NPS proxy | Trend forecasting |

---

## 3.3 Penalty Analysis — Worked Example

**Scenario:** A KTU lab is testing a new mango drink. The overall mean hedonic score is 7.2/9. For the attribute "Sweetness," the JAR (Just-About-Right) panel gives:

- "Much too sweet" → $\bar{H} = 5.8$
- "Too sweet" → $\bar{H} = 6.5$
- "JAR" → $\bar{H} = 7.4$
- "Not sweet enough" → $\bar{H} = 6.9$
- "Much too sweet" → $\bar{H} = 5.5$

**Step 1: Mean of "Too Sweet" group (penalty candidates):**

$$\bar{H}_{penalty} = \frac{5.8 + 6.5 + 5.5}{3} = \frac{17.8}{3} = 5.93$$

**Step 2: Compute the Penalty:**

$$\Delta J = \bar{H}_{JAR} - \bar{H}_{penalty}$$

$$\Delta J = 7.4 - 5.93 = 1.47 \text{ hedonic points}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (KTU threshold):**

> Penalty $> 1.0$ indicates that **reducing sweetness** could improve overall liking by ~1.5 points — a significant reformulation directive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule (KTU 2024):**
> If $\Delta J \geq 1.0$ for any attribute, that attribute is the **priority reformulation target**.

---

## 3.4 Net Promoter Score (NPS) Calculation — Worked Example

**Survey Data from 100 consumers of a new probiotic yogurt:**

- **Promoters** (rating 9–10): 55 consumers
- **Passives** (rating 7–8): 30 consumers
- **Detractors** (rating 0–6): 15 consumers

**Step 1: Compute Percentages:**

$$\%P = \frac{55}{100} \times 100 = 55\%$$

$$\%D = \frac{15}{100} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 2: Apply the NPS Formula:**

$$NPS = \%P - \%D$$

$$NPS = 55 - 15 = 40$$

**Step 3: KTU Benchmark Interpretation:**

> An NPS of **40** places the product in the **"Great"** category (industry benchmark: > 30). The product is **launch-ready** from a consumer-loyalty perspective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Consumer Behaviour Decision-Making Process (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus: Need Recognition] --> B[Information Search]
    B --> C1[Internal: Memory Recall]
    B --> C2[External: Reviews, Ads, Labels]
    C1 --> D[Evaluation of Alternatives]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Purchase Decision]
    E --> F[Post-Purchase Behaviour]
    F --> G1[Cognitive Dissonance]
    F --> G2[Satisfaction]
    F --> G3[Word-of-Mouth]
    G1 --> H[Return / Reformulate]
    G2 --> I[Repurchase / Loyalty]
    G3 --> J[Affects Other Consumers]
    H --> A
    I --> A
    J --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:**
> The diagram forms a **closed-loop feedback system** — the post-purchase phase is not the end, but the **trigger** for the next purchase cycle. This is the foundation of the **Stage-Gate consumer review** in product development.

---

## 4.2 Role of Consumer in the Food Product Development Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Idea Generation] --> P2[Stage 2: Concept Screening]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Product Development]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Market Testing]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Launch]

    C1((Consumer: FGD, Ethnography)) --> P1
    C2((Consumer: CLT, JAR Test)) --> P2
    C3((Consumer: HUT, PI Test)) --> P3
    C4((Consumer: Beta Test, NPS)) --> P4
    C5((Consumer: Social Listening)) --> P5
    P5 --> C1

    style C1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** At *every single stage* of food product development, a **distinct consumer research method** is applied. The orange nodes represent the consumer's intervention points.

---

## 4.3 The 4-Factor Influence Model on Consumer Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CB[Consumer Behaviour Outcome: PURCHASE]

    F1[Cultural Factors]
    F2[Social Factors]
    F3[Personal Factors]
    F4[Psychological Factors]

    S1[Culture and Subculture]
    S2[Reference Groups and Family]
    S3[Age, Lifestyle, Income]
    S4[Motivation, Perception, Learning]

    F1 --> CB
    F2 --> CB
    F3 --> CB
    F4 --> CB

    S1 --> F1
    S2 --> F2
    S3 --> F3
    S4 --> F4

    style CB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#ADD8E6
    style F2 fill:#ADD8E6
    style F3 fill:#ADD8E6
    style F4 fill:#ADD8E6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:**
> The **4 P's of Marketing** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) operate *through* these 4 consumer-influence factors. The product developer must engineer the **4 P's** to align with the **4 C's of Consumer**.

---

## 4.4 Diffusion of Innovation Curve — Adoption Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Innovators 2.5 percent] --> I2[Early Adopters 13.5 percent]
    I2 --> I3[Early Majority 34 percent]
    I3 --> I4[Late Majority 34 percent]
    I4 --> I5[Laggards 16 percent]

    style I1 fill:#FF6347
    style I2 fill:#FFA500
    style I3 fill:#FFD700
    style I4 fill:#9ACD32
    style I5 fill:#4682B4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Market Strategy (KTU Module 2):**
> Indian FMCG companies like **ITC** and **Britannia** target the **Innovators + Early Adopters (16%)** in metro cities for the first 6 months of a new food product launch. Penetration beyond 30% indicates successful **crossing-the-chasm** into the Early Majority segment.

---

## 4.5 Consumer Behaviour as a Feedback Control System (Engineering Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired State: Hunger Satisfied] --> B[Sensory Input]
    B --> C[Brain: Decision Unit]
    D[Food Product Stimulus] --> C
    C --> E[Output: Purchase and Consumption]
    E --> F[Feedback: Satiety Score]
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Analogy:**
> This is structurally identical to a **closed-loop control system** from control theory. Hunger = Set Point, Satiety Feedback = Error Signal, Food Product = Control Input, Decision = Controller Output. The product developer acts as the **control system engineer** tuning the product's sensory, nutritional, and price signals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
**Define consumer behaviour and list any four factors influencing it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Consumer behaviour** is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of products, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy their needs and wants. In food product design, it helps developers understand consumer preferences, decision-making, and post-purchase satisfaction.

> **Four influencing factors:**
> 1. **Cultural factors** — culture, subculture, and social class shape food preferences (e.g., regional cuisine).
> 2. **Social factors** — reference groups and family influence brand choice.
> 3. **Personal factors** — age, lifestyle, income, and personality affect purchase.
> 4. **Psychological factors** — motivation, perception, learning, and beliefs drive the final decision.

> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four factors — 0.5 each)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**What is the role of a consumer in product development? List any three roles.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The consumer plays an **active role** in the food product development process, not a passive one. Their role includes:

> 1. **Need Identifier** — Articulating unmet needs through surveys, complaints, or ethnographic research.
> 2. **Concept Tester** — Evaluating product prototypes through focus groups, central location tests, and home-use tests.
> 3. **Final Validator** — Confirming purchase intent and providing post-launch feedback that drives line extensions and reformulations.

> *(1 mark for introduction, 2 marks for three roles — 0.66 each, rounded appropriately)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Consumer Decision-Making Process (CDP)** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the **Engel–Blackwell–Miniard Model** and its stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Howard–Sheth Model** of consumer behaviour. Differentiate between **Extensive, Limited, and Routinized** problem-solving behaviour with food-industry examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a): The Consumer Decision-Making Process

**Step 1: Introduction (1 Mark)**
The Consumer Decision-Making Process (CDP) is a structured sequence of mental and physical stages a consumer undergoes before, during, and after a purchase. The **Engel–Blackwell–Miniard (EBM) Model** (1968, revised 2001) is the most cited framework in food marketing.

**Step 2: The Five Stages (5 Marks, 1 mark each)**

1. **Need Recognition** — The consumer perceives a gap between actual and desired state. *Food example:* A working mother notices her child's lunch box lacks variety.

2. **Information Search** — Internal (memory) and external (ads, reviews, sampling). *Food example:* Searching online for "healthy kids' snacks India."

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** — Comparison on attributes (price, taste, nutrition, brand). *Food example:* Comparing 5 brands of fruit-based snacks.

4. **Purchase Decision** — Actual point of purchase. *Food example:* Choosing the 200g pack at ₹99 from a local store.

5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour** — Satisfaction, cognitive dissonance, or complaint. *Food example:* The child rejects the taste → mother writes a negative review online.

**Step 3: Diagram (1 Mark)**

```
Need → Info Search → Alt Evaluation → Purchase → Post-Purchase
  ↑                                                          ↓
  └──────────── Feedback Loop ─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 Mark)**
The EBM Model is **iterative**, not linear. Post-purchase satisfaction is the new input to the next cycle, making it a **closed-loop consumer-driven system** essential to food product success.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b): The Howard–Sheth Model

**Step 1: Introduction (2 Marks)**
The **Howard–Sheth Model** (1969) explains consumer decision-making as a function of **inputs, perception, learning, and outputs**. It identifies three levels of consumer decision involvement:

**Step 2: Three Levels of Problem-Solving (4.5 Marks, 1.5 each)**

1. **Extensive Problem Solving (EPS)** — Used when the consumer has no prior experience.
   - *Food example:* First-time purchase of a plant-based meat analogue. The consumer needs maximum information, comparison, and trial.
   - *Time spent:* High; *information sources:* Multiple (online, peer, expert).

2. **Limited Problem Solving (LPS)** — Used for new brands within a familiar category.
   - *Food example:* Switching from *Amul* butter to a regional brand.
   - *Time spent:* Moderate; consumer evaluates a few alternatives.

3. **Routinized Response Behaviour (RRB)** — Used for habitual, low-involvement purchases.
   - *Food example:* Daily purchase of *Parle-G* or standard *Maggi* noodles.
   - *Time spent:* Negligible; *consumer behaviour:* Automatic.

**Step 3: KTU Conclusion (0.5 Mark)**
The food industry targets **RRB for mature products**, **LPS for line extensions**, and **EPS for genuine innovation** (e.g., cell-cultured meat in India).

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **four factors influencing consumer behaviour** with examples from the Indian food industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Diffusion of Innovation** theory? Explain the **five categories of adopters** and their relevance to launching a new food product in India. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a): Factors Influencing Consumer Behaviour

**Step 1: Introduction (1 Mark)**
Consumer behaviour is influenced by a multi-layered set of factors categorized by **Philip Kotler** into four major groups.

**Step 2: The Four Factors (5 Marks, 1.25 each)**

1. **Cultural Factors** — The most fundamental influencers. Include culture (e.g., Kerala's preference for coconut-based foods), subculture (regional cuisine), and social class (urban premium vs. rural value).
   - *Indian example:* The south Indian preference for rice-based breakfast vs. the north Indian wheat-based preference.

2. **Social Factors** — Reference groups, family, roles, and status. Family is the strongest social influence on food choice.
   - *Indian example:* A mother's brand loyalty for *Horlicks* passed across generations.

3. **Personal Factors** — Age, life stage, occupation, lifestyle, personality, and economic situation.
   - *Indian example:* The rise of *MyFitness* and *Yoga Bar* driven by millennial health-lifestyle changes.

4. **Psychological Factors** — Motivation (Maslow's hierarchy), perception, learning, and beliefs.
   - *Indian example:* The belief that "turmeric milk boosts immunity" drives demand for *haldi doodh* premixes.

**Step 3: KTU Conclusion (1 Mark)**
A food product developer must align the **4 P's** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) of marketing with the **4 C's** (Culture, Community, Convenience, Communication) of consumer behaviour to ensure success.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b): Diffusion of Innovation

**Step 1: Introduction (1 Mark)**
The **Diffusion of Innovation Theory** (Everett Rogers, 1962) describes how, why, and at what rate new products spread through a population. The adoption follows a **normal (bell) curve** with five distinct adopter categories.

**Step 2: The Five Adopter Categories (5 Marks, 1 each)**

1. **Innovators (2.5%)** — Venturesome, risk-takers. Try exotic foods (insect protein, lab-grown meat). *Strategy:* Make product available in select premium outlets.

2. **Early Adopters (13.5%)** — Opinion leaders, socially connected. Trigger the *crossing-the-chasm* phase. *Strategy:* Use influencers and health professionals for endorsement.

3. **Early Majority (34%)** — Deliberate, careful. Wait for peer validation. *Strategy:* Mass sampling and testimonials.

4. **Late Majority (34%)** — Skeptical, traditional. Adopt only after majority use. *Strategy:* Heavy promotions, free samples, price-offs.

5. **Laggards (16%)** — Tradition-bound. Adopt only when no alternative exists. *Strategy:* Focus on necessity, not innovation.

**Step 3: Indian Launch Strategy (1 Mark)**
For a new functional beverage in India, the **first 16% target is in metros (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi)**. Penetration beyond 30% requires a **tier-2 city expansion plan** (Kochi, Coimbatore, Indore).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing only 2-3 factors** in the "4-factor model" question — KTU deducts marks for incomplete answers. **Always list all 4.**
> 2. **Confusing CDP with Howard–Sheth Model** — CDP is a *sequential process*; Howard–Sheth is a *level of involvement* model. Mixing them up costs 3-4 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions** — KTU 2024 explicitly awards **1-2 marks** for a labelled diagram. Always include one.
> 4. **Not converting percentages correctly** in NPS / TOMA questions — show the calculation steps.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the sample size assumption** in the Z-formula derivation — examiners expect $p = 0.5$ to be explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Writing the diffusion curve in any sequence other than chronological** — the order Innovators → Laggards is **fixed** and frequently tested.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Consumer Behaviour**
> - ✅ **Definition**: Consumer behaviour = how individuals/groups select, buy, use, dispose.
> - ✅ **Consumer's 5 Roles**: Need Identifier → Idea Source → Concept Tester → Final Validator → Feedback Loop.
> - ✅ **CDP Stages**: Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation → Purchase → Post-Purchase.
> - ✅ **EBM Model**: Sequential, iterative, closed-loop.
> - ✅ **Howard–Sheth Model**: EPS → LPS → RRB (involvement levels).
> - ✅ **4 Influencing Factors**: Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological.
> - ✅ **Diffusion of Innovation**: 2.5 / 13.5 / 34 / 34 / 16 (Innovators → Laggards).
> - ✅ **NPS Formula**: $\%P - \%D$, benchmark > 30.
> - ✅ **Penalty Threshold**: $\Delta J \geq 1.0$ = reformulation priority.
> - ✅ **Sample Size Formula**: $n = \frac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$, 95% CI, p=0.5, e=0.05 → n = 385.
> - ✅ **9-point Hedonic Scale**: Industry standard for consumer acceptance.
> - ✅ **JAR Scale**: 5-point bipolar for attribute optimization.
> - ✅ **KTU Industrial Cases**: Maggi (line extensions), Britannia (cultural localization), ITC (rural diffusion).
> - ✅ **Test Markets in India**: Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi, Kochi.
> - ✅ **Post-purchase behaviour = starting point of next CDP cycle**.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍽️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Preference** is defined as the **degree of liking or disliking** exhibited by a consumer toward a specific food, food category, or food attribute. It is a **multi-dimensional affective response** that integrates sensory perception, physiological state, cognitive expectations, cultural conditioning, and past experiences to produce a measurable evaluative judgment.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Food Product Design and Development*, food preferences form the **psychological and behavioral foundation** upon which product conceptualization, formulation, sensory testing, and market positioning are constructed. Without rigorously mapping consumer preferences, no NPD (New Product Development) pipeline can be validated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. When defining "Food Preference," students must explicitly mention its **affective (emotional)** and **cognitive (evaluative)** dual nature — a single-line definition will not fetch full marks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the menu has **40 items**. Within 10 seconds, your eyes scan, your brain filters, and you instinctively narrow it down to **2–3 options** that "feel right." This rapid, almost invisible mental filtering is **food preference in action**.

Think of food preference as a **"mental library card"** 🗂️:

| Mental Library Component | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Stored memories** (childhood foods) | Old books you've read |
| **Cultural rules** (taboos, traditions) | Library access restrictions |
| **Sensory expectations** (taste, smell) | Book cover and title |
| **Physiological signals** (hunger, satiety) | Current mood while browsing |
| **Social context** (peer influence) | Friends' recommendations |

Just as a librarian helps you find the right book by understanding your past reading history, a **food product developer** must decode consumer preferences to design products people will instinctively pick up off the shelf.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature

- **9-Point Hedonic Scale** — the **gold standard** in preference testing
- **JAR (Just-About-Right) Scale** — measures attribute optimization
- **CATA (Check-All-That-Apply)** — qualitative attribute mapping
- **Preference Mapping (PrefMap)** — multivariate projection
- **Hedonic Rating**: numerical value on an ordinal scale
- **Degree of Liking**: consumer's affective score

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **9-Point Hedonic Scale** ranges: `1 = Dislike Extremely` → `9 = Like Extremely`. The midpoint `5 = Neither Like nor Dislike` is critical for statistical analysis in KTU lab-based questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Analytical Framework

## 2.1 Hierarchical Framework of Food Preference Formation

Food preferences are not random — they emerge from a **layered hierarchy** of influences. KTU examiners frequently test this structure.

### **Layer 1 — Innate / Biological Foundations**
- **Genetic taste sensitivity** (e.g., PROP — 6-n-propylthiouracil bitterness sensitivity)
- **Sweet preference** in newborns (universal, biological)
- **Bitter avoidance** (evolutionary protective mechanism against toxins)
- **Umami recognition** (savory protein-seeking behavior)

### **Layer 2 — Physiological / Homeostatic Drivers**
- **Hunger–satiety cycle** (ghrelin, leptin hormonal regulation)
- **Nutrient-specific appetite** (sodium appetite, protein leveraging)
- **Allostatic state** (chronic stress alters preference toward energy-dense foods)
- **Age-related shifts** (children prefer sweet; elderly prefer less intense flavors)

### **Layer 3 — Sensory Exposure & Learning**
- **Flavor-flavor learning** (pairing novel food with liked flavor)
- **Flavor-nutrient learning** (postingestive consequences)
- **Conditioned taste aversion** (e.g., nausea after spoiled food)
- **Neophobia → Neophilia progression**

### **Layer 4 — Cognitive & Psychological**
- **Expectation assimilation** (label, branding, color)
- **Halo effect** (organic label → "tastes better")
- **Cognitive dissonance in dietary choices**
- **Mood modulation by food** (comfort eating)

### **Layer 5 — Socio-Cultural & Environmental**
- **Family & peer influence** during childhood
- **Religious dietary rules** (halal, kosher, vegetarian)
- **Cultural identity** (regional cuisines)
- **Marketing, media, and trend cycles**

---

## 2.2 The Triadic Model of Food Acceptance (KTU High-Yield Framework)

KTU examiners favor a three-pillar model:

$$
P_{\text{acceptance}} = f(S, C, E)
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{acceptance}}$ = Predicted preference/acceptance score
- $S$ = **Sensory properties** (taste, aroma, texture, appearance)
- $C$ = **Contextual/Consumption situation** (time, place, mood, company)
- $E$ = **Consumer's individual characteristics** (age, culture, physiology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **not a strictly additive equation** — it is a **functional notation** indicating that preference is an *interaction effect*. Examiners expect students to explain that $S$, $C$, and $E$ **interact multiplicatively**, not just additively.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hedonic Mean** | $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | Average liking score across panel | Statistical analysis of 9-point data |
| **Preference Ratio** | $R_p = \frac{N_{\text{likers}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Proportion of consumers preferring sample | Market validation |
| **Penalty Analysis (JAR)** | $\Delta = \bar{H}_{\text{JAR}} - \bar{H}_{\text{non-JAR}}$ | Drop in liking when attribute deviates from JAR | Product optimization |
| **Preference Map Equation** | $Y_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{ik} X_{jk} + \epsilon_{ij}$ | External preference mapping regression | Linking sensory to liking |
| **Triangle Test Threshold** | $p < 0.05$ for $\sqrt{n} \geq 1.96$ | Minimum panelists to detect difference | Sensory discrimination |
| **NPD Funnel** | $N_{\text{concept}} \rightarrow N_{\text{launch}} \approx 1:100$ | Idea-to-launch conversion | Commercialization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse** *discrimination tests* (triangle, duo-trio) with *preference/acceptance tests* (hedonic, JAR). KTU question 8(a) from past papers has repeatedly tested this distinction.

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Food Product Development

A product development scientist at a company like **Nestlé or Britannia** must answer:

1. *Will the consumer choose this over the existing product?* → **Preference testing**
2. *Which sensory attribute is driving acceptance/rejection?* → **Descriptive analysis + preference mapping**
3. *Does the consumer's culture allow this product?* → **Cross-cultural preference studies**
4. *How does packaging/label change preference?* → **Cognitive-contextual studies**

This makes food preference a **cross-functional knowledge area** bridging Sensory Science, Consumer Psychology, and Marketing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Penalty Analysis Demonstration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following demonstrates a **Penalty Analysis on JAR data** — a standard KTU 14-mark analytical question. Every step is shown without truncation.

---

## 3.1 Sample Problem (As Seen in KTU ESE Pattern)

> A product development team tests a new **low-sodium soup** with **75 consumers** using a 9-point hedonic scale. They also ask whether **saltiness is "Just-About-Right" (JAR)** or "Too Weak"/"Too Strong." From the data below, perform a **Penalty Analysis** and recommend formulation changes.

| Group | Count ($n$) | Mean Hedonic Score ($\bar{H}$) |
|---|---|---|
| JAR (saltiness = optimal) | 45 | 7.8 |
| Too Weak | 20 | 6.0 |
| Too Strong | 10 | 5.5 |

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Define the Penalty

The **penalty** for each non-JAR segment is the drop in mean liking relative to the JAR segment.

$$
P_{\text{weak}} = \bar{H}_{\text{JAR}} - \bar{H}_{\text{weak}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{strong}} = \bar{H}_{\text{JAR}} - \bar{H}_{\text{strong}}
$$

**Textual logic:** We measure how much *happiness* (hedonic score) is lost when the consumer feels the saltiness is *not* just right. The bigger the penalty, the bigger the formulation problem.

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
P_{\text{weak}} = 7.8 - 6.0 = 1.8
$$

$$
P_{\text{strong}} = 7.8 - 5.5 = 2.3
$$

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Compute Mean Drops (Sign Convention)

We typically express penalty as a **positive number** (drop magnitude) and assign a **direction**:

- $P_{\text{weak}} = +1.8$ → loss when product is *under*-salted
- $P_{\text{strong}} = +2.3$ → loss when product is *over*-salted

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Weighted Penalty (Advanced)

If the examiner asks for **weighted penalty** (penalized by proportion of consumers in each non-JAR group):

$$
\text{Weighted Penalty} = \sum_{k} \left( \frac{n_k}{N} \right) \cdot P_k
$$

Where $N = 75$, $k \in \{\text{weak, strong}\}$.

$$
\text{Weighted Penalty} = \left( \frac{20}{75} \right)(1.8) + \left( \frac{10}{75} \right)(2.3)
$$

$$
= (0.2667)(1.8) + (0.1333)(2.3)
$$

$$
= 0.480 + 0.307
$$

$$
= 0.787
$$

---

## 3.6 Step 5 — Interpretation & Recommendation

| Insight | Data-Driven Conclusion |
|---|---|
| **Larger penalty** | $P_{\text{strong}} = 2.3 > P_{\text{weak}} = 1.8$ |
| **Direction of change** | Over-saltiness causes *more* consumer dissatisfaction than under-saltiness |
| **Formulation action** | **Reduce sodium content** in next iteration |
| **Proportion check** | 20/75 (27%) find it weak — investigate whether sub-segment has different salt expectations (e.g., elderly vs. young) |

---

## 3.7 Summary of Key Formulas Used

$$
\boxed{ P_k = \bar{H}_{\text{JAR}} - \bar{H}_k }
$$

$$
\boxed{ \text{Weighted Penalty} = \sum_k \left( \frac{n_k}{N} \right) P_k }
$$

$$
\boxed{ \bar{H} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i }
$$

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark KTU questions, always show: (1) formula, (2) substitution, (3) numerical evaluation, (4) interpretation, (5) recommendation. Skipping interpretation loses 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    SBlock["SENSORY INPUT BLOCK\nTaste Smell Texture Appearance"] --> Mixer

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series A]**
**Define food preference. List any four factors influencing it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Food preference is the degree of liking or disliking a consumer exhibits toward a specific food item, reflecting an **affective evaluation** based on sensory, psychological, and sociocultural factors.
- **Four factors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Sensory attributes** (taste, texture, aroma)
  2. **Cultural background** (regional, religious food norms)
  3. **Past experience** (childhood exposure, learning)
  4. **Physiological state** (hunger, age, health conditions)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Series B]**
**Differentiate between food preference and food acceptance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Food Preference (1.5 marks):** Refers to the **subjective degree of liking** expressed on a hedonic scale; an *attitudinal* measure of *desire* for a food.
- **Food Acceptance (1.5 marks):** Refers to the **observed/measured consumption behavior** — whether a person actually eats and continues to consume the food. Acceptance is *behavioral* and often measured by intake, frequency, or purchase data.
- **Key distinction:** Preference is *psychological* (wanting); acceptance is *behavioral* (doing). A consumer may prefer a food but not accept it due to price or availability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each module question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternatives are provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Variant]**

**(a) Explain in detail the physiological and psychological factors affecting food preferences.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Physiological Factors (3.5 marks):**
1. **Hunger and satiety hormones** (ghrelin, leptin) drive preference for energy-dense foods when hungry.
2. **Genetic taste sensitivity** (e.g., supertasters for PROP bitterness) shapes individual preferences.
3. **Age-related shifts** — children show strong sweet preference due to higher taste bud density; elderly prefer milder flavors.
4. **Health status** — diabetics may prefer sugar-free variants; hypertensive patients avoid salty foods.
5. **Pregnancy and hormonal state** create cravings (e.g., pickle cravings attributed to sodium need).

**Psychological Factors (3.5 marks):**
1. **Sensory-specific satiety** — preference for a food decreases after repeated consumption in one sitting.
2. **Mood modulation** — stress increases preference for "comfort foods" (high fat, high sugar).
3. **Classical conditioning** — pairing novel food with a liked flavor (e.g., cheese on broccoli) builds preference.
4. **Cognitive expectations** — labels like "organic," "homemade," or "premium" elevate perceived liking (halo effect).
5. **Neophobia** — innate fear of new foods; gradually overcome via repeated exposure (neophilia).

---

**(b) Describe the 9-point hedonic scale and its limitations in food preference testing.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Description of the 9-Point Hedonic Scale (4 marks):**
- Developed by **David Peryam & Francis Pilgrim** at the **U.S. Army Quartermaster Food and Container Institute** in the 1950s.
- Scale endpoints: `1 = Dislike Extremely` and `9 = Like Extremely`, with `5 = Neither Like nor Dislike` as the neutral midpoint.
- Administered to a **consumer panel** (typically 75–150 untrained consumers, not trained sensory panelists).
- Consumers rate their **degree of liking** for a food sample on this **ordinal scale**.
- Data is analyzed using **mean hedonic scores, ANOVA, and post-hoc tests** to compare products.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Scale compression** — consumers avoid extreme ends (central tendency bias).
2. **No diagnostic information** — does not explain *why* a food is liked/disliked (requires descriptive analysis alongside).
3. **Cultural bias** — the verbal anchors may not translate equivalently across languages/cultures.
4. **Hedonic desert phenomenon** — very bland or unfamiliar products cluster around the midpoint.
5. **Order and carry-over effects** — sample presentation order can influence ratings.
6. **Inability to capture individual differences** — a single mean score masks consumer segmentation (requires cluster analysis or external preference mapping).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Variant]**

**(a) Discuss the sociocultural determinants of food preferences with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Family and early socialization (1.5 marks):** Children develop preferences by imitating parents; e.g., acceptance of spicy food in South Indian families.
2. **Religious and ethical norms (1.5 marks):** Halal in Islam, vegetarianism in Hinduism/Jainism, kosher in Judaism — these rules shape acceptable food categories entirely.
3. **Regional and cultural identity (1.5 marks):** Idli-dosa in Tamil Nadu, appam-stew in Kerala — preferences are tied to geographic agricultural produce.
4. **Social class and income (1 mark):** As income rises, preference shifts from staple-based diets to protein-rich, processed, and convenience foods (**Bennett's Law**).
5. **Media, peers, and trends (1 mark):** Influencer-driven trends (e.g., avocado toast, oat milk) reshape preferences in young adults.
6. **Globalization effect (0.5 mark):** Cross-cultural fusion foods (e.g., Indo-Chinese) reflect blended preferences.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Penalty Analysis using a worked example. How does it help in product reformulation?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
- Penalty Analysis is a statistical technique that **quantifies the impact of deviation from the "Just-About-Right" (JAR) point** of a sensory attribute on consumer liking.
- It links **descriptive sensory data** (intensity ratings) with **hedonic liking data** to identify which attribute's deviation is *most damaging* to consumer acceptance.
- The penalty is computed as the **drop in mean hedonic score** for consumers who rate the attribute as non-JAR.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
- *Suppose a yogurt has 60% JAR for sweetness, 25% "Too Weak," 15% "Too Strong."*
- *Mean liking: JAR = 7.5, Too Weak = 6.2, Too Strong = 5.0.*
- *Penalties: P_weak = 7.5 − 6.2 = 1.3; P_strong = 7.5 − 5.0 = 2.5.*
- *Weighted penalty: (0.25)(1.3) + (0.15)(2.5) = 0.325 + 0.375 = 0.70.*

**Reformulation Decision (2 marks):**
- Since $P_{\text{strong}} > P_{\text{weak}}$, over-sweetness is more damaging than under-sweetness.
- **Recommendation:** Reduce added sugar by 5–8% in the next iteration and re-test.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Food Preference Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "preference" with "acceptance"** — these are NOT synonyms. Preference is attitudinal; acceptance is behavioral.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "affective" and "cognitive" components** when defining — the dual nature is mandatory in 2024 Scheme.
> 3. **Skipping the formula in Penalty Analysis** — KTU expects the formula first, then substitution.
> 4. **Listing factors without categorization** — always group them as biological, physiological, psychological, sociocultural.
> 5. **Ignoring the JAR concept** — JAR is a KTU-favorite topic; practice penalty calculations.
> 6. **Writing "9-point" without naming authors (Peryam & Pilgrim)** — fetch 0.5–1 bonus mark if remembered.
> 7. **Confusing "hedonic" with "discrimination" tests** — hedonic = liking; discrimination = difference detection.
> 8. **Not including diagrams in 14-mark answers** — KTU rubric awards 1 mark for "neat labeled diagram."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Food Preference = Degree of liking** (affective + cognitive evaluation).
- ✅ **Difference from Acceptance**: Preference is *attitudinal*; Acceptance is *behavioral* (consumption).
- ✅ **9-Point Hedonic Scale** by **Peryam & Pilgrim (1950s)**; endpoints `1=Dislike Extremely`, `9=Like Extremely`.
- ✅ **Triadic Model**: $P_{\text{acceptance}} = f(S, C, E)$ — Sensory, Context, Consumer interact.
- ✅ **5 Layers of Influence**: Biological → Physiological → Sensory learning → Cognitive/Psychological → Sociocultural.
- ✅ **JAR (Just-About-Right) Scale** used with hedonic to perform **Penalty Analysis**.
- ✅ **Penalty Formula**: $P_k = \bar{H}_{\text{JAR}} - \bar{H}_k$.
- ✅ **Weighted Penalty**: $\sum_k (n_k / N) \cdot P_k$.
- ✅ **CATA** (Check-All-That-Apply) for qualitative preference profiling.
- ✅ **Preference Mapping (PrefMap)** links descriptive sensory data to consumer liking via regression/PCA.
- ✅ **Neophobia** (fear of new foods) is overcome through **repeated exposure**.
- ✅ **Genetic taste sensitivity** (PROP) creates individual variability in bitterness preference.
- ✅ **Bennett's Law**: rising income → preference shift from carbohydrates to animal protein.
- ✅ **Sensory-specific satiety**: liking decreases during a meal for that specific food.
- ✅ **Always cite examples** in 14-mark answers (regional foods, religious restrictions, marketing trends).
- ✅ **Use diagrams** (flowcharts of preference determinants) — KTU awards marks for visual aids.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Avoiding and Acceptance of Consumers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Avoiding and Acceptance of Consumers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Consumer Acceptance** in the context of food product design and development is defined as the **multidimensional attitude** and behavioral response of a consumer towards a food product, measured through liking, preference, purchase intent, and actual consumption. It is the measurable outcome of integrating **sensory perception**, **cognitive evaluation**, **emotional response**, and **post-ingestive feedback**.

**Consumer Avoidance**, conversely, is the deliberate or instinctive rejection of a food product, driven by sensory dislike, food neophobia, negative prior experiences, cultural taboos, health concerns (allergies/intolerances), or perceived risk. In KTU 2024 terminology, avoidance is the **negative pole** of the consumer attitude continuum.

The integrated framework is known as the **Acceptance-Rejection Continuum (ARC) Model**, situating every consumer response between absolute aversion and enthusiastic acceptance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must distinguish between *sensory acceptance* (driven by perception) and *hedonic acceptance* (driven by pleasure/liking). Both are examinable in ESE questions worth up to 14 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **food product as a "blind date"** for the consumer's senses and mind:

- **First Impression (Sensory Cues):** The visual appearance, aroma, and sound of the package are like the "profile picture" — they create the first hook. If the visual cues mismatch expectations (e.g., a green-coloured mango drink), the consumer may "ghost" the product before even tasting it.
- **The Conversation (Taste & Texture):** Once tasted, the flavour, mouthfeel, and aftertaste are like the actual conversation. If it is too bland, too sour, or has an off-texture, the consumer "loses interest."
- **The After-Call Reflection (Post-Ingestive Feedback):** Hours later, the body tells the truth. If the consumer feels bloated, nauseous, or unsatisfied, the product is "blocked" forever.
- **The Cultural Context (Society's Lens):** Even a delicious product may be "rejected by family" if it contains pork in a Muslim household or beef in a Hindu household — the **social circle acts as a powerful gatekeeper**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Definition for Memorization:**
> - **Acceptance = "Yes, I want more of this"**
> - **Avoidance = "No, I will not eat this"**
> - The food technologist's job is to push consumers **rightward** on the acceptance scale through scientific design.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Food Neophobia** | Reluctance to eat or avoid novel foods | **FNS Scale (Food Neophobia Scale): 10–40 points** |
| **Hedonic Rating** | Degree of liking/disliking | **9-point Hedonic Scale (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely)** |
| **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** | Measures intensity of attribute acceptability | **5-point or 7-point bipolar scale** |
| **Purchase Intent** | Likelihood of buying | **5-point or 7-point Likert scale** |
| **CATA (Check-All-That-Apply)** | Descriptive sensory profiling | **Frequency of selection (%)** |
| **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** | Overall consumer advocacy | **Range: −100 to +100** |
| **CATA Penalty Analysis** | Identifies drivers of rejection | **Mean drop in liking (%)** |

> [!NOTE]
> All **KTU 2024 Scheme practicals and viva questions** in food product development often test the 9-point Hedonic Scale and JAR scale — these are **mandatory to memorize**.

---

## 1.4 Course Outcomes (COs) Mapped to this Topic

As per **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**:

- **CO 2 (Module 2):** *Understand the principles of consumer behaviour and sensory evaluation in food product development.*
- **CO 3 (Module 2):** *Apply consumer acceptance testing methods to evaluate new food products.*
- **Bloom's Levels Targeted:** L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tripartite Model of Food Acceptance

Food acceptance is governed by **three integrated pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Sensory Factors (60–70% of variance)
1. **Taste** — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami (the five basic tastes)
2. **Smell (Olfaction)** — retronasal and orthonasal aroma
3. **Texture (Mouthfeel)** — hardness, crunchiness, viscosity, granularity
4. **Appearance (Vision)** — colour, shape, size, gloss, transparency
5. **Sound (Audition)** — crispiness, fizz, sizzle (the "sound of fresh")

### Pillar 2: Psychological / Cognitive Factors
1. **Expectation** — shaped by packaging and advertising (the "Pepsi Paradox")
2. **Familiarity** — repeated exposure reduces neophobia
3. **Branding & Labeling** — health claims, organic, "natural"
4. **Memory of Prior Experience** — conditioned acceptance/aversion
5. **Mood and Context** — emotional state, eating environment

### Pillar 3: Physiological & Health Factors
1. **Hunger state** — hungry consumers are less neophobic
2. **Allergies & Intolerances** — gluten, lactose, nuts, shellfish
3. **Cultural & Religious restrictions** — halal, kosher, vegetarianism
4. **Age & Health conditions** — diabetes, hypertension, geriatric dysphagia
5. **Post-ingestive feedback** — satiety hormones (leptin, ghrelin), gut-brain axis

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework: The Acceptance-Rejection Decision Tree

A consumer's binary outcome (Accept / Avoid) is determined by the following logical chain:

1. **Sensory Gate Check** — Does the product pass the visual + olfactory threshold?
   - **If No** → *Rejection (Sensory Avoidance)*
2. **Cognitive Evaluation** — Does the product meet expectations and familiarity?
   - **If No** → *Rejection (Neophobic Avoidance)*
3. **Cultural / Health Filter** — Is the product permitted by diet, religion, health?
   - **If No** → *Rejection (Cultural/Health Avoidance)*
4. **Hedonic Confirmation** — Does the product deliver pleasure during consumption?
   - **If No** → *Rejection (Hedonic Avoidance)*
5. **Post-Ingestive Reward** — Does the body feel satisfied (no nausea, satiety)?
   - **If No** → *Rejection (Physiological Avoidance — future)*
6. **Final Verdict** — **ACCEPTANCE → Repeat Purchase / Loyalty**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree maps directly to **KTU 14-mark ESE questions** where students are asked to "Discuss the factors influencing consumer acceptance of a new food product."

## 2.3 The 9-Point Hedonic Scale — The Industry Standard

The **9-Point Hedonic Scale** (developed at the University of California, Davis) is the gold standard for measuring consumer acceptance. It is a **psychophysical measurement tool**.

**Anchor Points (Verbal Descriptors):**
- **9** = Like extremely
- **8** = Like very much
- **7** = Like moderately
- **6** = Like slightly
- **5** = Neither like nor dislike
- **4** = Dislike slightly
- **3** = Dislike moderately
- **2** = Dislike very much
- **1** = Dislike extremely

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Metric | Mathematical Form | Description | Units |
|------------------|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| **Mean Hedonic Score** | $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | Average liking across panelists | dimensionless (1–9) |
| **% Acceptance Rate** | $A\% = \frac{N_{acc}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Proportion scoring ≥ 6 | percentage (%) |
| **% Rejection Rate** | $R\% = \frac{N_{rej}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Proportion scoring ≤ 4 | percentage (%) |
| **Purchase Intent (PI)** | $PI = \frac{\sum (f_i \times s_i)}{N \times s_{max}}$ | Weighted likelihood | dimensionless (0–1) |
| **NPS** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Brand loyalty | dimensionless (−100 to +100) |
| **Top-Box Score** | $TB = \frac{N_{score=9}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % of "Like extremely" | percentage (%) |
| **CATA Penalty** | $P_j = \bar{L}_{all} - \bar{L}_{j,used}$ | Drop in liking due to attribute j | 0–9 scale |
| **FNS Score Range** | $FNS \in [10, 40]$ | Pliner & Hobden (1992) scale | points |
| **Signal Detection Theory (d′)** | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | Sensory sensitivity (Hit − False Alarm) | dimensionless (z-scores) |
| **Weber's Law (JND)** | \vert \Delta I / I \vert = k | Just-Noticeable Difference | dimensionless ratio |
| **Fechner's Law** | $S = k \cdot \log(I / I_0)$ | Perceived intensity vs. stimulus | subjective units (sone) |
| **Stevens' Power Law** | $\Psi(I) = k \cdot I^n$ | Psychophysical scaling | magnitude estimate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "calculate acceptance rate," always show the formula symbolically **before** substituting numbers. Marks are awarded for **methodology**, not just the final answer.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

| Application Area | Use of Acceptance Theory | Industry Example |
|-----------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| **New Product Development (NPD)** | Central Location Test (CLT) before launch | Frito-Lay, Nestlé |
| **Quality Assurance** | Consumer panel for batch-to-batch consistency | Dairy industry |
| **Sensory Marketing** | Multisensory branding (sound of chips, colour of soda) | Coca-Cola, KFC |
| **Functional Foods** | Masking bitterness of nutraceuticals | Protein bars, fortified rice |
| **Food for Special Groups** | Texture design for dysphagia patients | Nestlé Resource |
| **Reformulation** | Sugar/sodium reduction without losing acceptance | Unilever Knorr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application:** Module 2 of Food Product Design and Development directly uses this framework in **lab-based product development projects** where students must validate their prototype via consumer panels.

---

## 2.6 Food Neophobia — The Central Avoidance Mechanism

**Food Neophobia** (Greek: *neos* = new, *phobos* = fear) is the **reluctance to consume novel or unfamiliar foods**. It is the **single biggest barrier** to consumer acceptance of new food products.

### Pliner & Hobden (1992) FNS Scale — The 10 Items
The FNS consists of 10 statements rated on a **7-point scale (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree)**:
- *I am constantly sampling new and different foods.* (reverse-scored)
- *I don't trust new foods.*
- *If I don't know what is in a food, I won't try it.*
- *I am afraid to eat things I have never had before.*
- *I am very particular about the foods I will eat.*
- *I will eat almost anything.* (reverse-scored)
- *I like to try new ethnic restaurants.*
- *I am reluctant to try new food products from other countries.*
- *I am very cautious about what foods I eat.*
- *I will eat almost any kind of food.* (reverse-scored)

**Total Score Range: 10 – 40**
- **10–20:** Neophilic (low neophobia, food adventurous)
- **21–30:** Neutral
- **31–40:** Neophobic (high avoidance of novel foods)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5–10 Exposure Rule:** A novel food typically requires **5 to 10 positive exposures** to overcome neophobia in children, and **3 to 5 exposures** in adults. This is a frequently-tested KTU fact.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Mean Hedonic Score Calculation

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A food company tested a new **mango-flavoured probiotic drink** with **60 consumer panelists**. The frequency of hedonic scores obtained is given in the table below. Calculate the **(a) Mean Hedonic Score**, **(b) Acceptance Rate (% scoring ≥ 6)**, **(c) Rejection Rate (% scoring ≤ 4)**, and **(d) Top-Box Score**.

| Hedonic Score ($H_i$) | Verbal Descriptor | Frequency ($f_i$) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 9 | Like extremely | 8 |
| 8 | Like very much | 14 |
| 7 | Like moderately | 12 |
| 6 | Like slightly | 9 |
| 5 | Neither like nor dislike | 6 |
| 4 | Dislike slightly | 4 |
| 3 | Dislike moderately | 3 |
| 2 | Dislike very much | 3 |
| 1 | Dislike extremely | 1 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a): Mean Hedonic Score ($\bar{H}$)

The mean hedonic score is the sum of (score × frequency) divided by total panelists.

$$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{9} (H_i \times f_i)}{N}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the numerator $\sum (H_i \times f_i)$ by expanding each term:

$$\sum (H_i \times f_i) = (9 \times 8) + (8 \times 14) + (7 \times 12) + (6 \times 9) + (5 \times 6) + (4 \times 4) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) + (1 \times 1)$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each product term explicitly:

$$\sum (H_i \times f_i) = 72 + 112 + 84 + 54 + 30 + 16 + 9 + 6 + 1$$

**Step 3:** Sum all the products:

$$\sum (H_i \times f_i) = 384$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the formula. Total panelists $N = 60$:

$$\bar{H} = \frac{384}{60}$$

**Step 5:** Final division:

$$\bar{H} = 6.40$$

**Interpretation:** A mean score of **6.40** ("Like slightly" to "Like moderately") indicates **borderline acceptance**. The product is acceptable but not enthusiastically liked.

---

#### Part (b): Acceptance Rate ($A\%$)

Acceptance is defined as scoring **≥ 6** on the hedonic scale.

**Step 1:** Identify the categories that qualify for acceptance. From the table, scores 9, 8, 7, and 6 are the acceptance region:

$$\text{Accepted panelists} = f_9 + f_8 + f_7 + f_6 = 8 + 14 + 12 + 9 = 43$$

**Step 2:** Apply the acceptance rate formula:

$$A\% = \frac{N_{acc}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$A\% = \frac{43}{60} \times 100$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$A\% = 71.67\%$$

**Interpretation:** **71.67%** of consumers accepted the product. Industry benchmark for commercial launch is **≥ 70%**, so this product **passes the minimum threshold** for launch.

---

#### Part (c): Rejection Rate ($R\%$)

Rejection is defined as scoring **≤ 4** on the hedonic scale.

**Step 1:** Sum the frequencies of scores 4, 3, 2, and 1:

$$\text{Rejected panelists} = f_4 + f_3 + f_2 + f_1 = 4 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$R\% = \frac{11}{60} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$R\% = 18.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** **18.33%** of consumers disliked the product. A rejection rate of **>15%** indicates that **reformulation may be needed** before scaling up.

---

#### Part (d): Top-Box Score ($TB$)

The top-box score is the percentage of panelists rating the product **"Like extremely" (score = 9)**.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$TB = \frac{N_{score=9}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$TB = \frac{8}{60} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$TB = 13.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only **13.33%** of consumers rated it as "Like extremely." For a flagship product, the target is **≥ 25%**. This is a **major signal to the product developer** that sensory optimization is required.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Food Neophobia Scale (FNS) Scoring

**Problem:** A 32-year-old homemaker from Kerala completed the FNS questionnaire. Her responses are:

| Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:---:|
| 6  | 3  | 5  | 4  | 2  | 6  | 2  | 5  | 3  | 6  |

*Note: Q1, Q6, Q7, Q10 are reverse-scored.*

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify reverse-scored items and convert them using $R_i = 8 - x_i$ (since 7-point scale, formula is $8 - x$):

$$Q1_{rev} = 8 - 6 = 2$$
$$Q6_{rev} = 8 - 6 = 2$$
$$Q7_{rev} = 8 - 2 = 6$$
$$Q10_{rev} = 8 - 6 = 2$$

**Step 2:** Sum all 10 items (4 reversed + 6 direct):

$$FNS = (2 + 3 + 5 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 6 + 5 + 3 + 2)$$

**Step 3:** Sum:

$$FNS = 34$$

**Step 4:** Classify based on the standard FNS interpretation:

- Score 34 lies in the range **31–40**, which is classified as **High Food Neophobia**.

**Interpretation:** This consumer is highly neophobic and is likely to **avoid the new probiotic drink**. The product developer must design **familiar-flavour variants** (e.g., traditional mango-pulisseri or tender coconut) to overcome her neophobia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application:** When introducing functional foods to neophobic populations (children, elderly, traditionalists), use **familiar flavour profiles + small portion sizes + 5–10 repeated exposures**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect of Avoidance

| Avoidance Trigger | Sensory Domain | Psychological Mechanism | Engineering/Design Solution |
|-------------------|----------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Bitter taste** | Taste (T2R receptors) | Innate rejection (toxicity signal) | Bitterness masking via encapsulation, sugar/acid balance |
| **Off-odour (rancid)** | Olfactory (retro/orthonasal) | "Something is spoiled" — survival instinct | Antioxidant addition, MAP packaging, O₂ scavengers |
| **Unfamiliar colour** | Visual | Neophobia / cultural mismatch | Natural colourants aligned with food identity (e.g., yellow for mango) |
| **Hard texture** | Mechanoreceptor | Difficulty in mastication | Hydrocolloids, tenderization, controlled hydration |
| **Religious restriction** | Cognitive/Cultural | Ethical avoidance | Halal/Kosher/Jain certification, clean labelling |
| **Allergen presence** | Physiological | Health risk perception | Allergen-free formulation, front-of-pack warnings |
| **Negative prior experience** | Memory | Conditioned aversion | Sensory rebranding, gradual re-introduction |
| **High price** | Economic | Perceived low value | Cost engineering, value-size optimization |
| **Complex ingredient list** | Cognitive | "Ultra-processed" perception | Clean label, ≤5 ingredients |

---

## 3.4 KTU 2024 Numerical Problem: Purchase Intent (PI) Calculation

**Problem:** A new **high-protein atta (wheat flour)** was tested with 100 consumers. Each rated their purchase intent on a **5-point scale** (1 = Definitely will not buy, 5 = Definitely will buy). The frequency table is:

| Score ($s_i$) | Frequency ($f_i$) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 5 | 25 |
| 4 | 30 |
| 3 | 20 |
| 2 | 15 |
| 1 | 10 |

Calculate the **weighted Purchase Intent (PI)**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the formula:

$$PI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} (f_i \times s_i)}{N \times s_{max}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator $\sum (f_i \times s_i)$:

$$\sum (f_i \times s_i) = (25 \times 5) + (30 \times 4) + (20 \times 3) + (15 \times 2) + (10 \times 1)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each product:

$$\sum (f_i \times s_i) = 125 + 120 + 60 + 30 + 10 = 345$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator $N \times s_{max} = 100 \times 5 = 500$.

**Step 5:** Substitute and evaluate:

$$PI = \frac{345}{500} = 0.69$$

**Step 6:** Convert to percentage:

$$PI = 69\%$$

**Interpretation:** A **Purchase Intent of 69%** is considered **strong** (industry threshold ≥ 60% for launch). The product has **good commercial potential** if priced and distributed appropriately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary (for KTU valuation):**
> - Each frequency is multiplied by its score → **frequency-weighting** method.
> - The denominator normalizes to the **maximum possible score** (5), giving a value in [0, 1].
> - Multiplying by 100 converts to a **percentage scale** for easier commercial interpretation.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix: Acceptance vs. Avoidance Drivers

| Dimension | Acceptance Driver | Avoidance Driver |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Sensory** | Pleasant taste, aroma, texture, colour | Off-flavour, weird texture, off-colour |
| **Cognitive** | Familiarity, brand trust, expectations | Novelty, uncertainty, neophobia |
| **Cultural** | Aligned with food identity | Violates religious/social norms |
| **Health** | Nutritious, functional, safe | Allergenic, harmful, contaminated |
| **Economic** | Affordable, value-for-money | Expensive, low perceived value |
| **Emotional** | Comfort, nostalgia, reward | Fear, disgust, anxiety |
| **Contextual** | Right time, place, company | Inappropriate setting |
| **Post-Ingestive** | Satiety, energy, well-being | Bloating, nausea, sickness |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Consumer Acceptance-Avoidance Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Consumer Encounter] --> B[Visual Inspection of Package]
    B --> C{Visual Appeal OK?}
    C -- No --> D1[REJECT Avoidance 1: Visual]
    C -- Yes --> E[Olfactory Check Aroma]
    E --> F{Aroma Acceptable?}
    F -- No --> D2[REJECT Avoidance 2: Olfactory]
    F -- Yes --> G[First Taste and Bite]
    G --> H{Taste and Texture OK?}
    H -- No --> D3[REJECT Avoidance 3: Sensory Hedonic]
    H -- Yes --> I{Cultural and Health Filter Passed?}
    I -- No --> D4[REJECT Avoidance 4: Cultural or Health]
    I -- Yes --> J[Continued Consumption]
    J --> K{Post Ingestive Feedback Positive?}
    K -- No --> D5[REJECT Future Avoidance 5: Physiological]
    K -- Yes --> L[FULL ACCEPTANCE Purchase Intent Loyal]
    D1 --> M[Avoidance Decision]
    D2 --> M
    D3 --> M
    D4 --> M
    D5 --> M
    L --> N[Acceptance Decision]
    M --> O[Output Consumer Behaviour]
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> This decision flow is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that maps how a consumer's brain integrates multi-modal sensory, cognitive, and physiological signals to reach a final accept/reject decision. It can be used as a 7-mark descriptive answer in the KTU ESE.
