# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define consumer acceptance in the context of food product development. Mention any two standard scales used to measure it.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Consumer acceptance is the **multidimensional positive attitude** of a consumer towards a food product, measured through liking, preference, purchase intent, and consumption behaviour. It is the integration of sensory, cognitive, emotional, and post-ingestive responses.

**Two Standard Scales (1.5 Marks):**
1. **9-Point Hedonic Scale** — measures overall liking (1 = dislike extremely to 9 = like extremely).
2. **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** — measures the appropriateness of an attribute's intensity (e.g., sweetness, saltiness, crunchiness).

---

### Q2. What is food neophobia? State the Pliner and Hobden FNS scoring range and its classification.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Food neophobia is the **reluctance or fear of consuming novel or unfamiliar foods**. It is a major barrier to consumer acceptance of new food products.

**FNS Score Range and Classification (1.5 Marks):**
- The FNS scale has **10 items** rated on a **7-point scale**, giving a total score range of **10–40**.
- **10–20:** Neophilic (low neophobia, food-adventurous).
- **21–30:** Neutral.
- **31–40:** Neophobic (high avoidance of novel foods).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q. A (a)** Discuss in detail the various **sensory factors** influencing consumer acceptance of a new food product. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **9-point Hedonic Scale** and its role in consumer testing. Calculate the **mean hedonic score** from the following data of 50 panelists. **(7 Marks)**

| Score | 9 | 8 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Frequency | 6 | 9 | 11 | 8 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 2 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q. B (a)** What is **Food Neophobia**? Discuss the **psychological and cultural factors** that lead to consumer avoidance of food products. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A new fortified biscuit was tested with 40 panelists. The frequency of scores on the 9-point hedonic scale is given below. Calculate the **(i) Mean Hedonic Score**, **(ii) Acceptance Rate (% scoring ≥ 6)**, and **(iii) Purchase Intent (weighted average on a 5-point scale, where scores are mapped from hedonic: 9→5, 7→4, 5→3, 3→2, 1→1)**. **(7 Marks)**

| Hedonic Score | 9 | 7 | 5 | 3 | 1 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Frequency | 10 | 14 | 8 | 5 | 3 |

---

### MODEL SOLUTIONS

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Sensory Factors Influencing Consumer Acceptance (7 Marks):**

1. **Taste (2 Marks):** The five basic tastes — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami — directly drive acceptance. Humans are innately attracted to sweet and umami (energy/protein signals) and reject bitter (toxicity signal). Sugar and salt optimization are critical in product design.

2. **Smell / Olfaction (1.5 Marks):** Aroma accounts for 70–80% of flavour perception. Retronasal aroma during chewing is crucial. Off-odours (rancid, stale) cause immediate rejection. Example: Coffee, cheese, and vanilla are highly aroma-dependent.

3. **Texture / Mouthfeel (1.5 Marks):** Includes hardness, crispiness, viscosity, and granularity. Texture expectations differ by product category (e.g., crispy for chips, smooth for yogurt). Hydrocolloids are used to engineer desired textures.

4. **Appearance / Colour (1 Mark):** Visual cues create the first impression. Consumers expect strawberries to be red, mangoes to be yellow. Unusual colours (green ketchup) can be neophobic triggers unless contextually justified (Halloween branding).

5. **Sound (1 Mark):** The sound of crispiness (chips, biscuits) is a multisensory cue that enhances perceived freshness. Pepsi's "sound of the can opening" is a classic example.

**Concluding Statement:** Sensory factors collectively account for **60–70% of acceptance variance** and form the foundation of product design.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**The 9-Point Hedonic Scale (3 Marks):**

The 9-Point Hedonic Scale, developed at UC Davis, is a **psychophysical scale** used to measure consumer liking. It is a **labelled magnitude scale** with verbal anchors at each integer (1 = dislike extremely to 9 = like extremely). It is preferred over ranking or paired-preference tests because it is **simple, reliable, valid, and suitable for both trained and untrained panels**.

**Calculation of Mean Hedonic Score (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the formula:

$$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum (H_i \times f_i)}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator:

$$\sum (H_i \times f_i) = (9 \times 6) + (8 \times 9) + (7 \times 11) + (6 \times 8) + (5 \times 5) + (4 \times 4) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 2) + (1 \times 2)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each product:

$$= 54 + 72 + 77 + 48 + 25 + 16 + 9 + 4 + 2$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$= 307$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into formula. $N = 50$:

$$\bar{H} = \frac{307}{50} = 6.14$$

**Interpretation:** A mean score of **6.14** indicates that the product is **between "Like slightly" and "Like moderately"** — a moderate acceptance level. The product is commercially viable but sensory optimization is recommended.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Food Neophobia — Definition (2 Marks):**

Food neophobia, from Greek *neos* (new) and *phobos* (fear), is the **reluctance to consume novel or unfamiliar foods**. It is a well-documented phenomenon across all age groups, peaking in **2–6 year-old children** and **elderly populations**.

**Psychological Factors (3 Marks):**
1. **Fear of the Unknown:** Uncertainty about taste, safety, and consequences triggers avoidance.
2. **Negative Prior Experience:** A single bad experience (food poisoning) creates a **conditioned aversion** lasting decades.
3. **Sensory-Specific Satiety:** Repeated exposure to the same flavour causes boredom, prompting variety-seeking.
4. **Cognitive Rigidity:** Some individuals have an innate personality trait of resistance to change (measured by FNS).
5. **Marketing Influence:** Misleading or unappealing packaging creates distrust.

**Cultural Factors (2 Marks):**
1. **Religious Restrictions:** Pork avoidance in Islam/Judaism, beef avoidance in Hinduism, vegetarianism in Jainism.
2. **Cultural Food Identity:** Insects are delicacies in Thailand but avoided in Europe. Cheese with live larvae (casu marzu) is banned in many countries.
3. **Social Pressure:** Family and peer-group eating norms strongly influence individual choice.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**(i) Mean Hedonic Score (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum (H_i \times f_i)}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator:

$$\sum (H_i \times f_i) = (9 \times 10) + (7 \times 14) + (5 \times 8) + (3 \times 5) + (1 \times 3)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each product:

$$= 90 + 98 + 40 + 15 + 3 = 246$$

**Step 4:** Substitute. $N = 40$:

$$\bar{H} = \frac{246}{40} = 6.15$$

**(ii) Acceptance Rate (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Accepted panelists = those scoring ≥ 6. From the table, scores 9 and 7 qualify:

$$N_{acc} = 10 + 14 = 24$$

**Step 2:** Apply formula:

$$A\% = \frac{24}{40} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**(iii) Purchase Intent (2 Marks):**

The problem states a mapping: 9→5, 7→4, 5→3, 3→2, 1→1. The frequency table is the same (10, 14, 8, 5, 3).

**Step 1:** Apply the PI formula:

$$PI = \frac{\sum (f_i \times s_i)}{N \times s_{max}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator:

$$\sum (f_i \times s_i) = (10 \times 5) + (14 \times 4) + (8 \times 3) + (5 \times 2) + (3 \times 1)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each product:

$$= 50 + 56 + 24 + 10 + 3 = 143$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator: $N \times s_{max} = 40 \times 5 = 200$.

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$PI = \frac{143}{200} = 0.715 = 71.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** The biscuit has a **mean hedonic score of 6.15**, a **60% acceptance rate** (which is below the 70% launch threshold), and a **71.5% purchase intent**. The acceptance rate suggests the product needs **reformulation or rebranding** before commercial launch, even though purchase intent is high.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the formula** before substitution: Marks are awarded for the symbolic formula, not just the calculation. Always write $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum (H_i \times f_i)}{N}$ first.
> 2. **Wrong interpretation scale:** A score of 5.0 is "neutral" — not acceptable. Always use ≥ 6 for acceptance, not ≥ 5.
> 3. **Confusing Purchase Intent with Hedonic Score:** PI uses a *separate 5-point scale*; do not directly substitute 9-point scores.
> 4. **Ignoring the reverse-scoring in FNS:** Always check which items are reverse-coded before summing; failure to do so leads to wrong neophobia classification.
> 5. **Cultural factors as an afterthought:** Examiners specifically look for **religious examples** (halal, kosher, vegetarian) — generic answers like "culture matters" score zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Module 2**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Consumer Acceptance** = Multidimensional positive attitude (liking + preference + intent + consumption).
- ☐ **Consumer Avoidance** = Active or passive rejection of food.
- ☐ **Food Neophobia** = Fear of novel foods (Pliner & Hobden 1992; FNS range 10–40).

### Standard Scales (Memorize Anchors)
- ☐ **9-Point Hedonic Scale:** 1 = Dislike extremely ... 5 = Neutral ... 9 = Like extremely.
- ☐ **JAR Scale:** 5-point bipolar (Much too little — Just about right — Much too much).
- ☐ **5-Point Purchase Intent:** 1 = Definitely will not buy ... 5 = Definitely will buy.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $\bar{H} = \frac{\sum (H_i \times f_i)}{N}$
- ☐ $A\% = \frac{N_{acc}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ (with threshold ≥ 6)
- ☐ $PI = \frac{\sum (f_i \times s_i)}{N \times s_{max}}$

### Three Pillars of Acceptance
- ☐ **Sensory (60–70%):** Taste, smell, texture, appearance, sound.
- ☐ **Psychological:** Expectation, familiarity, branding, memory, mood.
- ☐ **Physiological/Health:** Hunger, allergies, culture, age, gut-brain axis.

### The 5–10 Exposure Rule
- ☐ Novel foods need **5–10 positive exposures** (children) and **3–5 exposures** (adults) to overcome neophobia.

### Industry Benchmarks
- ☐ **Acceptance Rate ≥ 70%** → launch-ready.
- ☐ **Rejection Rate > 15%** → reformulate.
- ☐ **Top-Box ≥ 25%** → flagship product.
- ☐ **Purchase Intent ≥ 60%** → commercially viable.

### Cultural Triggers (Exam-Ready Examples)
- ☐ Pork (Islam/Judaism avoidance), Beef (Hindu avoidance), Alcohol (Islam avoidance), Insect foods (Western avoidance), Insects (Asian acceptance), Raw fish (Western suspicion vs. Japanese sushi), Cheese with live larvae (Sardinia only).

### Cross-Cutting KTU 2024 Themes
- ☐ Map all answers to **Bloom's Levels L2–L4**.
- ☐ Use **CO 2 (Understand consumer behaviour)** and **CO 3 (Apply acceptance tests)** as answer-starters.
- ☐ Always conclude with an **engineering/design implication** for full marks.

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Do not write "consumer likes" without specifying **which sensory dimension**.
- ☐ Do not confuse **sensory acceptance** (perception) with **hedonic acceptance** (pleasure).
- ☐ Do not forget the **reverse-scoring** in FNS.
- ☐ Do not use **percentages when totals differ** — use weighted formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:** *"Acceptance is engineered. Avoidance is preventable. The food technologist's role is to design products that turn 'No' into 'Yes' through sensory science, cultural intelligence, and consumer empathy."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration of Consumer Needs in Product Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Integration of Consumer Needs in Product Development # Integration of Consumer Needs in Product Development ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Integration of Consumer Needs (ICN) in Product Development** is the systematic, iterative, and multi-disciplinary process of capturing, translating, prioritizing, and embedding the latent and explicit requirements, preferences, expectations, and usage contexts of the end-consumer into every stage of the food product development (FPD) pipeline — from ideation and concept generation through formulation, packaging, sensory optimization, and post-launch evaluation. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, this integration is operationalized through structured consumer research methodologies such as **Voice of Customer (VOC) capture**, **Kano Model categorization**, and **Quality Function Deployment (QFD / House of Quality)**, ensuring that the final food product delivers measurable **Consumer Acceptability (CA)**, **Purchase Intent (PI)**, and **Repeat Purchase Behavior (RPB)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** The integration is NOT a one-time survey at the beginning. It is a **closed-loop feedback system** that continues from concept screening → prototype testing → shelf-life trials → post-market surveillance. KTU examiners frequently test this "loop" nature. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are a **chef designing a new breakfast cereal** for the Indian/Kerala market. You could: - **(a) Chef-Centric Approach:** Pick ingredients YOU like, blend them, and launch it. Result → poor sales, because your "taste" is not the "market's taste." - **(b) Consumer-Integrated Approach:** Before touching the recipe, you *talk* to mothers (primary buyers), children (primary consumers), and dieticians (influencers). You observe breakfast tables. You ask: "What frustrates you about your current cereal?" You then *translate* "I want something that keeps my child full till lunch" into the technical specification: **"≥ 8 g dietary fibre per 40 g serving with low glycemic index."** The second approach IS the integration of consumer needs. The chef doesn't abandon expertise — instead, expertise is **steered** by consumer intelligence. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Table | Term | Expansion | Function in ICN | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Raw qualitative + quantitative consumer inputs | | **CTQ** | Critical To Quality | Translated technical specifications | | **Kano** | Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984) | Categorizes needs as Must-be / One-dim / Attractive | | **QFD** | Quality Function Deployment | Matrix translating VOC → CTQ (House of Quality) | | **HOQ** | House of Quality | The visual matrix used in QFD | | **NPD** | New Product Development | The broader umbrella process | | **CATA** | Check-All-That-Apply | Sensory consumer descriptive test | | **JAR** | Just-About-Right | Sensory optimization scale | > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric:** Consumer Acceptability is typically measured on a **9-point hedonic scale** (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely), with a Mean Acceptability Score $\geq 6.5$ considered commercially viable in most food categories. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Stage Integration Framework (KTU Board Standard) The integration is sequenced into **five logically sequential stages**, each with a specific output that feeds the next: 1. **Stage 1 — VOC Capture (Listening Phase)** - Methods: Focus groups (n = 8–12), in-depth interviews, ethnographic observation, social listening, online reviews mining. - Output: A raw database of **consumer statements** (verbatim quotes + observed behaviors). 2. **Stage 2 — VOC Interpretation (Translating Phase)** - Affinity Diagramming: Group raw statements into clusters (e.g., "Health," "Convenience," "Taste," "Price"). - Voice-of-Customer Tree: Drill from generic attributes → specific needs → measurable specs. 3. **Stage 3 — Prioritization (Kano Categorization)** - Each need is plotted on the **Kano Two-Dimensional Grid** (x-axis = functional fulfilment, y-axis = satisfaction). - Categories: **M (Must-be)**, **O (One-dimensional)**, **A (Attractive)**, **I (Indifferent)**, **R (Reverse)**. 4. **Stage 4 — Technical Translation (QFD / HOQ Construction)** - The prioritized consumer needs form the **"Whats"** rows of the House of Quality. - The engineering/technical characteristics form the **"Hows"** columns. - The central matrix (relationship matrix) connects them with weights **9 (strong)**, **3 (medium)**, **1 (weak)**. 5. **Stage 5 — Validation & Iteration (Closed-Loop Phase)** - Prototype testing via Central Location Tests (CLT) and Home-Use Tests (HUT). - Re-feeding consumer data into reformulation cycles. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage | Stage | Why It Exists | How It Operates | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | VOC Capture | You cannot design what you do not know | Qualitative tools (low n, high depth) | | VOC Interpretation | Raw data is unusable without structure | Affinity mapping, KJ Method | | Prioritization | Resources are finite — not all needs are equal | Kano survey + Importance-Satisfaction scoring | | Technical Translation | Consumer language ≠ Engineering language | QFD matrix with weighted relationships | | Validation | The model is hypothetical until tested | Hedonic tests, CATA, JAR scales | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The following equations are frequently asked as **3-mark derivations** in KTU ESE. Memorize both the formula and its interpretation. **Equation 2.1 — Mean Hedonic Acceptability Score** $$\bar{H} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$$ Where $H_i$ is the individual hedonic rating on a 9-point scale, and $n$ is the sample size (typically $\geq 100$ for statistical reliability). **Equation 2.2 — Purchase Intent (PI) Index** $$PI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i \cdot p_i)}{n}$$ Where $w_i$ is the weight of each PI category (e.g., Definitely Buy = 5, Probably Buy = 4, etc.) and $p_i$ is the proportion of respondents in that category. **Equation 2.3 — Kano Categorization (Pairwise Logic)** For each consumer requirement, two questions are posed: - **Functional form (F):** "If [feature] IS present, how do you feel?" (Like / Must-be / Neutral / Live with / Dislike) - **Dysfunctional form (D):** "If [feature] is NOT present, how do you feel?" (Like / Must-be / Neutral / Live with / Dislike) The **Kano Evaluation Table** cross-tabulates F vs D answers to assign category M / O / A / I / R. **Equation 2.4 — Kano Satisfaction Coefficient (CS / CD)** $$CS = \frac{A + O}{A + O + M + I}$$ $$CD = \frac{-O - M}{A + O + M + I}$$ Where $CS$ = Customer Satisfaction coefficient (range 0 to 1), $CD$ = Customer Dissatisfaction coefficient (range -1 to 0). **Equation 2.5 — House of Quality (HOQ) Technical Importance Weight** $$W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (R_i \cdot w_{ij})$$ Where $W_j$ = absolute importance of technical descriptor $j$, $R_i$ = importance rank of consumer requirement $i$, $w_{ij}$ = relationship weight between consumer requirement $i$ and technical descriptor $j$ (values: 9, 3, 1, or 0). **Equation 2.6 — Importance-Satisfaction (I-S) Plot Coordinates** $$x_i = \bar{I}_i \quad ; \quad y_i = \bar{S}_i$$ The I-S plane is divided into **4 quadrants** by mean lines: Concentrate Here (high I, low S), Keep Up Good Work (high I, high S), Low Priority (low I, low S), Possible Overkill (low I, high S). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Kano Satisfaction Coefficient We will derive how the **Customer Satisfaction (CS)** coefficient is constructed from raw Kano survey frequencies. **Step 1 — Frequency Tabulation** For a sample of $n$ respondents answering one requirement, each response is classified into one of five Kano categories: $$n_M + n_O + n_A + n_I + n_R = n$$ Where $n_M$, $n_O$, $n_A$, $n_I$, $n_R$ are the counts of Must-be, One-dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, and Reverse responses respectively. **Step 2 — Identifying the Dominant Category** The respondent's classification into one of M / O / A / I / R is determined by the **Kano Evaluation Table** (pairwise lookup of F vs D answers). **Step 3 — Constructing the CS Coefficient** The CS coefficient represents the **maximum potential increase in satisfaction** if a fully functional attribute is delivered: $$\begin{aligned} CS &= \frac{\text{Sum of positive-impact categories}}{\text{Sum of all non-reverse categories}} \\ &= \frac{n_A + n_O}{n_A + n_O + n_M + n_I} \end{aligned}$$ *Conversion Logic:* Categories A and O contribute to satisfaction when present; M does not increase satisfaction above the baseline (it only prevents dissatisfaction); I has no effect either way. Hence the numerator sums only A + O, and the denominator excludes R (Reverse responses indicate a need opposite to what is proposed). **Step 4 — Constructing the CD Coefficient** The CD coefficient represents the **maximum potential decrease in satisfaction** if the attribute is completely absent: $$\begin{aligned} CD &= -\frac{n_O + n_M}{n_A + n_O + n_M + n_I} \end{aligned}$$ *Conversion Logic:* Categories O and M contribute to dissatisfaction when absent; A does not cause dissatisfaction when absent (the user never expected it); I has no effect. The negative sign ensures CD ranges from -1 to 0. **Step 5 — Interpreting the Coefficient Pair** | CS Range | CD Range | Strategic Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $CS \approx 1$ | $CD \approx 0$ | Pure **Attractive** need — delight the consumer | | $CS \approx 0$ | $CD \approx -1$ | Pure **Must-be** need — non-negotiable baseline | | $CS \approx 0.5$ | $CD \approx -0.5$ | **One-dimensional** — linear satisfaction driver | | $CS \approx 0$ | $CD \approx 0$ | **Indifferent** — do not invest resources | ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: House of Quality (HOQ) Step-by-Step Construction The HOQ is the master QFD matrix. Below is the **exhaustive construction logic** KTU examiners expect. **Step 1 — Define the "Whats" (Consumer Requirements / CRs)** CRs are placed as rows. Each row receives an **Importance Rating (IR)** from consumers, typically on a 1–5 Likert scale: $$IR_i = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} r_{ik}}{N}$$ Where $r_{ik}$ is the rating given by respondent $k$ to requirement $i$, and $N$ is the total number of respondents. **Step 2 — Define the "Hows" (Technical Descriptors / TDs)** TDs are the columns. They are the **measurable engineering parameters** derived from CRs. Examples in a food product: - CR: "Crispy texture" → TD: "Water activity ($a_w < 0.4$)" - CR: "Long shelf life" → TD: "Packaging oxygen transmission rate (OTR $< 1 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$)" **Step 3 — Populate the Relationship Matrix (Central Matrix)** For each CR–TD pair, assign a strength: $$\text{Relationship Strength} \in \{9, 3, 1, 0\}$$ | Symbol | Strength | Visual (in HOQ) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 9 | Strong | Double circle (filled) | | 3 | Medium | Single circle | | 1 | Weak | Triangle | | 0 | None | Blank | **Step 4 — Compute Technical Importance Weights** For each TD column $j$: $$W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} IR_i \cdot w_{ij}$$ **Worked Example:** Suppose we have 3 CRs and 3 TDs. The Importance Ratings of CRs are $IR_1 = 5$, $IR_2 = 4$, $IR_3 = 3$. The relationship matrix $w_{ij}$ is: $$\begin{aligned} W_1 &= (5 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 3) + (3 \cdot 1) = 45 + 12 + 3 = 60 \\ W_2 &= (5 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 1) = 15 + 36 + 3 = 54 \\ W_3 &= (5 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 1) + (3 \cdot 9) = 5 + 4 + 27 = 36 \end{aligned}$$ *Conversion Logic:* TD1 is the most strategically important ($W_1 = 60$), so engineering effort should be concentrated there. **Step 5 — Add the Roof (Correlation Matrix)** The roof of the HOQ captures **technical trade-offs** between TDs (positive = synergy, negative = conflict). For food products: - High protein ↔ Low sugar = **+** (synergy, both aligned with health positioning) - High crispness ↔ High moisture = **−** (conflict, physical incompatibility) **Step 6 — Benchmark Against Competitors** The bottom rows of the HOQ list competitor products with their performance on each CR, allowing **strategic positioning** of the new product. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Consumer Integration Closed-Loop System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 1: VOC Capture
Focus Groups, Ethnography, Surveys] B[Stage 2: VOC Interpretation
Affinity Mapping, KJ Method] C[Stage 3: Prioritization
Kano Model and I-S Plot] D[Stage 4: Technical Translation
House of Quality QFD] E[Stage 5: Validation and Iteration
CLT, HUT, CATA, JAR Tests] F[Launch and Post Market Surveillance] A -->|Raw consumer statements| B B -->|Clustered needs tree| C C -->|Prioritized Kano categories| D D -->|Weighted technical descriptors| E E -->|Iterative reformulation| A E -->|Acceptance threshold met| F F -.->|Consumer feedback loop| A ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: Kano Two-Dimensional Categorization Grid ```mermaid flowchart TB K[Kano Categorization Logic] K --> CAT1[Must Be M
Basic expectations
Present gives no delight, absent causes anger] K --> CAT2[One Dimensional O
More is better linear
Direct satisfaction relationship] K --> CAT3[Attractive A
Delighters and exciters
Unexpected value addition] K --> CAT4[Indifferent I
Consumer does not care] K --> CAT5[Reverse R
Opposite preference exists] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Mapping Consumer Voice to Product Specs ```mermaid flowchart TD V1[Consumer Says: I want it to be healthier] V2[Consumer Says: I want it to be tastier] V3[Consumer Says: I want easy to prepare] V1 --> S1[Affinity Cluster: HEALTH] V2 --> S2[Affinity Cluster: TASTE] V3 --> S3[Affinity Cluster: CONVENIENCE] S1 --> K1[Kano Tag: MUST BE] S2 --> K2[Kano Tag: ONE DIMENSIONAL] S3 --> K3[Kano Tag: ATTRACTIVE] K1 --> T1[TD: Add 8g fibre per serving
Reduce sodium below 300mg] K2 --> T2[TD: Optimize sugar acid balance
Use natural flavour enhancers] K3 --> T3[TD: Single serve microwave pouch
Ready in 90 seconds] T1 --> P1[Final Spec Sheet for R and D Team] T2 --> P1 T3 --> P1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Note:** The bottom diagram (Section 4.3) is the **core exam-friendly visualization** KTU examiners expect when the question reads "Explain how consumer needs are integrated into product specifications." Trace from VOC verbatim statements all the way down to the Technical Descriptor (TD) block. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Kano Model** and list its five categories of consumer requirements. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Kano Model**, developed by Prof. Noriaki Kano in 1984, is a two-dimensional framework that categorizes consumer preferences based on their functional fulfilment versus the resulting satisfaction. It is widely used in food product design to prioritize features. The five categories are: | Code | Category | Behaviour | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **M** | Must-be (Basic) | Dissatisfier if absent; no extra delight if present | | **O** | One-dimensional (Performance) | Satisfaction is linearly proportional to fulfilment | | **A** | Attractive (Exciters) | Delighters when present; not missed when absent | | **I** | Indifferent | Consumer does not care either way | | **R** | Reverse | Consumer prefers the OPPOSITE of what is offered | *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing categories + 1 mark for one-line behaviour of each.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **House of Quality (HOQ)**? Name any four components of the HOQ matrix. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the primary matrix used in **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** to translate the Voice of Customer (VOC) into measurable engineering/technical specifications for product development. Four key components of the HOQ are: 1. **Whats (Consumer Requirements)** — rows on the left, with importance ratings. 2. **Hows (Technical Descriptors)** — columns at the top. 3. **Relationship Matrix (Central Matrix)** — weights 9, 3, 1, 0 linking Whats to Hows. 4. **Roof (Correlation Matrix)** — synergies/conflicts between Technical Descriptors. *(1 mark definition + 0.5 marks × 4 components = 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** *(a)* Explain in detail the **five-stage framework for integrating consumer needs into food product development**. Describe the tools used at each stage and the output generated. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* A company is developing a **ready-to-drink (RTD) protein shake** for working professionals aged 25–40. Apply the **Kano Model methodology** to categorize five hypothetical consumer requirements (high protein content, low sugar, chocolate flavour, eco-friendly packaging, lactose-free). Construct the Kano satisfaction coefficient and interpret the strategic priority. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Seven-Mark Answer **Stage 1 — VOC Capture (2 marks)** Methods include **focus groups** (guided discussion with 8–12 participants), **in-depth interviews** (one-on-one exploration), **ethnographic observation** (studying consumer behavior in real usage settings like gyms, offices), **online sentiment analysis**, and **lead-user analysis**. **Output:** Raw verbatim statements and observed pain points. **Stage 2 — VOC Interpretation (1.5 marks)** The **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)** clusters hundreds of raw statements into a manageable tree of **consumer attributes → specific needs → measurable expectations**. **Output:** Hierarchical needs tree (e.g., "Healthy" → "High protein" → "≥ 20 g per serving"). **Stage 3 — Prioritization using Kano (1.5 marks)** Each need is surveyed with the **paired Kano questionnaire** (functional + dysfunctional questions). Results are tabulated into the Kano Evaluation Table to assign each need to category M / O / A / I / R. **Output:** Prioritized list with Kano tags and CS/CD coefficients. **Stage 4 — Technical Translation via QFD (1.5 marks)** The prioritized CRs populate the **House of Quality** rows. Technical descriptors populate the columns. The relationship matrix (9/3/1/0) connects them. The roof captures engineering trade-offs. **Output:** Weighted importance ranking of technical parameters + competitive benchmarks. **Stage 5 — Validation & Iteration (0.5 marks)** **Central Location Tests (CLT)**, **Home-Use Tests (HUT)**, and **CATA / JAR scales** validate the prototype against consumer expectations. Failures feed back to Stage 1 (closed-loop). --- #### Part (b) — Seven-Mark Answer with Worked Coefficients **Step 1 — Hypothetical Survey Result (2 marks)** Assume 100 respondents answered the paired Kano questionnaire. The categorization result is tabulated as: | Requirement | M | O | A | I | R | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | High protein content | 60 | 25 | 5 | 8 | 2 | | Low sugar | 70 | 20 | 4 | 5 | 1 | | Chocolate flavour | 10 | 30 | 50 | 8 | 2 | | Eco-friendly packaging | 5 | 15 | 55 | 22 | 3 | | Lactose-free | 40 | 35 | 15 | 8 | 2 | **Step 2 — Compute CS Coefficient for each requirement (2 marks)** Applying Equation 2.4: $$\begin{aligned} CS_{\text{protein}} &= \frac{5 + 25}{5 + 25 + 60 + 8} = \frac{30}{98} \approx 0.306 \\ CS_{\text{sugar}} &= \frac{4 + 20}{4 + 20 + 70 + 5} = \frac{24}{99} \approx 0.242 \\ CS_{\text{flavour}} &= \frac{50 + 30}{50 + 30 + 10 + 8} = \frac{80}{98} \approx 0.816 \\ CS_{\text{eco}} &= \frac{55 + 15}{55 + 15 + 5 + 22} = \frac{70}{97} \approx 0.722 \\ CS_{\text{lactose-free}} &= \frac{15 + 35}{15 + 35 + 40 + 8} = \frac{50}{98} \approx 0.510 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute CD Coefficient (1 mark)** $$\begin{aligned} CD_{\text{protein}} &= -\frac{25 + 60}{98} \approx -0.867 \\ CD_{\text{sugar}} &= -\frac{20 + 70}{99} \approx -0.909 \\ CD_{\text{lactose-free}} &= -\frac{35 + 40}{98} \approx -0.765 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Strategic Interpretation (2 marks)** | Requirement | Kano Category (Dominant) | CS | CD | Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | High protein | **Must-be (M)** | 0.31 | -0.87 | **Non-negotiable baseline** — must deliver, will not delight | | Low sugar | **Must-be (M)** | 0.24 | -0.91 | **Critical** — its absence will destroy the brand | | Chocolate flavour | **Attractive (A)** | 0.82 | -0.41 | **Differentiator** — strong delight driver, use for marketing | | Eco-friendly packaging | **Attractive (A)** | 0.72 | -0.21 | **Premium positioning** — appeals to Gen-Z segment | | Lactose-free | **One-dimensional (O)** | 0.51 | -0.77 | **Quality driver** — linear improvement with marketing claims | **Strategic Conclusion:** The product **must** meet the protein and sugar specs (otherwise consumers reject it). **Flavour** and **eco-packaging** are the levers for differentiation and should receive the marketing budget. **Lactose-free** is the segment-defining feature. --- ### Alternative Question Choice **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** *(a)* Define **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**. Construct the **House of Quality (HOQ)** step-by-step for a new **low-sodium instant soup** targeted at hypertensive consumers. Identify at least **four consumer requirements** and **four technical descriptors**, and compute the technical importance weights. **(7 Marks)** *(b)* Discuss the **Importance-Satisfaction (I-S) Analysis** method. How is it used to identify which consumer needs require immediate corrective action versus those that represent over-engineering? Illustrate with a 2×2 quadrant diagram. **(7 Marks)** *(Model solution framework for Question B is omitted for brevity, but the structure follows the same exhaustive logic — QFD construction followed by I-S quadrant plotting and interpretation.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing "Importance" with "Satisfaction"** in the I-S plot: Importance = how much the consumer CARES; Satisfaction = how well you are CURRENTLY DELIVERING. Many students invert the axes and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to exclude "Reverse" (R) responses** when calculating the Kano CS coefficient — this contaminates the denominator and yields an invalid coefficient. > 3. **Writing "Kano Model has 3 categories"** — it has FIVE (M, O, A, I, R). Examiners deduct 1 mark for incomplete listing. > 4. **Conflating QFD with TQM or Six Sigma** — QFD is a *design translation* tool; TQM is a *process management* philosophy. > 5. **Skipping the closed-loop arrow** in the integration diagram — KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the "iteration" aspect. Always show the feedback arrow from validation back to VOC capture. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Integration of Consumer Needs** - **Definition to memorize:** ICN is the systematic process of capturing, translating, prioritizing, and embedding consumer requirements into every FPD stage. - **Five stages:** VOC Capture → VOC Interpretation → Kano Prioritization → QFD Technical Translation → Validation & Iteration (closed-loop). - **Kano categories (5):** Must-be (M), One-dimensional (O), Attractive (A), Indifferent (I), Reverse (R). - **CS coefficient** formula: $CS = \frac{n_A + n_O}{n_A + n_O + n_M + n_I}$ (range 0 to 1). - **CD coefficient** formula: $CD = -\frac{n_O + n_M}{n_A + n_O + n_M + n_I}$ (range -1 to 0). - **HOQ components (must know all):** Whats (rows), Hows (columns), Relationship Matrix (9/3/1/0), Roof (Correlation), Importance Weights, Competitive Benchmark. - **Technical Importance Weight formula:** $W_j = \sum_{i} IR_i \cdot w_{ij}$. - **Hedonic scale:** 9-point (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely); commercial viability $\bar{H} \geq 6.5$. - **I-S Plot quadrants (4):** Concentrate Here / Keep Up Good Work / Low Priority / Possible Overkill. - **Tools to remember:** Affinity Diagram (KJ Method), VOC Tree, Kano Questionnaire (paired F/D form), House of Quality, CATA, JAR scale, CLT, HUT. - **Conceptual difference:** Importance ≠ Satisfaction. Importance is what the consumer wants; Satisfaction is what you deliver. - **Closed-loop principle:** Consumer data is fed back to formulation; iteration is mandatory, not optional. - **Real-world anchor:** Always illustrate with a food example (e.g., RTD shake, breakfast cereal, instant soup) — abstract answers without food context lose marks under KTU valuation norms. ---

topics.title: Principles of Product Development Management: People in Product Development Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Product Development Management: People in Product Development Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Development Management (PDM)** is the systematic orchestration of people, processes, and resources to conceptualize, design, commercialize, and continuously improve products that deliver measurable value to consumers and sustainable profit to the organization. Within this discipline, **"People in Product Development Management"** refers specifically to the human capital subsystem — the roles, competencies, team structures, leadership behaviors, communication patterns, and organizational cultures that collectively convert an idea into a market-ready food product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** People are the *primary determinant* of PDM success or failure. Research cited in the KTU 2024 module identifies that approximately **70%** of new food product launches in India fail, and management/people issues — not technical formulation problems — are cited as the leading cause in **over 60%** of these failures.

The formal pillars of "People in PDM" are:

1. **Role Architecture** — Who does what (R&D, Marketing, Production, QA, Regulatory, Supply Chain).
2. **Team Topology** — How those roles are grouped (functional, cross-functional, project-based).
3. **Leadership & Decision Rights** — Who decides and how authority flows.
4. **Communication Geometry** — How information moves (vertical, horizontal, diagonal).
5. **Culture & Motivation** — The shared values that energize collaboration.
6. **Competency & Learning** — The skills required and how they are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition First

> [!NOTE]
> **The Restaurant Brigade Analogy 🍳**
> Imagine a Michelin-star restaurant launching a brand-new tasting menu. The **Head Chef** (Project Leader) designs the concept, but the dish only reaches the table when the **Sous Chef** (R&D Scientist) perfects the recipe, the **Pastry Chef** (Food Technologist) balances texture, the **Sommelier** (Marketing Manager) writes the menu description, the **Floor Manager** (Sales Lead) explains it to guests, and the **Dishwasher** (Quality Technician) ensures hygiene standards are met. If the Head Chef shouts orders but never listens, the team freezes. If the Pastry Chef hides recipes from the Floor Manager, guests get confused orders. The food fails — not because the recipe was bad, but because the **people-system** broke down. Food Product Development Management is exactly this kitchen, scaled to an industrial FMCG or F&B company.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for a Food Technologist

As a B.Tech Food Technology student, you will graduate into roles where you must:
- Lead or participate in **cross-functional NPD (New Product Development) teams**.
- Translate lab-scale breakthroughs into **commercial-scale launches**.
- Negotiate with **marketing, regulatory, supply chain, and finance** stakeholders who do not speak your technical language.

Understanding the *people architecture* of PDM is therefore as essential as understanding formulation chemistry.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Time-to-Market (TTM) for a new food product in the Indian FMCG sector averages **18–24 months**, and cross-functional team effectiveness can compress this by **30–40%** according to KTU-cited industry benchmarks.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of "People in PDM" — Structured Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Role Architecture (Who Does What)

In a food product development project, the following **core roles** are mandatory under KTU 2024 expectations:

| # | Role | Primary Mandate | Typical Background |
|---|------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Project Leader / NPD Manager** | Orchestrates timeline, budget, gate decisions | Food Tech + MBA |
| 2 | **R&D Food Scientist** | Formulation, prototype, sensory bench | B.Tech / M.Tech Food Tech |
| 3 | **Marketing Manager** | Consumer insight, positioning, launch plan | MBA Marketing |
| 4 | **Production / Process Engineer** | Scale-up, line trials, yield optimization | B.Tech Mechanical/Food |
| 5 | **Quality Assurance (QA) / QC** | FSSAI, ISO, HACCP, shelf-life testing | Food Tech / Microbiology |
| 6 | **Regulatory Affairs Officer** | Labeling, claims, legal compliance | Food Tech + Law |
| 7 | **Supply Chain / Procurement** | Ingredient sourcing, vendor qualification | SCM / Food Tech |
| 8 | **Finance / Costing Analyst** | COGS, margin, ROI modelling | CA / MBA Finance |
| 9 | **Consumer Insights / Sensory Panel Lead** | Qualitative & quantitative consumer testing | Psychology / Statistics |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A **Cross-Functional Team (CFT)** is a temporary, goal-aligned collection of specialists from different functional departments who jointly own a product from concept to launch.

---

### Pillar 2: Team Topology — How Roles Are Organized

Three dominant topologies appear in food industry PDM:

1. **Functional (Silo) Structure**
   - Each department works independently.
   - R&D finishes → hands off to Production → hands off to Marketing.
   - **Strength:** Deep specialization.
   - **Weakness:** Slow handoffs, low accountability, "throw-it-over-the-wall" syndrome.

2. **Cross-Functional / Project Matrix Structure** *(KTU recommended)*
   - Specialists from all departments form one team for the project lifetime.
   - **Dual reporting:** Functional manager (technical excellence) + Project manager (delivery).
   - **Strength:** Fast iteration, shared accountability, holistic decision-making.
   - **Weakness:** Role conflict if authority is unclear.

3. **Dedicated Innovation Unit (Skunkworks / Intrapreneurship Cell)**
   - A small, isolated team given autonomy and resources.
   - **Strength:** Speed, creativity, low bureaucracy.
   - **Weakness:** Re-integration challenges back into the mainstream organization.

---

### Pillar 3: Leadership & Decision Rights

The project leader must adopt a **stage-appropriate leadership style**. KTU expects familiarity with the **Situational Leadership Model (Hersey-Blanchard)**:

$$L = f(\text{Competence},\ \text{Commitment})$$

Where:
- $L$ = Leadership style (Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating).
- Competence = the team's skill level.
- Commitment = the team's motivation/confidence.

| Stage | Team State | Leader Behaviour |
|-------|------------|------------------|
| **Forming** | Low skill, high enthusiasm | **Directing** — tell, show, supervise |
| **Storming** | Some skill, frustrated | **Coaching** — explain, sell, get buy-in |
| **Norming** | Growing skill, committed | **Supporting** — facilitate, encourage |
| **Performing** | High skill, high commitment | **Delegating** — monitor, empower |

---

### Pillar 4: Communication Geometry

Effective PDM communication follows the **3-C rule**:
- **Clear** — no jargon between departments.
- **Consistent** — same message across all stakeholders.
- **Continuous** — no "radio silence" between gates.

Common channels: kick-off meetings, stage-gate reviews, RACI charts, shared digital dashboards, and consumer co-creation workshops.

---

### Pillar 5: Culture & Motivation

A culture of **psychological safety** (Edmondson, 1999 — concept only, do not cite as exam text) allows R&D scientists to admit a failed prototype without fear of blame — accelerating learning cycles. Motivators specific to PDM teams include:
- Autonomy over experimental design.
- Mastery via sensory training and pilot-plant exposure.
- Purpose via consumer impact storytelling.

---

### Pillar 6: Competency & Learning

The **T-shaped skills model** is mandated in KTU 2024 notes:
- **Vertical bar** = deep technical skill (e.g., food chemistry).
- **Horizontal bar** = breadth across disciplines (marketing, QA, regulatory).

Modern PDM professionals are expected to be **T-shaped or even comb-shaped** (multi-disciplinary depth).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Nestlé India** operates a **matrix NPD structure** where brand managers, R&D, and supply chain co-own launches — directly applying the CFT model.
- **Mother Dairy & Amul** use **stage-gate reviews** at Concept → Development → Commercialization to control product proliferation.
- **Start-up food brands** (e.g., boAt-style disruptors in snacking) use **skunkworks units** for rapid iteration.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** When asked "Which team structure is best for PDM?", the model answer is **Cross-Functional Project Matrix** — never answer "Functional" because silos slow food innovation cycles.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Models

This section provides exhaustive, examiner-grade breakdowns of the three frameworks most frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.

---

## 3.1 The Tuckman Team-Development Model (Stage-Wise Derivation)

The model is a four- (now five-) stage descriptive framework describing how PDM teams evolve. We will derive the behavioural and managerial implications at each stage.

### Step 1 — Establish the Independent Variable
Let:
- $T$ = time elapsed since team formation (in weeks).
- $C(t)$ = Team Competence at time $t$ (0 to 1 scale).
- $M(t)$ = Team Commitment at time $t$ (0 to 1 scale).
- $L(t)$ = Required Leadership intensity at time $t$ (0 to 1 scale).

### Step 2 — Write the Behavioural Trajectory

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Stage 1 — Forming}\ (0 \le T \le 2 \text{ weeks}) \\
&\quad C(t) \approx 0.20,\quad M(t) \approx 0.85,\quad L(t) \approx 0.90 \\
&\textbf{Stage 2 — Storming}\ (2 < T \le 6 \text{ weeks}) \\
&\quad C(t) \approx 0.40,\quad M(t) \approx 0.45,\quad L(t) \approx 0.70 \\
&\textbf{Stage 3 — Norming}\ (6 < T \le 12 \text{ weeks}) \\
&\quad C(t) \approx 0.70,\quad M(t) \approx 0.75,\quad L(t) \approx 0.40 \\
&\textbf{Stage 4 — Performing}\ (T > 12 \text{ weeks}) \\
&\quad C(t) \approx 0.90,\quad M(t) \approx 0.90,\quad L(t) \approx 0.20 \\
&\textbf{Stage 5 — Adjourning}\ (T = T_{\text{launch}}) \\
&\quad L(t) \approx 0.60 \text{ (transition support)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Translate Each Stage into PDM Managerial Action

| Stage | What the PDM Team Feels | What the Manager Must Do |
|-------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Forming** | Excitement, anxiety, polite behaviour | Assign clear roles; explain the Stage-Gate roadmap. |
| **Storming** | Conflict over design, territory, priorities | Mediate, coach, do NOT avoid conflict. |
| **Norming** | Trust, shared language, rhythm | Step back; reward collaboration. |
| **Performing** | Autonomy, high output, low friction | Delegate; remove external obstacles. |
| **Adjourning** | Loss, pride, transition anxiety | Celebrate launches; capture lessons learned. |

### Step 4 — The Managerial Decision Rule

$$
\text{Leadership Style}(T) = \begin{cases}
\text{Directing} & \text{if } T \in [0,2] \\
\text{Coaching} & \text{if } T \in (2,6] \\
\text{Supporting} & \text{if } T \in (6,12] \\
\text{Delegating} & \text{if } T > 12 \\
\text{Transitioning} & \text{if } T = T_{\text{launch}} \\
\end{cases}
$$

This is the **Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership** mapping, derived from the same $T$-axis as Tuckman's stages.

---

## 3.2 The RACI Matrix — Step-by-Step Construction

### Step 1 — Define the Four Roles

$$
\text{RACI} = \{ \text{Responsible},\ \text{Accountable},\ \text{Consulted},\ \text{Informed} \}
$$

- **Responsible** $\to$ $R$ = Person who actually executes the task (many possible per task).
- **Accountable** $\to$ $A$ = Person who *owns* the outcome; **exactly one** per task.
- **Consulted** $\to$ $C$ = Subject-matter expert whose input is sought before action.
- **Informed** $\to$ $I$ = Stakeholder kept in the loop after the action.

### Step 2 — Build a RACI for a Typical Food NPD Launch

| Activity | R&D Scientist | Marketing Mgr | Prod. Engineer | QA Lead | NPD Project Mgr |
|----------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:-------:|:---------------:|
| Formulation design | **R/A** | C | I | C | A (gate) |
| Consumer sensory test | C | **R/A** | I | I | A (gate) |
| Pilot-plant scale-up | C | I | **R/A** | C | A (gate) |
| FSSAI label approval | I | C | I | **R/A** | A (gate) |
| Launch communication | I | **R/A** | I | I | A (gate) |

### Step 3 — Apply the RACI Quality Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Rule 1: Exactly one A per row.} \\
&\text{Rule 2: At least one R per row.} \\
&\text{Rule 3: Total C + I should not exceed } 8 \text{ to avoid paralysis.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Assigning multiple "A"s in one row. This destroys accountability and is the #1 RACI failure in KTU scripts.

---

## 3.3 The Stage-Gate Process — Sequential Derivation for Food NPD

### Step 1 — List the Standard Gates

$$
G = \{ G_0,\ G_1,\ G_2,\ G_3,\ G_4,\ G_5 \}
$$

Where:
- $G_0$ = Idea screening
- $G_1$ = Concept approval
- $G_2$ = Business case approval
- $G_3$ = Development approval
- $G_4$ = Test-market approval
- $G_5$ = Commercial launch

### Step 2 — Define the People Decision Rights at Each Gate

| Gate | Decision Body | Required Attendees |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| $G_0$ | NPD Manager | R&D, Marketing |
| $G_1$ | Steering Committee | All CFT members |
| $G_2$ | Steering Committee + Finance | All CFT + CFO |
| $G_3$ | Steering Committee | All CFT + Plant Head |
| $G_4$ | Brand Director | Marketing + Sales + R&D |
| $G_5$ | CEO / MD | Full leadership |

### Step 3 — Define the Go/Kill/Recycle/Hold Logic

$$
D_i = \begin{cases}
\text{GO} & \text{if Score}_i \geq 70 \\
\text{HOLD} & \text{if } 50 \le \text{Score}_i < 70 \\
\text{RECYCLE} & \text{if } 30 \le \text{Score}_i < 50 \\
\text{KILL} & \text{if } \text{Score}_i < 30 \\
\end{cases}
$$

Where $\text{Score}_i$ is the weighted gate score combining technical feasibility, consumer appeal, financial return, and regulatory clearance — typically weighted **30 / 30 / 25 / 15** respectively in food PDM.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define a *Cross-Functional Team* in the context of food product development. Why is it preferred over a functional silo structure for NPD?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Cross-Functional Team (CFT)** is a temporary, project-based team comprising specialists from different functional departments — R&D, Marketing, Production, QA, Regulatory, Supply Chain, Finance — who jointly own a food product from concept to launch. **(1 mark)**
It is preferred over a functional (silo) structure because: **(2 marks)**
1. It eliminates the "throw-it-over-the-wall" delay between formulation, scale-up, and commercialization.
2. It enables *concurrent* (parallel) rather than sequential work, compressing **time-to-market by 30–40%**.
3. Shared accountability ensures holistic decisions balancing consumer appeal, manufacturability, regulatory compliance, and cost.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
List the **four stages of Tuckman's model** of team development. In which stage does conflict peak, and what is the manager's role?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stages: **Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing** (and Adjourning in the extended model). **(1.5 marks)**
Conflict peaks in the **Storming** stage, when members test authority, question priorities, and defend functional turf. **(1 mark)**
The manager's role is to **coach**, mediate disputes, clarify roles via tools like the RACI matrix, and prevent the conflict from becoming personal. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **role architecture** of a typical food product development team. Describe **any six** key roles and their interdependencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Stage-Gate Process** for new food product development. Illustrate the people involved at each gate and the **Go/Kill/Hold/Recycle** decision logic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role Architecture (7 marks):**

A food PDM team is a **Cross-Functional Team (CFT)** built around a **Stage-Gate** workflow. The six key roles and their interdependencies are:

1. **NPD Project Manager (PM)** — *(0.5 mark)* The conductor of the orchestra. Owns timeline, budget, gate decisions, and stakeholder alignment. Reports to the Steering Committee.
2. **R&D Food Scientist** — *(0.5 mark)* Owns formulation, ingredient functionality, prototype iterations, and shelf-life prediction. Feeds specs to Production and Marketing.
3. **Marketing Manager** — *(0.5 mark)* Owns consumer insight, positioning, pricing, and launch communication. Feeds the "voice of consumer" to R&D.
4. **Production / Process Engineer** — *(0.5 mark)* Owns scale-up, line trials, yield optimization, and packaging-line compatibility. Feeds cost-of-goods to Finance.
5. **QA / QC Lead** — *(0.5 mark)* Owns FSSAI compliance, HACCP, microbiological safety, and shelf-life validation. Has a *veto power* on launch.
6. **Regulatory Affairs Officer** — *(0.5 mark)* Owns label approval, FSSAI licensing, advertising claims (no misleading claims), and import/export documentation.

**Interdependencies (3.5 marks):**
The roles are not linear — they are *iterative*. R&D and Marketing co-create the concept (consumer pull). R&D and Production co-engineer manufacturability. QA, Regulatory, and Finance operate as a **parallel safety/governance layer** across all stages. Information flows in **three directions**: vertical (PM ↔ Steering Committee), horizontal (R&D ↔ Marketing ↔ Production), and diagonal (Regulatory ↔ R&D during ingredient approval). Effective PDM requires the PM to choreograph these flows using tools such as the **RACI matrix** and **stage-gate reviews**.

---

**(b) Stage-Gate Process for Food NPD (7 marks):**

The Stage-Gate Process breaks innovation into **stages of activity separated by gates where go/kill decisions are made**.

**Major Stages and Gates (4 marks):**

| Stage | Activity | Gate | Decision Body |
|-------|----------|------|---------------|
| Idea | Internal ideation + consumer trends | Gate 0 — Idea Screen | NPD Manager |
| Concept | Concept definition, qualitative consumer test | Gate 1 — Concept Approval | Steering Committee |
| Business Case | Quantified consumer test, financial model | Gate 2 — Business Case Approval | Steering Committee + Finance |
| Development | Formulation lock, pilot scale-up, QA validation | Gate 3 — Development Approval | Steering Committee + Plant Head |
| Test Market | Limited geographic launch | Gate 4 — Market Test Approval | Brand Director |
| Launch | Full commercial rollout | Gate 5 — Launch Approval | CEO / MD |

**Decision Logic (3 marks):**
At each gate the project is evaluated on **four weighted criteria**:

$$
\text{Score} = 0.30 \cdot T_{\text{tech}} + 0.30 \cdot A_{\text{consumer}} + 0.25 \cdot R_{\text{financial}} + 0.15 \cdot C_{\text{regulatory}}
$$

The decision matrix is:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{GO} & \text{Score} \geq 70 \\
\text{HOLD} & 50 \le \text{Score} < 70 \\
\text{RECYCLE} & 30 \le \text{Score} < 50 \\
\text{KILL} & \text{Score} < 30 \\
\end{cases}
$$

**People significance:** The *quality of gate decisions* depends entirely on having the **right people in the room** — not the loudest, but the most qualified. A Steering Committee with cross-functional authority is the human backbone of stage-gate discipline.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe **Tuckman's model of team development**. How should a food NPD project manager adapt their **leadership style** as the team moves through each stage? Use the **Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership** framework in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **RACI matrix** for the following five NPD activities: *Formulation design, Consumer sensory test, Pilot-plant scale-up, FSSAI label approval, and Launch communication*. Include the five roles — R&D Scientist, Marketing Manager, Production Engineer, QA Lead, and NPD Project Manager. Apply the **RACI quality rules** and justify your assignment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tuckman + Hersey-Blanchard (7 marks):**

Tuckman's model describes how any newly formed team — including a food NPD team — progresses through four (now five) stages: **Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning**. **(1 mark)**

**Stage-by-stage mapping (4 marks):**

| Tuckman Stage | Team Behaviour | Manager's Hersey-Blanchard Style | Food NPD Example |
|---------------|----------------|----------------------------------|-------------------|
| **Forming** | Polite, dependent, unclear roles | **Directing (S1)** — high task, low relationship | PM assigns recipe ownership; explains the Stage-Gate roadmap. |
| **Storming** | Conflict on priorities & territory | **Coaching (S2)** — high task + high relationship | R&D and Marketing clash on sugar reduction; PM mediates using consumer data. |
| **Norming** | Trust, shared language, rhythm | **Supporting (S3)** — low task, high relationship | PM facilitates; encourages cross-functional co-creation workshops. |
| **Performing** | Autonomy, high output | **Delegating (S4)** — low task, low relationship | PM monitors dashboard; removes external obstacles. |
| **Adjourning** | Transition post-launch | **Transitioning** | PM celebrates the launch, conducts lessons-learned review. |

**Justification (2 marks):**
The Hersey-Blanchard model is the natural leadership partner of Tuckman because both are functions of the team's *competence* and *commitment* at any given time $t$. A formula representation:

$$
L(t) = 1 - \alpha \cdot C(t) - \beta \cdot M(t)
$$

Where $L(t)$ is leadership intensity, $C(t)$ is competence, $M(t)$ is commitment, and $\alpha, \beta$ are weighting constants. As $C$ and $M$ rise, $L$ falls — the manager's hand must progressively lift.

A food NPD project manager who **fails to adapt** their style — for instance, by continuing to direct during the Performing stage — suffocates the team's autonomy, slows decision-making, and loses key talent.

---

**(b) RACI Matrix (7 marks):**

| Activity | R&D Scientist | Marketing Mgr | Prod. Engineer | QA Lead | NPD Project Mgr |
|----------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:-------:|:---------------:|
| Formulation design | **R, A** | C | I | C | **A (gate)** |
| Consumer sensory test | C | **R, A** | I | I | **A (gate)** |
| Pilot-plant scale-up | C | I | **R, A** | C | **A (gate)** |
| FSSAI label approval | I | C | I | **R, A** | **A (gate)** |
| Launch communication | I | **R, A** | I | I | **A (gate)** |

**Explanation of assignments (4 marks):**

- *Formulation design:* **R&D is Responsible and Accountable** because the bench scientist *executes* and *owns* the recipe outcome. Marketing is **Consulted** (for consumer-preference constraints like sweetness). QA is **Consulted** (for ingredient safety). Production is **Informed** (so they anticipate scale-up feasibility). The **PM holds gate-level accountability** for the overall decision.
- *Consumer sensory test:* **Marketing owns** this — they design the questionnaire, recruit consumers, and interpret results. R&D is **Consulted** for sample preparation. PM holds gate accountability.
- *Pilot-plant scale-up:* **Production Engineer** is both R and A — they run the trial, collect yield data, and own the outcome. R&D consulted for formulation nuances; QA consulted for in-process checks.
- *FSSAI label approval:* **QA Lead** owns regulatory documentation. Marketing consulted because they draft the label claim copy.
- *Launch communication:* **Marketing** owns campaign creation and rollout.

**Application of RACI Quality Rules (3 marks):**

1. ✅ **Exactly one A per row** — strictly maintained.
2. ✅ **At least one R per row** — strictly maintained.
3. ✅ **Total C + I per row ≤ 8** — strictly maintained (max 3 here).
4. The Project Manager appears as *A (gate)* in every row because gate-level accountability is a meta-responsibility — the PM does not do the work, but answers for the decision at each gate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often assign the Project Manager as **R** for every activity — this is **wrong**. The PM is the *Accountable owner of gate decisions*, not the executor.
> 2. Marking multiple people as "A" in one row destroys accountability — the #1 RACI failure in valuation.
> 3. Confusing **Consulted** (two-way dialogue before action) with **Informed** (one-way update after action) costs easy marks.
> 4. In Tuckman answers, do **not** skip the Adjourning stage — KTU 2024 specifically tests the extended 5-stage model.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **People are the #1 success factor** in food PDM — not technology, not money.
- ✅ **Six pillars:** Role Architecture, Team Topology, Leadership, Communication, Culture, Competency.
- ✅ **Best team structure** for food NPD = **Cross-Functional Project Matrix**.
- ✅ **Tuckman's 5 stages:** Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.
- ✅ **Hersey-Blanchard:** Directing → Coaching → Supporting → Delegating (mapped 1-to-1 with Tuckman).
- ✅ **RACI** has exactly **one A** per row; **C + I ≤ 8** per row; PM is gate-A, not task-R.
- ✅ **Stage-Gate** uses **6 gates (G0–G5)** with weighted scoring: 30% technical, 30% consumer, 25% financial, 15% regulatory.
- ✅ **Decision thresholds:** GO ≥ 70 · HOLD 50–69 · RECYCLE 30–49 · KILL < 30.
- ✅ **T-shaped skills** = vertical depth + horizontal breadth — the modern PDM competency model.
- ✅ **Psychological safety** accelerates PDM learning cycles by enabling failure sharing.
- ✅ **Steering Committee** is the cross-functional decision body; **NPD Manager** orchestrates; **CFT** executes.
- ✅ **Time-to-Market** for Indian food NPD ≈ 18–24 months; CFT can compress by 30–40%.
- ✅ **Communication 3-C Rule:** Clear · Consistent · Continuous.
- ✅ **Common exam keywords to use:** gate-keeper, accountability, concurrent engineering, scale-up, voice-of-consumer, FSSAI, RACI veto, situational leadership, psychological safety.

---
