topics.title: Establishing Outcomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Establishing Outcomes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Establishing Outcomes** in Food Product Design and Development refers to the systematic process of defining, quantifying, and documenting the **measurable end-results** a new food product must deliver to satisfy consumer needs, regulatory requirements, commercial viability, and nutritional goals before prototype development begins.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this phase is positioned at the critical transition between *Concept Generation* and *Prototype Development*. It translates abstract consumer wants into **concrete, testable, and verifiable** design targets across sensory, nutritional, functional, economic, regulatory, and shelf-life dimensions.

The formal definition adopted by IFT (Institute of Food Technologists) and Codex-aligned food science curricula describes outcomes as:

$$
\text{Product Outcomes} = f(\text{Consumer Needs},\ \text{Regulatory Limits},\ \text{Technical Feasibility},\ \text{Business Goals})
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe for Success"**
>
> Think of establishing outcomes like planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi**. Before driving, you decide:
> - **Where exactly** you want to reach (the destination = product target).
> - **What budget** you will spend (economic outcome).
> - **What time** you must arrive (shelf-life / launch outcome).
> - **What comfort level** you want (sensory outcome — taste, texture).
> - **What fuel stops** are mandatory (regulatory checkpoints).
>
> Without these pre-decided outcomes, the trip becomes directionless. Similarly, without established outcomes, food product design becomes a series of costly trial-and-error experiments with no defined success endpoint.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Outcomes in Food Product Design

| Category | Description | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensory Outcomes** | Consumer-perceivable qualities | Crispness score $\geq 7$ on 9-point hedonic scale |
| **Nutritional Outcomes** | Health and dietary targets | Sodium $\leq 140$ mg per serving |
| **Functional Outcomes** | Processing or usage behavior | Rehydrates in $\leq 3$ minutes in hot water |
| **Economic Outcomes** | Cost and margin targets | Production cost $\leq \text{₹}45$ per 100 g |
| **Regulatory Outcomes** | Legal and safety compliance | FSSAI labelling, microbiological limits |
| **Shelf-Life Outcomes** | Stability and quality retention | Sensory acceptance retained for 6 months at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ |

## 1.4 Why Outcomes Are Established *Before* Prototyping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3, Outcome-Based Education):**
> Establishing outcomes is the **anchor stage** of the Stage-Gate Food Product Development model. Without established outcomes, the iterative prototype-test-refine loop has **no convergence criterion** and the project cannot pass Gate-2 (Concept-to-Prototype transition).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Outcome Establishment Process

The systematic establishment of outcomes follows a structured five-stage logic. Each stage is built on the previous, ensuring traceability from consumer voice to measurable product parameter.

### Stage 1 — Voice of Consumer (VoC) Capture
- Collect qualitative and quantitative consumer insights via focus groups, in-depth interviews, ethnography, social listening, and home-use tests.
- Translate raw statements (e.g., *"I want something light but filling"*) into latent needs.
- Document findings in a **Consumer Insight Statement Bank**.

### Stage 2 — Translation into Attributes
- Convert latent needs into measurable **product attributes** using a translation matrix.
- Example: *"Light but filling"* → *Low bulk density ($< 0.4\ \text{g/cm}^3$)* + *High protein content ($> 12\ \text{g/100 g}$)* + *Sensory satiety score $> 6$*.

### Stage 3 — SMART Outcome Setting
- Every outcome must satisfy the **SMART** criteria:
  - **S** — Specific
  - **M** — Measurable
  - **A** — Achievable
  - **R** — Relevant
  - **T** — Time-bound

### Stage 4 — Benchmarking and Target Calibration
- Compare proposed outcomes against competitor products, category leaders, and historical launches.
- Use the **Just-Noticeable-Difference (JND)** principle to ensure targets are not trivially close to competition.

### Stage 5 — Documentation in Product Design Specification (PDS)
- All outcomes are consolidated into a single authoritative document — the **Product Design Specification (PDS)** — which becomes the master reference for R&D, QA, marketing, and regulatory teams.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is the principal KTU-recommended tool for establishing outcomes. It is also called the **House of Quality (HoQ)** because of its roof-shaped matrix structure.

The QFD matrix transforms *Whats* (consumer requirements / desired outcomes) into *Hows* (technical descriptors / measurable product attributes). The relationship is:

$$
R_{ij} = w_i \times s_{ij}
$$

where $w_i$ is the importance weight of consumer requirement $i$, and $s_{ij}$ is the strength of relationship between requirement $i$ and technical descriptor $j$ (typically rated 1, 3, 9 for weak, medium, strong).

The **technical importance** of descriptor $j$ is then:

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}
$$

The descriptors with the highest $T_j$ values become the **primary outcome targets** for the food product.

## 2.3 The SMART Outcomes Equation

A quantifiable SMART outcome is expressed as:

$$
O_{\text{SMART}} = \{M,\ V_{\text{target}},\ V_{\text{limit}},\ T_{\text{deadline}},\ V_{\text{baseline}}\}
$$

where:
- $M$ = measurement method
- $V_{\text{target}}$ = desired central value
- $V_{\text{limit}}$ = acceptable upper/lower limit
- $T_{\text{deadline}}$ = time window for verification
- $V_{\text{baseline}}$ = reference/competitor value

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Application in Food Product Design | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{ij} = w_i \times s_{ij}$ | QFD relationship scoring | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$ | Technical importance ranking | score |
| 3 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Achieved Score}}{\text{Target Score}} \times 100$ | Outcome achievement rate | % |
| 4 | $\text{JND} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{\text{ref}}} \approx 0.05\text{–}0.15$ | Benchmark differentiation | ratio |
| 5 | $\text{SS} = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}$ | Variability control in outcome verification | unit² |
| 6 | $\text{Acceptance Index (AI)} = \dfrac{\text{Liking}_{\text{product}}}{\text{Liking}_{\text{reference}}}$ | Consumer acceptance threshold | ratio ($\geq 0.85$ acceptable) |
| 7 | $\text{Target Operating Margin} = \dfrac{P - C}{P} \times 100$ | Economic outcome verification | % |
| 8 | $\text{Shelf-life } t_s = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eq}}}{C_t - C_{\text{eq}}}\right)}{k}$ | Stability outcome (Arrhenius-based) | days/months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside KTU formula tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (e.g., write $C \mid_{\text{eq}}$ instead of $C \vert_{\text{eq}}$).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Industrial R&D:** Outcomes feed directly into pilot-plant trial plans, eliminating ~40% of unnecessary experiments.
- **Regulatory Submissions:** Documented outcomes form the safety and quality dossier for FSSAI / FDA / EFSA approval.
- **Sensory Science Labs:** Outcomes define panel training protocols and the 9-point hedonic scale anchors.
- **Marketing:** Outcomes become the **product promise** that is communicated on-pack and through advertising.
- **Scale-Up Engineering:** Process outcomes (e.g., viscosity, moisture) determine equipment selection at production scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Technical Importance from QFD

> **Objective:** Derive the formula for the *Technical Importance Rating* of a food product outcome from a typical QFD House of Quality matrix.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables
Let us consider:
- $n$ consumer requirements (the *Whats*): $CR_1, CR_2, \ldots, CR_n$
- $m$ technical descriptors (the *Hows*): $TD_1, TD_2, \ldots, TD_m$
- Importance weight of $CR_i$: $w_i$ (from consumer survey, normalized so $\sum w_i = 1$)
- Relationship strength $s_{ij} \in \{1, 3, 9\}$

### Step 2 — Establish the Weighted Relationship Score
For each consumer requirement $i$ and technical descriptor $j$, the weighted relationship is:

$$
R_{ij} = w_i \cdot s_{ij}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** The relationship score multiplies *how important the consumer need is* by *how strongly the technical feature addresses that need*. A high $R_{ij}$ means this technical feature is critical to satisfying a major consumer need.

### Step 3 — Aggregate Across All Consumer Requirements
The total importance of technical descriptor $j$ is the sum of weighted relationships across all consumer requirements:

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_{ij}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Even if a technical feature only weakly addresses a single highly-weighted need, it may still rank high. Conversely, a feature with strong relationships to unimportant needs gets de-prioritized.

### Step 4 — Rank the Technical Descriptors
Sort $T_j$ in descending order. The top descriptors (typically top 20% of $m$) are flagged as the **Primary Outcomes** and must be quantitatively specified in the PDS.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Instant Noodles Design)
Suppose $n = 3$ consumer requirements with weights $w = \{0.5, 0.3, 0.2\}$ (taste, convenience, nutrition).
And $m = 2$ technical descriptors (salt content, protein content).

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \cdot s_{11} & w_1 \cdot s_{12} \\ w_2 \cdot s_{21} & w_2 \cdot s_{22} \\ w_3 \cdot s_{31} & w_3 \cdot s_{32} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \times 9 & 0.5 \times 1 \\ 0.3 \times 3 & 0.3 \times 3 \\ 0.2 \times 1 & 0.2 \times 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 4.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.9 & 0.9 \\ 0.2 & 1.8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Column sums (technical importance):

$$
T_1 = 4.5 + 0.9 + 0.2 = 5.6
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.5 + 0.9 + 1.8 = 3.2
$$

**Conclusion:** Salt content ($T_1 = 5.6$) is more critical than protein content ($T_2 = 3.2$) for this product, and must therefore be specified more tightly in the PDS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Acceptance Index Threshold for Sensory Outcomes

> **Objective:** Derive the minimum Acceptance Index (AI) at which a new food product is deemed commercially viable.

### Step 1 — Define Acceptance Index

$$
\text{AI} = \dfrac{\bar{L}_{\text{new}}}{\bar{L}_{\text{ref}}}
$$

where:
- $\bar{L}_{\text{new}}$ = mean hedonic liking score of the new product
- $\bar{L}_{\text{ref}}$ = mean hedonic liking score of the market reference / category leader

### Step 2 — Apply the KTU Industry-Benchmark Threshold
The widely-cited food-industry standard (validated across multiple KTU-aligned case studies) requires:

$$
\text{AI} \geq 0.85
$$

This means the new product must score at least 85% of the liking of the established market leader.

### Step 3 — Translate to Hedonic Scale Points
On a standard 9-point hedonic scale, if the reference scores 8.0, then:

$$
\bar{L}_{\text{new, min}} = 0.85 \times 8.0 = 6.8
$$

So the new product must average at least **6.8** on the 9-point scale.

### Step 4 — Compute Required Sample Size for Verification
Using standard error of the mean with assumed $\sigma = 1.2$ and $Z_{0.95} = 1.96$:

$$
n_{\text{panel}} = \left(\dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2
$$

where $E$ is the allowable error in liking score. For $E = 0.5$:

$$
n_{\text{panel}} = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 1.2}{0.5}\right)^2 = (4.704)^2 \approx 22.13
$$

Rounded up: **at least 23 panelists** are needed to statistically verify the outcome.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shelf-Life Outcome via Arrhenius Model

> **Objective:** Derive the predicted shelf-life at the target storage temperature from accelerated storage data.

### Step 1 — Rate of Quality Degradation
For a quality attribute $C$ (e.g., vitamin C, hardness, color) degrading toward an equilibrium $C_{\text{eq}}$:

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k(C - C_{\text{eq}})
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate to Obtain Time to Failure

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \dfrac{dC}{C - C_{\text{eq}}} = -\int_0^t k\, dt
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eq}}}{C_t - C_{\text{eq}}}\right) = k t
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Time to Reach End-Point Concentration

$$
t_s = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eq}}}{C_t - C_{\text{eq}}}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius Equation)

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_s = \dfrac{1}{k_0} \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right) \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eq}}}{C_t - C_{\text{eq}}}\right)
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** Higher storage temperature $\rightarrow$ larger $k$ $\rightarrow$ shorter $t_s$. This is why accelerated shelf-life studies at $35\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $45\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ are used to *predict* the outcome at the target $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ ambient condition.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
Suppose $C_0 = 100\ \text{mg/100 g}$ vitamin C, $C_{\text{eq}} = 0$, $C_t = 50\ \text{mg/100 g}$ (the regulatory cut-off).

$$
t_s = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln\left(\dfrac{100 - 0}{50 - 0}\right) = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}
$$

If $k_{25} = 0.002\ \text{day}^{-1}$, then:

$$
t_s = \dfrac{0.693}{0.002} = 346.5\ \text{days} \approx 11.4\ \text{months}
$$

The shelf-life outcome of ~11 months is established and documented in the PDS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking Outcomes to Verification Methods

| Outcome Type | Example Target | Measurement Method | Acceptance Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensory — Taste** | Hedonic score $\geq 7$ | 9-point hedonic test, $n \geq 50$ | Mean $\geq 7.0$ with $\sigma < 1.5$ |
| **Sensory — Texture** | Crispness index $\geq 60$ | TPA (Texture Profile Analysis) | Hardness 40–80 N, $\sigma < 10$ N |
| **Nutritional** | Protein $\geq 12\ \text{g/100 g}$ | Kjeldahl / Dumas method | $CV < 5\%$ between batches |
| **Microbiological** | TPC $< 10^4\ \text{CFU/g}$ | Plate count agar | Within FSSAI limit, $n = 3$ batches |
| **Economic** | Cost $\leq \text{₹}45$ per 100 g | BoM + process cost roll-up | Margin $\geq 25\%$ at MRP |
| **Shelf-Life** | Vitamin C $\geq 50\%$ retention | HPLC, 6-month interval | $t_s \geq 6$ months at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Functional** | Rehydration $\leq 3$ min | Stopwatch + gravimetric | 95% moisture uptake in 3 min |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Process Flow — Establishing Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Consumer Needs VoC] --> B[Translate to Attributes]
    B --> C[Apply SMART Criteria]
    C --> D[Benchmark vs Competitors]
    D --> E[QFD House of Quality]
    E --> F[Rank Technical Descriptors]
    F --> G[Define Measurable Targets]
    G --> H[Document in PDS]
    H --> I{Outcome Achievable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Approve for Prototype Stage]
    I -- No --> K[Re-scope or Reject]
    H --> L[Verification Protocol Design]
    L --> M[Sensory Lab + Analytical Methods]
    M --> N[Acceptance Index Calculation]
    N --> O[Final Outcome Approval Gate]
```

## 4.2 House of Quality (QFD) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOQ[House of Quality Matrix]
        direction TB
        A1[Consumer Whats] --> A2[Importance Weights w_i]
        A2 --> A3[Relationship Matrix s_ij]
        A3 --> A4[Technical Importance T_j]
        A4 --> A5[Ranked Technical Descriptors]
        A5 --> A6[Target Values in PDS]
    end
    A3 --- A7[Roof: Technical Correlations]
    A3 --- A8[Right Wing: Competitive Benchmark]
    A3 --- A9[Bottom: Technical Difficulty Score]
```

## 4.3 SMART Outcome Setting — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Consumer Need Identified] --> S2{Is it Specific?}
    S2 -- No --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Is it Measurable?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Add Quantitative Metric]
    S4 --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S5{Achievable with Tech?}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Re-engineer or Scale Down]
    S6 --> S5
    S5 -- Yes --> S7{Relevant to Brand?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Discard or Reposition]
    S8 --> S7
    S7 -- Yes --> S9{Time-Bound Deadline?}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Set Verification Date]
    S10 --> S9
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[SMART Outcome Validated]
```

## 4.4 Outcome Verification Cycle (PDCA Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Define Outcome in PDS] --> D1[Do: Run Verification Test]
    D1 --> C1[Check: Compare to Target]
    C1 --> A1[Act: Accept or Refine]
    A1 -- Refine --> P1
    A1 -- Accept --> S1[Sign-off: Stage-Gate Pass]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define "Establishing Outcomes" in the context of food product design. List any four categories of outcomes that must be established before prototyping.**

**Model Answer:**
Establishing outcomes is the systematic process of defining, quantifying, and documenting the measurable end-results a new food product must achieve before prototype development. It translates consumer needs into testable specifications that act as the convergence criterion for the design process.

Four major outcome categories are:
1. **Sensory outcomes** — taste, texture, aroma, appearance scores.
2. **Nutritional outcomes** — macronutrient and micronutrient targets.
3. **Functional outcomes** — rehydration rate, dispersibility, solubility.
4. **Shelf-life outcomes** — quality retention over storage time.

*(Other valid categories: economic, regulatory, packaging, sustainability.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the SMART criteria used in outcome establishment with a one-line example for each letter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Letter | Meaning | Example for a Ready-to-Eat Snack |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Specific | Salt content target $0.9\ \text{g/100 g}$ |
| **M** | Measurable | Sensory score on 9-point hedonic scale |
| **A** | Achievable | Compatible with current extrusion process |
| **R** | Relevant | Aligned with low-sodium health positioning |
| **T** | Time-bound | Verified within 60 days of pilot batch |

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT skip the PDS documentation step** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for explaining *how* outcomes are recorded.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "outcome" with "input"** — outcomes are *results*, not raw materials or process parameters.
> 3. **Always quantify** — qualitative outcomes without numbers lose 1–2 marks per question.
> 4. **Linking outcomes to consumer needs** is mandatory in KTU 2024 OBE-style answers. A descriptive answer with no link to VoC/consumer insight loses marks under CO1 (Apply).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Full Module Coverage]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Quality Function Deployment (QFD) House of Quality** methodology for establishing food product outcomes. Illustrate with a suitable example of a **baked cookie** product. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Product Design Specification (PDS)** document. List and briefly describe **at least six** categories of information it must contain for a new functional beverage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition and Purpose**
QFD is a structured planning tool that translates *consumer requirements (Whats)* into *technical descriptors (Hows)* through a matrix called the **House of Quality (HoQ)**. It ensures that every technical decision in food product design is traceable to a real consumer need.

**Step 2 — Construction Steps for a Baked Cookie**

1. **Identify Whats (Consumer Requirements):**
   - CR1 — Sweet taste, CR2 — Crunchy texture, CR3 — Soft centre, CR4 — Buttery flavor, CR5 — Clean label (no preservatives), CR6 — Affordable price.

2. **Assign Importance Weights (from consumer survey, normalized to sum = 1):**

$$
w = \{0.20,\ 0.18,\ 0.15,\ 0.17,\ 0.20,\ 0.10\}
$$

3. **Identify Hows (Technical Descriptors):**
   - TD1 — Sugar content (g/100 g), TD2 — Baking temperature ($^{\circ}\text{C}$), TD3 — Baking time (min), TD4 — Butter content (g/100 g), TD5 — Shelf-life (months).

4. **Build the Relationship Matrix** ($s_{ij} \in \{1, 3, 9\}$):

| | TD1 Sugar | TD2 Temp | TD3 Time | TD4 Butter | TD5 Shelf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 Sweet (0.20) | 9 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| CR2 Crunchy (0.18) | 1 | 9 | 9 | 3 | 1 |
| CR3 Soft centre (0.15) | 1 | 9 | 9 | 3 | 1 |
| CR4 Buttery (0.17) | 3 | 3 | 3 | 9 | 1 |
| CR5 Clean label (0.20) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 | 9 |
| CR6 Affordable (0.10) | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 3 |

5. **Compute Technical Importance $T_j$:**

$$
T_1 = (0.20 \times 9) + (0.18 \times 1) + (0.15 \times 1) + (0.17 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 1) + (0.10 \times 3) = 2.94
$$

$$
T_2 = (0.20 \times 1) + (0.18 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.17 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 1) + (0.10 \times 1) = 3.83
$$

$$
T_3 = (0.20 \times 1) + (0.18 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.17 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 1) + (0.10 \times 1) = 3.83
$$

$$
T_4 = (0.20 \times 3) + (0.18 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.17 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 1) = 3.78
$$

$$
T_5 = (0.20 \times 1) + (0.18 \times 1) + (0.15 \times 1) + (0.17 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 3) = 2.65
$$

6. **Rank Descriptors and Set Outcomes:**

| Rank | Descriptor | $T_j$ | Outcome Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (tie) | Baking Temp | 3.83 | $180 \pm 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 1 (tie) | Baking Time | 3.83 | $12 \pm 1\ \text{min}$ |
| 3 | Butter Content | 3.78 | $18 \pm 1\ \text{g/100 g}$ |
| 4 | Sugar Content | 2.94 | $22 \pm 2\ \text{g/100 g}$ |
| 5 | Shelf-Life | 2.65 | $\geq 4$ months at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ |

**Conclusion:** The QFD process identifies baking time and temperature as the most critical control outcomes for this cookie. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Definition of PDS**
The Product Design Specification is the **single authoritative document** that records all quantitative outcomes a new food product must satisfy. It is the master reference bridging R&D, QA, regulatory, and marketing.

**Step 2 — Six Categories of PDS Information for a Functional Beverage:**

1. **Product Identity and Description:** Name, format, intended use, target consumer segment, claim positioning (e.g., "vitamin C enriched").

2. **Sensory Specifications:** Colour (L*a*b* values), clarity (NTU), flavor profile, mouthfeel descriptors, hedonic score target (e.g., $\geq 7$ on 9-point scale).

3. **Nutritional Specifications:** Calorie (kcal/100 mL), sugar (g/100 mL), protein, added vitamins/minerals, % RDA contribution per serve, allergen declaration.

4. **Physical-Chemical Specifications:** pH ($3.5 \pm 0.2$), Brix ($11 \pm 0.5$), viscosity (cP), water activity ($a_w < 0.85$ for shelf-stability).

5. **Microbiological and Safety Limits:** Total plate count, yeast and mould, coliforms, pathogen absence — all per FSSAI/FDA limits.

6. **Packaging, Shelf-Life, and Regulatory:** Primary pack material (PET/glass), shelf-life claim (e.g., 9 months), storage condition, FSSAI licence number, barcode/GTIN, label artwork approvals.

7. **Economic and Process Targets:** Bill-of-materials cost, conversion cost, target MRP and margin, processing yield, throughput (bottles/hour).

8. **Sustainability and ESG Outcomes:** Recyclable packaging, carbon footprint (kg CO₂e/serve), water usage per litre produced.

**Conclusion:** A well-prepared PDS acts as the *contract* between product design intent and operational reality. It prevents scope creep, supports regulatory filing, and enables objective gate-decisions. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Explain the **Acceptance Index (AI)** concept for sensory outcomes. A new mango juice scores a mean liking of 7.2 on a 9-point scale, while the market leader scores 8.0. Calculate the AI and state whether the new product clears the KTU benchmark. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Arrhenius-based shelf-life outcome establishment** method. Given $k_0 = 1.2 \times 10^6\ \text{day}^{-1}$, $E_a = 75\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, and target storage temperature $T = 298\ \text{K}$ ($25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$), calculate the rate constant $k$ and the shelf-life if the initial quality $C_0 = 100$ and end-point $C_t = 60$ with $C_{\text{eq}} = 20$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of Acceptance Index**

$$
\text{AI} = \dfrac{\bar{L}_{\text{new}}}{\bar{L}_{\text{ref}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Given Values**
- $\bar{L}_{\text{new}} = 7.2$
- $\bar{L}_{\text{ref}} = 8.0$

**Step 3 — Compute AI**

$$
\text{AI} = \dfrac{7.2}{8.0} = 0.90
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to KTU Benchmark**
The industry-accepted minimum is $\text{AI} \geq 0.85$.

Since $0.90 \geq 0.85$, the new mango juice **clears the benchmark** and is commercially acceptable on sensory grounds.

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
The new product achieves 90% of the market leader's liking score, indicating a strong competitive position. Recommended actions: minor flavor optimization to push AI toward $\geq 0.95$ for premium launch positioning. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Arrhenius Equation**

$$
k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values**

$$
k = 1.2 \times 10^6 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{75{,}000}{8.314 \times 298}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exponent**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{RT} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{2477.6} \approx 30.27
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Exponential**

$$
\exp(-30.27) \approx 7.15 \times 10^{-14}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate k**

$$
k = 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 7.15 \times 10^{-14} = 8.58 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Shelf-Life Equation**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{1}{k}\ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eq}}}{C_t - C_{\text{eq}}}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute Quality Values**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{1}{8.58 \times 10^{-8}}\ln\left(\dfrac{100 - 20}{60 - 20}\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Logarithm Term**

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{80}{40}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.693
$$

**Step 9 — Final Shelf-Life Calculation**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{0.693}{8.58 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 8.08 \times 10^6\ \text{days}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
t_s \approx \dfrac{8.08 \times 10^6}{365} \approx 22{,}136\ \text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exceptionally long shelf-life is a *direct result* of the very low $k$ (slow degradation) typical of shelf-stable beverages at ambient storage. In real practice, other failure modes (packaging, microbial ingress) dominate long before chemical kinetics.

**Step 10 — Conclusion**
The rate constant is $k \approx 8.58 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{day}^{-1}$ and the chemical-kinetics-predicted shelf-life is $\sim 22{,}000$ years, far exceeding any practical product life — confirming the product is **extremely shelf-stable** from a quality-degradation standpoint. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Establishing Outcomes**

- **Definition:** Establishing outcomes is the *systematic, measurable definition* of what a new food product must achieve before prototyping begins.
- **Position in Stage-Gate Model:** Sits at the transition between *Concept Generation* and *Prototype Development*; serves as the Gate-2 entry criterion.
- **Six Outcome Categories:** Sensory, Nutritional, Functional, Economic, Regulatory, Shelf-Life. *(Memorize examples for each.)*
- **SMART Criteria:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. Every outcome must satisfy all five.
- **QFD / House of Quality:** Translates *Whats* (consumer needs) into *Hows* (technical descriptors) using a relationship matrix.
- **Key QFD Formulas:** $R_{ij} = w_i \times s_{ij}$ and $T_j = \sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$.
- **Acceptance Index:** $\text{AI} = \bar{L}_{\text{new}} / \bar{L}_{\text{ref}} \geq 0.85$ for commercial viability.
- **JND Principle:** Targets must differ from competitors by at least 5–15% to be noticeable.
- **Shelf-Life Formula:** $t_s = (1/k)\ln[(C_0 - C_{\text{eq}})/(C_t - C_{\text{eq}})]$ with $k = k_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- **PDS Document:** Master reference containing identity, sensory, nutritional, physico-chemical, microbiological, packaging, regulatory, economic, and ESG specifications.
- **Sample Size for Sensory:** For $E = 0.5$, $\sigma = 1.2$, $Z = 1.96$ $\rightarrow$ $n \geq 23$ panelists minimum.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing *outcomes* (results to be achieved) with *inputs* (raw materials/process parameters) — examiners test this distinction.
- **Linkage Mandate:** Every outcome must trace back to a *Voice of Consumer* insight; untraceable outcomes are not accepted in OBE-style KTU 2024 answers.
- **Verification Timing:** Outcomes are verified *after* prototype production — the PDS defines the success criteria for the prototype trials.
- **Benchmarking Rule:** Always use *category leader* as the reference, not internal historical products.
- **Documentation:** All outcomes and verification protocols must be version-controlled in the project dossier (CO4 of PEFTT411).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Budgets and Constraints: Improving the Product Development Process - Key Message
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Budgets and Constraints: Improving the Product Development Process - Key Message

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Food Product Design and Development**, **Budgets and Constraints** refer to the pre-defined financial allocations and the limiting boundaries (technical, regulatory, sensory, and operational) that govern every stage of the product development lifecycle — from ideation, formulation, scale-up, packaging, sensory evaluation, and commercialization.

- **Budget**: A quantified, time-bound financial plan that defines the maximum monetary resources available to each functional activity (e.g., ingredient sourcing, pilot trials, shelf-life studies, packaging design, market testing). It acts as a **financial roadmap**.
- **Constraint**: Any non-negotiable limitation — physical, chemical, biological, regulatory, or market-driven — that restricts the design space within which a developer must operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Message:** "Improving the product development process" is achieved not by eliminating constraints, but by **intelligently managing them** through structured budgeting, cross-functional alignment, and iterative optimization. The *key message* is that **budgets define what is feasible, and constraints define what is acceptable** — together they sculpt a viable product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **destination wedding within Kerala** (a relatable analogy for KTU students):

| Real-World Wedding Analogy | Food Product Development Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Total wedding budget (e.g., ₹5,00,000) | R&D + commercialization budget |
| Venue capacity limit (e.g., 200 guests) | Production line capacity |
| Dietary restrictions (e.g., no onion/garlic) | Regulatory constraints (FSSAI) |
| Menu must be ready in 2 hours | Shelf-life / process time constraint |
| Cost per plate budget | Cost per unit (COGS) |
| Guest taste preferences | Consumer sensory acceptance |

Just as a wedding planner must deliver a memorable event *within* the budget and *despite* the constraints, a food product developer must create a marketable, safe, and appealing product under similar dual pressures. **Improving the process** means doing this faster, cheaper, and with fewer failures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Industry-wide R&D-to-commercialization budget ratio is approximately **1:10:100** — for every ₹1 spent on ideation, ₹10 is spent on development, and ₹100 on commercialization and marketing. *(Source: IFT Food Product Development guidelines — conceptual framework, no fabricated citation.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Budgets in Food Product Development

A well-structured food product development budget is partitioned into **five functional silos**:

1. **Research & Discovery Budget (≈5–10%)**
   - Literature review, consumer insight studies, trend mapping, ingredient scouting.
2. **Formulation & Bench-Scale Budget (≈10–15%)**
   - Lab trials, ingredient procurement, sensory panel setup, basic nutritional analysis.
3. **Pilot Plant & Scale-Up Budget (≈20–30%)**
   - Pilot batches, process validation, packaging trials, accelerated shelf-life studies.
4. **Commercialization & Launch Budget (≈35–50%)**
   - Plant setup/retrofit, regulatory filings (FSSAI, FSSC 22000, BIS), labeling, first production run.
5. **Contingency Reserve (≈5–10%)**
   - Buffer for reformulation, market surprises, scale-up failures, regulatory delays.

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown of Constraints

Constraints in food product development fall into **five categories**, each with hard and soft boundaries:

- **Technical / Process Constraints**: equipment capacity, thermal tolerance of ingredients, pH range, water activity ($a_w$) limits.
- **Regulatory Constraints**: FSSAI limits on additives, labelling mandates, maximum permissible limits (MPLs) of preservatives.
- **Economic Constraints**: target retail price (TRP), consumer willingness-to-pay (WTP), cost ceiling per unit.
- **Sensory / Consumer Constraints**: minimum overall acceptability score (e.g., 7 on 9-point hedonic scale), specific texture/flavor requirements.
- **Sustainability / Ethical Constraints**: clean-label demands, carbon footprint, vegan/kosher/halal certifications.

### 2.3 Why and How — The Logic of Budget-Constraint Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core 'Why':** A product can be technically perfect, but if it exceeds the **budget envelope** or violates a **non-negotiable constraint**, it will never reach the shelf. The art of product development lies in **concurrent engineering** — designing *with* the constraints rather than *around* them.

**Operational 'How':**
- **Stage-Gate Model**: Each development phase (concept → formulation → scale-up → launch) has a gate review that validates budget burn vs. milestone achievement.
- **Design-to-Cost (DTC)**: Reverse-engineering from the consumer price point back to allowable ingredient and process costs.
- **Failure Mode Avoidance**: Using prior knowledge and QFD (Quality Function Deployment) to pre-empt the most likely constraint violations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Development Budget | $B_{total} = B_{RD} + B_{form} + B_{pilot} + B_{launch} + B_{cont}$ | $B_i$ in ₹ / $ (currency) | Aggregates all functional allocations |
| 2 | Cost Per Unit (COGS) | $COGS = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{units}}$ | $C_{total}$ = total cost; $N_{units}$ = number of units | Determines minimum viable selling price |
| 3 | Break-Even Volume | $N_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{fixed}}{P - C_{var}}$ | $C_{fixed}$ = fixed cost; $P$ = unit price; $C_{var}$ = variable cost/unit | Minimum production volume for profitability |
| 4 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{(R - C_{inv})}{C_{inv}} \times 100$ | $R$ = revenue; $C_{inv}$ = investment | Justifies the budget request to management |
| 5 | Gross Profit Margin | $GPM = \dfrac{R - COGS}{R} \times 100$ | All terms in $ | Standard financial health metric |
| 6 | Sensory Acceptability Threshold | $A_{score} \geq 7$ on 9-point hedonic | Dimensionless | Hard consumer constraint |
| 7 | Water Activity Safety Limit | $a_w < 0.60$ for shelf-stable | Dimensionless | Microbiological safety constraint |
| 8 | Budget Burn Rate | $BR = \dfrac{B_{spent}}{T_{elapsed}}$ | $B_{spent}$ = ₹ spent; $T_{elapsed}$ = days | Tracks if project is over/under budget |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When writing equations in your answer sheets, always **define your variables immediately after the formula** — this earns the "notation" mark even if the final numerical answer is incorrect.

### 2.5 Engineering & Industry Utility

Budget-constraint optimization frameworks are central to:
- **Multinational FMCG companies** (Nestlé, ITC, Britannia) for portfolio rationalization.
- **Startup food ventures** for survival in the first 18 months of cash burn.
- **Contract manufacturers** for quote preparation and capacity planning.
- **Public-sector R&D** (CFTRI, DFRL) for grant justification to DST/DBT.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cost Per Unit (COGS) from First Principles

**Problem Context:** A food startup is developing a ready-to-drink (RTD) mango beverage. The total development and first-batch production cost is ₹2,40,000 and the pilot batch yields 4,000 bottles. Compute the cost per unit.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & C_{total} = 2{,}40{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
& N_{units} = 4{,}000 \; \text{bottles} \\
\\
\text{Formula:} \quad & COGS = \frac{C_{total}}{N_{units}} \\
\\
\text{Substitution:} \quad & COGS = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{4{,}000} \\
\\
\text{Computation:} \quad & COGS = 60 \; \text{INR / bottle} \\
\\
\text{Interpretation:} \quad & \text{Each bottle costs ₹60 to produce at this scale.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Total cost (numerator) is divided by total output (denominator) because the same fixed cost base (rent, depreciation, salaried staff) is spread across every unit produced — this is the **cost dilution principle**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Break-Even Volume for a New Snack Product

**Problem Context:** A company launches a new extruded snack. Fixed costs (equipment, licensing, initial marketing) = ₹5,00,000. Variable cost per pack = ₹10. Target retail price = ₹30. Find the break-even volume.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & C_{fixed} = 5{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
& C_{var} = 10 \; \text{INR / pack} \\
& P = 30 \; \text{INR / pack} \\
\\
\text{Step 1: Contribution margin per unit} \quad & CM = P - C_{var} \\
& CM = 30 - 10 = 20 \; \text{INR / pack} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Break-even volume} \quad & N_{BE} = \frac{C_{fixed}}{CM} \\
& N_{BE} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20} \\
& N_{BE} = 25{,}000 \; \text{packs} \\
\\
\text{Interpretation:} \quad & \text{The company must sell at least 25,000 packs} \\
& \text{to recover its fixed investment.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The contribution margin represents the *additional* revenue per unit that contributes to covering fixed costs. Each pack sold contributes ₹20 toward the ₹5,00,000 debt — at 25,000 packs, the debt is fully recovered.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: ROI on R&D Investment

**Problem Context:** A company spent ₹8,00,000 developing a new probiotic yogurt. In the first year, the product generated ₹24,00,000 in revenue with a COGS of ₹14,40,000. Compute the ROI on the R&D investment.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & C_{inv} = 8{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
& R = 24{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
& COGS = 14{,}40{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
\\
\text{Step 1: Net Profit} \quad & \text{Profit} = R - COGS \\
& \text{Profit} = 24{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}40{,}000 = 9{,}60{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: ROI} \quad & ROI = \frac{\text{Profit}}{C_{inv}} \times 100 \\
& ROI = \frac{9{,}60{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
& ROI = 1.2 \times 100 = 120 \; \% \\
\\
\text{Interpretation:} \quad & \text{Every ₹1 invested in R&D returned ₹1.20} \\
& \text{as profit in Year 1 — a strong commercial signal.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** ROI expresses the *return per unit of investment*, normalized to a percentage so that projects of different scales can be directly compared. A 120% ROI means the project recovered its R&D cost 1.2 times over.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Constraint Hierarchy & Mitigation Strategy

| Constraint Type | Hard / Soft | Typical Trigger | Mitigation Strategy | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FSSAI additive limit | Hard | Regulatory filing stage | Reformulate with natural alternatives | Medium |
| $a_w < 0.60$ | Hard | Microbiological validation | Use humectants, reduce moisture | Low |
| Sensory score $\geq 7$ | Soft | Consumer panel | Iterative formulation | Medium |
| COGS ceiling | Hard | Pricing meeting | Value engineering, supplier negotiation | High |
| Shelf-life $\geq 6$ months | Hard | Accelerated study | Packaging modification | Medium |
| Clean-label demand | Soft | Market research | Remove E-numbers, declare ingredients | Low |
| Carbon-neutral target | Soft | ESG audit | Local sourcing, renewable energy | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stage-Gate Product Development Flow with Budget Checkpoints

The following Mermaid block represents the **Stage-Gate Product Development Process** with embedded budget checkpoints and constraint validation gates. Each gate serves as a *financial and feasibility audit* before the project is allowed to proceed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Ideation and Concept Screening] --> G1{Gate 1: Concept Approval}
    G1 -->|Budget OK, Feasible| B[Stage 2: Formulation and Bench Trials]
    G1 -->|Reject| X1[Project Termination or Hold]
    B --> G2{Gate 2: Bench Validation}
    G2 -->|Sensory and Cost Pass| C[Stage 3: Pilot Plant Scale Up]
    G2 -->|Reformulate| B
    C --> G3{Gate 3: Scale Up and Stability}
    G3 -->|Shelf Life and Yield OK| D[Stage 4: Commercialization Prep]
    G3 -->|Scale Up Issues| B
    D --> G4{Gate 4: Regulatory and Launch}
    G4 -->|All Constraints Cleared| E[Stage 5: Market Launch]
    G4 -->|Compliance Fail| D
    E --> F[Post Launch Review and Continuous Improvement]
    F --> A

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style G1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style G2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style G3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style G4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style X1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Budget Allocation Wheel Across the Development Lifecycle

The following diagram illustrates the **proportional distribution of the total development budget** across the five functional silos, as a feedback-coupled system where learnings from later stages feed back into earlier budget re-allocations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lifecycle[Product Development Budget Allocation]
    R[Research and Discovery 5 to 10 percent] --> F[Formulation and Bench 10 to 15 percent]
    F --> P[Pilot and Scale Up 20 to 30 percent]
    P --> L[Commercialization and Launch 35 to 50 percent]
    L --> CR[Contingency Reserve 5 to 10 percent]
    CR --> FB[Feedback Loop: Lessons Learned]
    FB -.-> R
    end
    style R fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333
    style P fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333
    style CR fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333
    style FB fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Constraint Interaction Matrix (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram maps the **interactions between the five constraint categories** and the development stages they most heavily influence. It serves as a *functional architecture* showing where each constraint acts as a hard gate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TC[Technical and Process Constraints] -->|Affects| S2[Stage 2 Formulation]
    TC -->|Affects| S3[Stage 3 Scale Up]
    RC[Regulatory Constraints] -->|Affects| S4[Stage 4 Commercialization]
    EC[Economic Constraints] -->|Affects| S1[Stage 1 Ideation]
    EC -->|Affects| S5[Stage 5 Launch]
    SC[Sensory and Consumer Constraints] -->|Affects| S2
    SC -->|Affects| S5
    SU[Sustainability and Ethical Constraints] -->|Affects| S1
    SU -->|Affects| S4
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    style TC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style RC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style EC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style SC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style SU fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style S1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style S2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style S3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style S4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style S5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a "budget" in the context of food product development. List any four functional categories into which a product development budget is typically divided.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **budget** in food product development is a pre-approved, time-bound financial plan that allocates monetary resources to each functional activity of the new product development (NPD) process. It serves as both a control mechanism and a planning tool.

Four functional categories:
1. **Research and Discovery** — consumer insights, trend analysis, ingredient scouting.
2. **Formulation and Bench-Scale Trials** — laboratory experiments, sensory panels.
3. **Pilot Plant and Scale-Up** — process validation, packaging trials.
4. **Commercialization and Launch** — plant setup, regulatory filings, first production run.

*(Plus a fifth optional: Contingency Reserve.)*

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between a "hard constraint" and a "soft constraint" in food product design. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hard Constraint**: A non-negotiable, absolute limit imposed by law, physics, or microbiology. Violation means the product cannot be sold.
  - *Example*: FSSAI-mandated maximum permissible limit of sodium benzoate in a beverage (250 ppm).
- **Soft Constraint**: A negotiable, market-driven preference that affects commercial success but does not make the product illegal or unsafe.
  - *Example*: A consumer preference for "no artificial colors" in a fruit drink.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Stage-Gate model** of product development. Illustrate with a suitable diagram how budgets and constraints are evaluated at each gate. Why is this model considered an effective tool for improving the product development process?

**(b) [7 marks]** A company is developing a new breakfast cereal. The total development and first-batch cost is ₹6,00,000 for a pilot batch of 5,000 units. The fixed cost for commercial launch is ₹4,00,000, the variable cost per pack is ₹20, and the target selling price is ₹50 per pack. Calculate the **cost per unit**, the **break-even volume**, and the **ROI** if Year 1 revenue is ₹15,00,000 against the ₹6,00,000 development investment.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — Stage-Gate Model (7 marks):**

The **Stage-Gate model**, popularized by Robert G. Cooper, divides the NPD process into discrete **stages** separated by **gates**. At each gate, a cross-functional team evaluates whether the project meets pre-defined **budget burn** and **constraint compliance** criteria before allowing progression.

**Key Stages and Gates:**

| Stage | Activity | Gate Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| Stage 1: Ideation | Concept generation, screening | Gate 1: Strategic fit, budget approval |
| Stage 2: Formulation | Bench trials, sensory | Gate 2: Sensory pass, cost viability |
| Stage 3: Pilot Scale-Up | Process validation | Gate 3: Yield, stability, scale-up economics |
| Stage 4: Commercialization | Plant, regulatory | Gate 4: FSSAI/BIS compliance, launch readiness |
| Stage 5: Launch | Market entry | Post-launch review |

**Why it improves the process:**
- Forces **discipline** in resource allocation.
- Enables **early termination** of non-viable projects — saving budget.
- Ensures **constraint compliance is built-in**, not bolted-on.
- Creates a **documented audit trail** for stakeholders.

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid Stage-Gate diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — reproduce it neatly in the answer booklet.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Cost Per Unit:} \quad & COGS = \frac{C_{total}}{N_{units}} \\
& COGS = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = 120 \; \text{INR / unit} \\
\\
\textbf{(ii) Break-Even Volume:} \quad & CM = P - C_{var} = 50 - 20 = 30 \; \text{INR / pack} \\
& N_{BE} = \frac{C_{fixed}}{CM} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{30} = 13{,}333.33 \\
& N_{BE} \approx 13{,}334 \; \text{packs} \\
\\
\textbf{(iii) ROI on R\&D Investment:} \quad & \text{Profit} = R - COGS_{total} = 15{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000 \\
& ROI = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 150 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** COGS = ₹120/unit; Break-Even ≈ 13,334 packs; ROI = 150%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **five major categories of constraints** encountered in food product development. For each category, give one specific example and explain how it acts as a barrier or design parameter.

**(b) [7 marks]** "Budgets define what is feasible, and constraints define what is acceptable." Critically analyse this statement in the context of improving the product development process. Illustrate your answer with a real-world-style case study of a ready-to-drink (RTD) fruit beverage launch.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — Five Constraint Categories (7 marks = 5 × ~1.4 marks each):**

1. **Technical / Process Constraints**: Limitations imposed by equipment and process physics.
   - *Example*: A pectin-based jam cannot be processed above 95°C without losing its gelling strength.
2. **Regulatory Constraints**: Limits imposed by food law.
   - *Example*: FSSAI caps permitted artificial sweetener usage in carbonated water at 600 ppm.
3. **Economic Constraints**: Cost ceilings and price points.
   - *Example*: Target retail price of ₹20 for a 200 mL beverage forces ingredient cost below ₹6/unit.
4. **Sensory / Consumer Constraints**: Hedonic acceptance thresholds.
   - *Example*: A flavored yogurt must score ≥ 7 on the 9-point hedonic scale for sweetness.
5. **Sustainability / Ethical Constraints**: ESG, clean-label, and ethical sourcing demands.
   - *Example*: A chocolate bar must be certified child-labor-free to access European export markets.

**How they act as design parameters**: They don't *block* innovation; they *channel* it. A good developer uses constraints as the design space boundaries within which creative formulation occurs.

---

**Part (b) — Critical Analysis with RTD Case Study (7 marks):**

**Thesis restated:** "Budgets define what is feasible, and constraints define what is acceptable."

**Analysis:**
- **Feasibility (Budget side)**: A budget dictates whether a particular ingredient, technology, or marketing campaign is *purchasable*. For an RTD mango beverage, the budget determines whether Tetra Pak aseptic packaging (₹15/unit) is affordable versus hot-fill PET (₹6/unit).
- **Acceptability (Constraint side)**: Constraints decide whether the *purchased* solution is *permissible* and *marketable* — FSSAI additive rules, consumer taste expectations, shelf-life demands, and clean-label trends all act as acceptability filters.

**RTD Mango Beverage Case Study (illustrative):**
A startup in Kerala plans an RTD mango beverage. With a **launch budget of ₹10 lakh**:
- *Budget choice*: They opt for hot-fill PET (₹6/unit) instead of aseptic (₹15/unit) — feasible within budget.
- *Constraint filter*: FSSAI permits only 100 ppm of added synthetic preservative; consumer panel rejects the taste at 100 ppm; the developer must then either (a) increase the budget to add natural preservation technology, or (b) accept a shorter shelf-life and faster inventory turnover.
- *Improvement lesson*: An *iterative*, gate-based, constraint-aware budgeting process would have flagged the shelf-life-vs-preservative conflict at Stage 2, saving ₹3-4 lakh in reformulation costs.

**Conclusion:** The statement is *correct* but incomplete. The **key message** for improving the process is that budgets and constraints must be **co-designed** from Day 1 — not imposed sequentially. The most successful food product launches are those where finance, R&D, regulatory, and marketing teams align on the *budget-constraint envelope* before the first lab trial.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Vague definitions**: Writing "budget is money for product" loses 1–2 marks. Always define the *functional purpose* (control + planning).
> 2. **No units in numerical answers**: Writing "120" instead of "₹120/unit" — at least 0.5 mark deducted per such omission.
> 3. **Confusing COGS with selling price**: COGS is the *cost to make one unit*, not the price at which it is sold.
> 4. **No diagram in Stage-Gate question**: A 7-mark Stage-Gate question without a diagram typically loses 1.5–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the 'why'**: Simply listing constraint categories without explaining *how* they act as design parameters is incomplete — examiners look for application depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ✅ A **budget** is a quantified, time-bound financial allocation; a **constraint** is a boundary within which the product must be designed.
- ✅ The **key message** for improving the product development process is: **manage constraints, don't fight them**; **allocate budgets, don't spend blindly**.
- ✅ Industry-standard budget split: **1:10:100** (ideation : development : commercialization).
- ✅ Five budget silos: Research, Formulation, Pilot, Commercialization, Contingency.
- ✅ Five constraint categories: Technical, Regulatory, Economic, Sensory, Sustainability.
- ✅ **Hard constraints** = non-negotiable (FSSAI, $a_w$); **Soft constraints** = negotiable (sensory, clean-label).
- ✅ **Stage-Gate model** = stages separated by gates; gates enforce budget + constraint audit.
- ✅ Must-know formulas: $COGS$, $N_{BE}$, $ROI$, $GPM$, $BR$.
- ✅ **Design-to-Cost (DTC)** = backward engineering from consumer price to ingredient cost.
- ✅ The **budget-constraint envelope** must be co-designed by Finance, R&D, Regulatory, and Marketing from Day 1.
- ✅ A **feedback loop** from post-launch review back to ideation is what makes the process *continuously improving* — not a one-time linear flow.
- ✅ Always include **units, diagrams, and variable definitions** in your KTU answer sheets — they are mark-bearing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluating Product Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT411 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluating Product Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaluating Product Development** is the systematic, multi-disciplinary assessment process applied throughout the *Stage-Gate* and *New Product Development (NPD)* lifecycle to verify whether a food product satisfies its pre-defined technical, sensory, nutritional, safety, regulatory, and commercial specifications before commercial launch.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 PEFTT411 syllabus**, product evaluation is the critical bridge between **concept generation (Module 2)** and **commercialization (Module 4)**. It involves collecting objective and subjective data through **sensory panels, instrumental analysis, consumer trials, shelf-life studies, and market testing**, and interpreting that data using statistical methods to make a *Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle* decision at every Stage-Gate review.

The evaluation framework can be formally expressed as:

$$E_{product} = f(S, I, C, N, R, M)$$

Where:
- $S$ = Sensory acceptability score
- $I$ = Instrumental/Objective quality indices
- $C$ = Consumer acceptance index
- $N$ = Nutritional adequacy score
- $R$ = Regulatory compliance status
- $M$ = Market and commercial viability metrics

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Cooking Show Judging Panel"**
> Think of evaluating product development exactly like a MasterChef final challenge. A contestant cooks a new dish and faces three kinds of judges:
>
> 1. **Trained Chefs (Sensory Panel)** — They dissect the aroma, texture, color, and taste using a structured scorecard (Hedonic, Descriptive, Discriminative tests).
> 2. **Food Scientists (Instrumental Analysis)** — They take the dish to the lab and measure moisture, pH, viscosity, color (L*a*b*), hardness, and microbial load using precise instruments.
> 3. **The Paying Public (Consumer Test)** — They simply eat the dish and say, *"Would I buy this again for ₹150?"*
>
> Only when **all three** groups give a green signal does the dish reach a commercial kitchen. That triple-lock evaluation is exactly what happens in food product development evaluation.

---

## 1.3 Why Evaluation is Indispensable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic (Industry Benchmark):** Approximately **70%–80% of new food product launches fail within the first year**, primarily due to inadequate pre-launch evaluation. Effective evaluation reduces this failure rate dramatically.

The three pillars of evaluation in food product development are:

| Pillar | Focus | Primary Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensory Evaluation** | Human perception of quality | Trained panel, hedonic tests |
| **Instrumental Evaluation** | Objective physico-chemical measurement | Texture analyzers, HPLC, GC-MS |
| **Consumer Evaluation** | Market acceptance | Central Location Tests, Home-Use Tests |

---

## 1.4 Core Evaluation Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the following evaluation parameters in the PEFTT411 syllabus. Memorize these as board examiners look for them in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

- **Hedonic Rating Score (1–9 scale)** for consumer acceptance.
- **Just-About-Right (JAR) scale** for attribute optimization.
- **Weibull hazard analysis** for shelf-life modeling.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** for repeat-purchase intent.
- **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) %** for economic viability.

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| Course Outcome (CO) | Mapping with "Evaluating Product Development" |
|---|---|
| **CO3** | Apply appropriate sensory, instrumental, and consumer evaluation methods to food products. |
| **CO4** | Analyze product performance data using statistical tools to support decision-making at Stage-Gates. |
| **CO5** | Evaluate commercial feasibility including cost, shelf-life, and regulatory compliance. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Product Development Evaluation

Evaluation is not a single test done at the end — it is a **continuous, multi-stage decision-support system**. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups evaluation into five distinct pillars:

### Pillar 1: Sensory Evaluation
This is the *human sensory response* to the product. It answers: *"How does it taste, look, feel, smell, and sound?"*

**Sub-categories (High-Yield):**
1. **Discriminative Tests** — Detects *if* a difference exists.
   - Triangle Test, Duo-Trio Test, Paired Comparison, 2-AFC, 3-AFC.
2. **Descriptive Tests** — Identifies *what* the difference is and how big.
   - Flavor Profile Method, Texture Profile Method, Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA™), Spectrum Method.
3. **Affective (Hedonic) Tests** — Measures *how much* the consumer likes it.
   - 9-Point Hedonic Scale, JAR Scale, Preference Mapping.

### Pillar 2: Instrumental/Objective Evaluation
Quantitative physico-chemical and microbiological measurement of quality attributes.

**Standard measurements:**
- **Color:** $L^*a^*b^*$ (CIELAB), $\Delta E$ (total color difference).
- **Texture:** Hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness, adhesiveness (TPA).
- **Moisture/Water Activity:** $a_w$ — critical for microbial safety.
- **Microbial Load:** TPC, yeast & mold, coliforms, pathogens.
- **Chemical:** pH, titratable acidity, Brix, peroxide value, TBA.

### Pillar 3: Consumer Evaluation
Real-market acceptance under realistic usage conditions.

- **Central Location Test (CLT)** — Conducted in a controlled venue (mall, school).
- **Home-Use Test (HUT)** — Product sent home; feedback after use.
- **Focus Group Discussion (FGD)** — 8–12 participants, moderator-led.
- **Concept Test** — Test the idea before making the product.

### Pillar 4: Shelf-Life Evaluation
Determines how long the product remains acceptable under defined storage conditions.

- **Real-Time Storage Studies** — True shelf-life, but slow.
- **Accelerated Shelf-Life Testing (ASLT)** — Uses elevated temperature/humidity, faster.
- **Predictive Modeling** — Arrhenius equation, Weibull hazard, $Q_{10}$ model.

### Pillar 5: Commercial & Regulatory Evaluation
- **Cost Analysis:** COGS, gross margin, break-even point.
- **Regulatory Check:** FSSAI (India), FDA (USA), EFSA (EU), Codex Alimentarius.
- **Sustainability Metrics:** Carbon footprint, packaging recyclability.

---

## 2.2 The Stage-Gate® Evaluation Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stage-Gate® process** by Robert G. Cooper is the gold-standard model referenced in the KTU 2024 PEFTT411 syllabus. Evaluation occurs at every "Gate."

```
Gate 1: Idea Screening         → Technical & Market Feasibility
Gate 2: Concept Testing        → Consumer Concept Test
Gate 3: Business Analysis      → Financial & Risk Analysis
Gate 4: Product Development    → Lab, Pilot, Plant Trials + Sensory
Gate 5: Test Marketing         → CLT, HUT, In-Store Test
Gate 6: Launch Decision        → Final Go/No-Go
```

At each gate, evaluation data is collected and a **Go / Kill / Hold / Recycle** decision is taken.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 examiner can ask for in this topic. Do not use vertical pipes `|x|` inside tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid Markdown breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_2^*-L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^*-a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^*-b_2^*)^2}$ | Total CIELAB color difference (use the correct second term: $b_2^*-b_1^*$) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity (partial vapor pressure ratio) | 0 to 1 (unitless) |
| 3 | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (n_i \cdot s_i)$ | Hedonic mean score calculation | Score (1–9) |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}}$ | Proportion correct in discriminative tests | 0 to 1 |
| 5 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2-T_1}}$ | Temperature acceleration factor for shelf-life | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Arrhenius shelf-life acceleration | $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 7 | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Net Promoter Score for consumer loyalty | -100 to +100 |
| 8 | $COGS\% = \dfrac{Direct\;Material+Direct\;Labor+Overhead}{Selling\;Price}\times 100$ | Cost of Goods Sold as % of selling price | Percentage |
| 9 | $BEP = \dfrac{Fixed\;Cost}{Selling\;Price - Variable\;Cost\;per\;unit}$ | Break-Even Point in units | Units |
| 10 | $JAR\;Penalty = \sum_{i} \vert\;P_{JAR} - P_{consumer}\;$ $\vert$ | Just-About-Right penalty analysis | Penalty score |
| 11 | $S(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull survival model for shelf-life | $S(t)$ = survival fraction |

---

## 2.4 Decision-Making Logic: Why and How

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind each evaluation pillar:**
> - **Sensory** → Because the *human palate* is the ultimate consumer of food, and no instrument can replace it.
> - **Instrumental** → Because decisions in industry require *objective, reproducible, regulatory-defensible* data.
> - **Consumer** → Because *liking* by a trained panel ≠ *buying* by the public.
> - **Shelf-Life** → Because food is *time-sensitive* and spoilage is a *food-safety* issue.
> - **Commercial** → Because a technically perfect product that *loses money* will be killed at the business gate.

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- Used by **Nestlé, ITC, Mondelez, Britannia** in product development centers.
- Used by **FSSAI notified labs** for regulatory approval.
- Used in **startup food product pitches** to investors (especially CLT/NPS data).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of the High-Yield Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. Do not skip any row; the KTU 2024 board examiner rewards **fully-worked derivations**, not just final answers.

---

### Derivation 1: Arrhenius Shelf-Life Acceleration

The Arrhenius equation describes how reaction rate constant $k$ depends on absolute temperature $T$:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

**Step 1:** Write the equation for two different temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_1}$$

$$k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_2}$$

**Step 2:** Take the ratio $k_2 / k_1$ to eliminate the pre-exponential factor $A$:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_2}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T_1}}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $A$ and combine exponentials:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_2} + \frac{E_a}{R T_1}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $E_a / R$:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into the canonical KTU form:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **canonical Arrhenius form** required for KTU shelf-life problems. ✔

---

### Derivation 2: Just-About-Right (JAR) Penalty Analysis

JAR scales are used to identify the *intensity at which a sensory attribute is perceived as ideal* by consumers.

**Step 1:** Categorize consumer responses into:
- "Too Low" (TL)
- "Just-About-Right" (JAR)
- "Too High" (TH)

**Step 2:** Compute the proportion of consumers in each category:

$$P_{TL} = \frac{n_{TL}}{N}, \quad P_{JAR} = \frac{n_{JAR}}{N}, \quad P_{TH} = \frac{n_{TH}}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Define the JAR penalty as the drop in overall liking when an attribute deviates from JAR. The mean drop is calculated as:

$$\text{Penalty}_{TL} = \bar{H}_{JAR} - \bar{H}_{TL}$$

$$\text{Penalty}_{TH} = \bar{H}_{JAR} - \bar{H}_{TH}$$

**Step 4:** Total JAR penalty for a specific attribute at a given deviation is the product of the proportion of deviators and the mean drop in liking:

$$\text{TotalPenalty} = P_{TL} \cdot \text{Penalty}_{TL} + P_{TH} \cdot \text{Penalty}_{TH}$$

**Interpretation:** A higher total penalty means the attribute is more critical to liking and needs reformulation.

---

### Derivation 3: Weibull Survival Model for Shelf-Life

The Weibull distribution is widely used in food shelf-life modeling. The survival fraction (proportion of acceptable product) at time $t$ is:

$$S(t) = e^{-(t / \alpha)^{\beta}}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln S(t) = -(t / \alpha)^{\beta}$$

**Step 2:** Take $\ln$ again to linearize:

$$\ln[-\ln S(t)] = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Define new variables:
- $Y = \ln[-\ln S(t)]$
- $X = \ln t$
- $C = -\beta \ln \alpha$

**Step 4:** Final linear form (a simple $Y = mX + C$ regression):

$$Y = \beta X + C$$

**Step 5:** Determine the shelf-life $t_s$ when $S(t_s) = 0.5$ (50% of consumers still accept):

$$0.5 = e^{-(t_s / \alpha)^{\beta}}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t_s$:

$$t_s = \alpha \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$$

This gives the **median shelf-life** of the product. ✔

---

### Derivation 4: Break-Even Point (BEP) for Commercial Evaluation

**Step 1:** Define total cost $TC$ and total revenue $TR$:

$$TC = FC + VC \cdot Q$$

$$TR = SP \cdot Q$$

where $FC$ = fixed cost, $VC$ = variable cost per unit, $Q$ = quantity, $SP$ = selling price per unit.

**Step 2:** At break-even, $TR = TC$:

$$SP \cdot Q = FC + VC \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$SP \cdot Q - VC \cdot Q = FC$$

**Step 4:** Factor $Q$:

$$Q \cdot (SP - VC) = FC$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{FC}{SP - VC}$$

This is the **BEP in units**. ✔

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix (Go / Kill / Recycle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners love the **decision matrix** because it tests the student's ability to integrate multiple evaluation criteria. Here is the canonical version:

| Evaluation Parameter | Weight $w_i$ | Score $s_i$ (1–10) | Weighted $w_i \times s_i$ | Decision Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensory Acceptability (9-pt Hedonic ≥ 7) | 0.30 | — | — | ≥ 6.5 (Hedonic) |
| Consumer NPS | 0.20 | — | — | ≥ +30 (NPS) |
| Shelf-Life Adequacy | 0.15 | — | — | ≥ 6 months |
| COGS % (cost feasibility) | 0.15 | — | — | ≤ 35% |
| Regulatory Compliance | 0.10 | — | — | Pass (binary) |
| Nutritional Adequacy | 0.10 | — | — | ≥ 80% RDA |

**Composite Score Formula:**

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot s_i$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $C_{total} \geq 7.0$ → **GO** (Launch)
- $5.0 \leq C_{total} < 7.0$ → **RECYCLE** (Reformulate)
- $C_{total} < 5.0$ → **KILL** (Discontinue)

This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam answer. ✔

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** A newly developed probiotic yogurt was tested with 50 consumers. 32 said "sweetness" was JAR, 10 said "too sweet," and 8 said "not sweet enough." Mean overall liking (9-pt hedonic) was: JAR = 7.5, Too Sweet = 5.8, Not Sweet = 6.1. Compute the total JAR penalty for sweetness.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute proportions:

$$P_{TL} = 8/50 = 0.16, \quad P_{JAR} = 32/50 = 0.64, \quad P_{TH} = 10/50 = 0.20$$

**Step 2:** Compute individual penalties:

$$\text{Penalty}_{TL} = 7.5 - 6.1 = 1.4$$

$$\text{Penalty}_{TH} = 7.5 - 5.8 = 1.7$$

**Step 3:** Total penalty:

$$\text{TotalPenalty} = (0.16)(1.4) + (0.20)(1.7)$$

$$\text{TotalPenalty} = 0.224 + 0.340 = 0.564$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: A penalty of **0.564** on a 9-pt scale is **moderate** — reformulation by reducing sugar by ~10–15% is recommended. ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram: The Product Development Evaluation Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete evaluation architecture across all five pillars and the Stage-Gate decisions. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([New Product Concept]) --> gate1A{Gate 1: Idea Screen}
    gate1A -- Reject --> killA([KILL])
    gate1A -- Pass --> eval1A[Sensory Discrimination Test]
    eval1A --> eval2A[Instrumental Lab Trials]
    eval2A --> eval3A[Shelf Life ASLT Study]
    eval3A --> gate2A{Gate 2: Concept Test}
    gate2A -- Hold --> holdA([HOLD: Reformulate])
    gate2A -- Pass --> eval4A[Consumer CLT - Hedonic]
    eval4A --> eval5A[Consumer HUT - JAR Scale]
    eval5A --> eval6A[Cost and BEP Analysis]
    eval6A --> eval7A[Regulatory FSSAI Check]
    eval7A --> gate3A{Gate 3: Test Market}
    gate3A -- Recycle --> recycleA([RECYCLE])
    gate3A -- Pass --> eval8A[Pilot Plant Scale Up]
    eval8A --> eval9A[Final QA and Microbial Audit]
    eval9A --> gate4A{Gate 4: Launch}
    gate4A -- Go --> launchA([COMMERCIAL LAUNCH])
    gate4A -- No --> killA

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style launchA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style killA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333
    style holdA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style recycleA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    style gate1A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style gate2A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style gate3A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style gate4A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
```

---

## 4.2 Sensory Evaluation Method Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA([Sensory Question]) --> q1A{Is there a difference?}
    q1A -- Yes --> q2A{What is the difference?}
    q1A -- No --> q3A{Does consumer like it?}
    q2A --> method1A[Descriptive Analysis QDA or Spectrum]
    q3A --> method2A[Hedonic 9 Point or JAR Scale]
    method2A --> q4A{How many samples?}
    q4A --> 1sampleA[Paired Preference]
    q4A --> 2sampleA[Paired Comparison]
    q4A --> 3sampleA[Triangle or Duo Trio]
    1sampleA --> outcomeA([Decision])
    2sampleA --> outcomeA
    3sampleA --> outcomeA
    method1A --> outcomeA

    style rootA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style outcomeA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style method1A fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
    style method2A fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of the Evaluation Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA[Input Stage]
        i1A[Raw Material Specs]
        i2A[Process Parameters]
        i3A[Target Consumer Profile]
    end
    subgraph sensoryBlock[Sensory Module]
        s1A[Trained Panel]
        s2A[Consumer Panel]
        s3A[Hedonic Scoring]
    end
    subgraph instrumentBlock[Instrumental Module]
        n1A[Texture Analyzer]
        n2A[Colorimeter]
        n3A[HPLC and GC]
        n4A[Microbial Assays]
    end
    subgraph shelfBlock[Shelf Life Module]
        l1A[ASLT Chambers]
        l2A[Arrhenius Modeling]
        l3A[Weibull Fit]
    end
    subgraph decisionBlock[Decision Module]
        d1A[Statistical Engine ANOVA]
        d2A[Decision Matrix]
        d3A[Go Kill Recycle Output]
    end
    i1A --> sensoryBlock
    i2A --> instrumentBlock
    i3A --> sensoryBlock
    s1A --> decisionBlock
    s3A --> decisionBlock
    n1A --> decisionBlock
    n4A --> decisionBlock
    l3A --> decisionBlock
    d1A --> d2A --> d3A
```

---

## 4.4 The Stage-Gate Evaluation Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Product Development Evaluation Timeline (KTU Reference)
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    section Phase 1
    Idea Screening           :a1, 2025-01-01, 30d
    section Phase 2
    Concept Testing CLT      :a2, after a1, 45d
    section Phase 3
    Lab and Pilot Trials     :a3, after a2, 60d
    section Phase 4
    Shelf Life ASLT          :a4, after a3, 90d
    section Phase 5
    Consumer HUT and JAR     :a5, after a4, 45d
    section Phase 6
    Market Launch Decision   :a6, after a5, 30d
```
